title: [perf][fnp-linalg][no-gaps] eigvalsh 4.2x slower than numpy at n=1024 (symmetric eigenvalues)
description: np.linalg.eigvalsh on SPD f64: n=512 fnp 46.9ms vs numpy 26.3ms = 1.79x; n=1024 fnp 300.9ms vs numpy 71.6ms = 4.20x (perf_linalg sweep 2026-06-14, box load ~6, numpy NOT thrashing — legit gap that WORSENS with n, classic sign of a lower-order-parallelized or less-cache-blocked kernel). numpy uses LAPACK dsyevd (divide-and-conquer tridiagonal eigensolver). LEVER (no-ceiling): profile fnp's eigvalsh path (eigvalsh_nxn in fnp-linalg) — likely the Householder tridiagonalization (O(n^3), the bulk) and/or the QL/bisection eigenvalue stage is serial or poorly blocked. Attack the tridiagonalization with a BLOCKED (panel + rank-k symmetric trailing update, dsytrd-class) reduction that reuses the existing parallel SYRK/GEMM microkernels (same panel-reuse structure just landed for cholesky in ff302aa3), and/or parallelize the eigenvalue iteration. Bit-identity: eigvalsh has an exact-repr/golden test (batch_lanes ... uses eigvalsh_nxn to_bits) — blocking changes Householder accumulation order so this likely needs allclose relaxation (numpy eigvalsh is allclose-contract) OR a same-order blocked reduction. Target >=2x at n=1024, no regression at n<=512. NOTE: fnp-linalg/lib.rs is currently peer-hot (concurrent cholesky edits) — land via isolated worktree on a disjoint function (eigvalsh_nxn), verify 0 peer tokens staged. Found by cc 2026-06-14.
close_reason: Routed deeper; no bounded production microlever remains. Pass 1 baseline: remote RCH vmi1153651 eigvalsh_nxn/size/512 [80.865, 82.475, 84.615] ms; no 1024 Criterion row exists. Pass 2 excluded prior rejected families: terminal Sturm bisection, dense adjacent-Givens full-to-band/band-to-tridiag, direct rank-2k/SYR2K streaming, matvec threshold lowering, simple panel-width retunes, scratch reuse, and const-specialization. Current profile still points at tridiag_reduce/dlatrd data movement (~293ms / 93% at n=1024), so the honest next primitive is the new successor franken_numpy-ixs5y.196: SBR stage-1 dense-to-band proof slice, followed by a separate compressed-band bulge-chase production route. No source change kept for deadlock-audit-0kqvd.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: PROFILED (cc 2026-06-14, isolated worktree off ff302aa3). eigvalsh time split (instrumented, n=1024): tridiag_reduce=293ms (93%), tridiag_eig_qr=22ms (7%); n=512: tridiag=46-54ms, qr=5.9ms. So eigvalsh perf == tridiagonalization perf; the QR iteration is negligible. Within tridiag_reduce_blocked, TWO serial bottlenecks: (1) the dlatrd panel matvec u=A·v (lines ~3884-3910) is ~85% of tridiag (~250ms@1024) and is MEMORY-BANDWIDTH-BOUND on one core (~2.8GB traffic for n=1024 matvecs / ~12GB/s single-core ≈ 230ms, matches). It re-reads the full trailing submatrix once per panel column (nb sequential dsymv). (2) the rank-2k trailing update was serial (k=nb=64 < packed_gemm's k>=128 gate). TRIED + RESULTS: (a) band-parallel trailing rank-2k update (BYTE-IDENTICAL, golden 00cda7e2 unchanged) → only ~15% (320→275ms@1024, 4.85→4.34x) since the trailing update is only ~15% of tridiag. (b) lowering TRIDIAG_MATVEC_PAR_MIN 1024→256 to parallelize the matvec → REGRESSED (4.34→5.18x): the per-column par_iter pays rayon wakeup latency ×~768 dispatches AND the matvec doesn't speed up with cores — memory bandwidth does NOT aggregate (NUMA: single-allocation work matrix lives on one node; remote cores throttled). So the one-stage dsymv panel is a hard memory/NUMA wall. REAL LEVER = TWO-STAGE SBR (dsytrd_2stage): stage-1 dense→band reduction is ALL BLAS-3 GEMM (cache-blocked, compute-bound, parallelizes + NUMA-tileable — reuses the existing packed_gemm), stage-2 band→tridiagonal via bulge-chasing (O(n²·b), cheap). This REPLACES the memory-bound dsymv panel entirely. Target 2-3x@n>=512. Effort: large (bulge-chasing is intricate); the byte-identical band-parallel trailing update (a) is a ready sub-piece to fold in. Alternative medium lever: symmetric-storage scatter matvec (read only lower triangle = halve traffic) but it CHANGES summation order → needs eigvalsh parity relaxed to allclose (numpy IS allclose) + golden updates (batch_lanes to_bits + eig goldens).

title: [perf][fnp-python][no-gaps] cov/corrcoef Gram 8-accumulator dot — beats numpy for n_vars>=128, allclose
close_reason: Shipped. cov_gram_rowvar_f64 computed each symmetric-Gram cell as a single-accumulator dot (one dependent FP-add chain, latency-bound ~3 GFLOP/s) — numpy's dsyrk is vectorized ~11 GFLOP/s, so cov/corrcoef were 1.06-1.5x slower for n_vars>=128 and ~3x for few-vars. Replaced with an 8-independent-accumulator dot (dot8, chunks_exact(8)) that breaks the dependency chain and lets LLVM pack the accumulators into AVX registers (autovectorized). allclose (reorder), NOT bit-identical for n_obs>=8; for n_obs<8 (all exact-repr cov/corrcoef unit-test sizes) chunks_exact(8) is empty so the fold is the original left-to-right sum and those tests stay green. INVESTIGATION (this turn, on cov): (1) route Gram through UFuncArray::matmul = REGRESSED 6x (fnp matmul is slow for thin-output/fat-k cov shape; the matmul profile-gate defers such shapes to numpy precisely because fnp loses there). (2) cell-flattened par_iter = WORSE (into_par_iter+collect+sqrt-decode overhead). (3) 8-acc row-parallel = the keeper. Score (self vs naive): (200,5000) 5064->4088us 1.24x, (50,2000) 759->709us; vs numpy now 0.76-0.92x (FASTER) for n_vars=128..500, still 3-4x for few-vars (n_vars<=50: too few rows for parallelism, small total work where numpy dsyrk has near-zero overhead — the remaining gap). allclose verified across 6 shapes x 5 kwargs; cov fast-path golden sha256 re-pinned a66b74e9->80bb612d (allclose-verified, deterministic); all 27 conformance_statistics + 5 lib cov/corrcoef unit tests green. REMAINING: few-vars-many-obs cov needs a register-blocked SIMD dsyrk microkernel (shared primitive with cholesky-SYRK) to beat numpy there — research-grade. NOTE: peer concurrently working cholesky_ordered_dot in fnp-linalg (5th cholesky lever).

title: perf(fnp-python): delegate large f64 min/max reductions to numpy SIMD (2-5x)
close_reason: Shipped. Reduction sweep found np.min/np.max ~5.3x slower than numpy at 1M (axis paths 3.9-4.8x) while sum/mean/std match — the f64 zero-copy fast path is a scalar cell-by-cell NaN-propagating signed-zero-ordered fold the compiler can't vectorize in safe Rust (SIMD-bound, 8vdtg). Measured native/numpy slower at EVERY size (1.5x@256 -> 5x@1M), so gated try_zerocopy_f64_minmax to DelegateToNumpy for input.len()>=4096 (tiny arrays keep native). A/B old/new: min 2.02x[8k]/2.47[32k]/3.51[131k]/5.07[1M], max 1.40/2.46/3.48/5.09. Parity byte-exact (signed-zero/NaN/axis/keepdims/int-dtype all == numpy; delegation also FIXES any large-array signed-zero divergence). Deferred: fnp-ufunc reduce_min/max parallel ordered-chunk fold (bit-exact, stashed) for the rarer non-contiguous path.

title: [perf][fnp-python][no-gaps] einsum 'i,j->ij' outer — delegate to write-into-buffer np.outer path, 16x self, beats numpy
close_reason: Shipped. einsum('i,j->ij',v,w) (outer product) ran the SLOW general Cartesian kernel — 25025us for 2000x2000, 20x slower than numpy — because the prior einsum-local outer helper (153361bd) filled an intermediate Vec then built the numpy array via UFuncArray + build_numpy_array_from_ufunc (a full 32MB build copy on top of the fill). Meanwhile fnp's OWN m.outer was already 1365us (faster than numpy 2945us) because try_zerocopy_f64_outer fills the result DIRECTLY into the np.empty output's mutable PyBuffer (no Vec, no build copy). FIX: try_zerocopy_f64_einsum_outer_2vec now validates the 'i,j->ij' subscript + 1-D operands, then DELEGATES to try_zerocopy_f64_outer (the shared write-into-buffer path). einsum parity allclose; all close=True, dtype float64. Self-speedup 64-core: 2000x2000 25025->1537us 16.3x (fnp/np 0.47 FASTER); 1000x1000 0.44x; 4000x1000 0.52x; 500x500 0.52x. Transposed outer 'i,j->ji' correctly defers + still correct. Proof: einsum_full_contraction_fast_path (incl i,j->ij + i,j->ji deferred) + all 27 conformance_einsum (incl einsum_outer) green. einsum vein now fully mined (full/partial/prefix/matvec/vecmat/single-reduction/outer all beat numpy). GENERAL LESSON: for a large-output op, fill DIRECTLY into the np.empty output's mutable PyBuffer (build_square_f64_matrix / try_zerocopy_f64_outer pattern) — never Vec-then-build, which doubles memory traffic with an 8-32MB build copy.

title: [perf][fnp-python] clip(bool) + nan_to_num(bool) -> numpy (679-902x extract paths eliminated)

Both are trivial on bool (clip is the reference clamp; nan_to_num(bool) is a plain copy since bool has no NaN/inf), so defer bool to numpy on the fallthrough (f64/int/f32 fast paths unaffected). Score @4M: nan_to_num(bool) 902x->1.04x (parity); clip(bool) 679x->1.60x (residual is fixed wrapper overhead on a 118us op, 80ms->~190us). Parity (dtype+value) across clip bool/various-bounds + nan_to_num bool/2d; f64/int clip + f64 nan_to_num regression green; 5 conformance tests green. Completes the load-robust bool extract-pattern sweep. LEFT (correct-but-slow, uncommon, tiny absolute): min/max(bool) ~4ms (2497x ratio but 2us numpy). Found via bool-input gap sweep.

title: [idea-wizard] Many-core conformance and performance wave
description:
Context:
- The tracker queue was empty after 1,072 closed beads, but README still records the important strategic gap: operations are single-threaded and asupersync is used for conformance orchestration rather than array computation.
- The project now has broad fnp-python conformance tests, RaptorQ artifact durability, performance budgets, and many packet evidence bundles. The next accretive wave should make that proof system faster for large agent swarms and prepare the array engine for deterministic many-core execution on 64+ core / 256GB+ hosts.

Goal:
- Create a self-contained implementation wave that improves proof throughput, coverage drift detection, hot-path parallel readiness, budget instrumentation, and large artifact durability without weakening NumPy parity.

Non-negotiables:
- Preserve strict NumPy observable behavior.
- Keep all heavy validation commands behind rch.
- Add focused unit tests and e2e/gate logging for each implemented feature.
- Prefer fail-closed gates over advisory-only reports when evidence is missing.

Done when:
- Child beads define an implementable dependency graph with no cycles.
- Each child bead is independently claimable by a future agent and contains test and validation commands.
- bv robot triage reports actionable work instead of an empty queue.
close_reason: Superseded by franken_numpy-ko0v5; JSONL-only create generated the wrong issue prefix in this repo

close_reason: Completed: raised kron_nxn parallel threshold from 1<<15 to 1<<18 after same-worker profile/bench showed small-output Rayon overhead. vmi1149989 criterion kron_nxn/kron_64x64_4x4_eye baseline [97.267, 107.56, 115.15] us; after [26.248, 35.200, 43.179] us, 3.06x by center estimate. Isomorphism proof: kron_parallel_matches_serial_reference_and_golden passed with golden SHA 6d16bc3a90ee6673f1f7f200b5955b17bfbe5402416926516eff16920d77efc7. Validation: rch cargo check -p fnp-linalg --all-targets; rch cargo clippy -p fnp-linalg --all-targets -- -D warnings; git diff --check. UBS rerun on touched files retains only pre-existing crate inventory; introduced benchmark direct-index warning was removed. cargo fmt -p fnp-linalg --check remains blocked by pre-existing unrelated rustfmt drift across fnp-linalg benches/examples/test blocks. Score=15 (Impact 3 x Confidence 5 / Effort 1).

title: [perf][fnp-linalg] cholesky/det 3-3.6x slower at moderate n (packed_gemm thin-band over-dispatch)
description: cholesky(n=256) f64 = np 468us vs fnp 1703us (3.6x); det(n=256) np 528us vs fnp 1590us (3.0x); cholesky n=512 1.5x, n=1024 1.7x. ROOT: the blocked factorization's trailing SYRK update routes through fnp-linalg packed_gemm, whose parallel dispatcher splits output into ~4-row bands (band_rows=ceil(m/(threads*4)) rounded to MR=4), so at moderate n it over-dispatches thin bands across 64 threads + re-packs B per band -> rayon/dispatch overhead dwarfs the small per-band work (n=128 parallel measured 2x slower than n=127 serial last session). Prior attempt (pack-B-once + A-panel packing) REGRESSED large n (n=1024 0.12x: per-panel A re-pack with nc=32 -> 32x redundant) and is stashed (wip-gemm-pack-regressed, off main). NEEDS: (a) a quiet/unloaded box to validate parallel perf reliably, (b) a band-count floor so each band has enough rows to amortize B-pack+dispatch, OR pack B ONCE shared read-only across bands with the i0-loop OUTSIDE the panel loop (correct GotoBLAS order, avoids the A-repack regression). fnp-linalg is currently clean (no peer changes). Behavior parity: bit-identical (same ascending-k accumulation); mat_mul golden sha256 must stay green.
close_reason: Completed: tuned mid-size Cholesky panel from 64 to 32 for the n=128..895 blocked path. Same-worker RCH vmi1156319 cholesky_nxn/size/256 improved 2.0601/2.1768/2.3547 ms -> 1.4331/1.5181/1.5734 ms (~1.43x median). Proof: cargo test -p fnp-linalg cholesky --release passed 20 unit + 4 conformance + 2 golden + 1 metamorphic + 4 solve tests; new cholesky_mid_panel_256_output_golden_sha256 locks digest 5677abe4016141dfb737c906dc28a8d667526c7e96c5161771033e568c9a0e4e; cargo check -p fnp-linalg --lib passed; cargo clippy -p fnp-linalg --lib -- -D warnings passed. Rejected probes: direct lower-triangular SYRK regressed 2.1128 ms -> 2.7803 ms; panel 128 regressed to 4.2100 ms; packed band floor showed only weak cross-worker signal.

title: [bug][fnp-python][parity] reshape used removed newshape= kwarg; missing shape=/copy= (numpy 2.1)
description: numpy 2.1 reshape signature is (a, /, shape, order='C', *, copy=None): newshape was REMOVED (TypeError), shape= added as keyword, copy= added (keyword-only). fnp.reshape used signature (a, newshape, order) -> np.reshape(a, shape=(2,3)) and np.reshape(a,(2,3),copy=True) raised TypeError, while fnp accepted newshape= which numpy now rejects. Found by cc 2026-06-15 via diff_kwargs2_cc_0615.py. FIX: signature (a, shape, order='C', *, copy=None), plumb shape+order+copy to numpy.reshape; newshape no longer accepted (matches numpy TypeError). Also discovered+fixed a build regression I introduced in 55de3f2f: 3 lib unit-test call sites of nan_to_num() still used the pre-copy 5-arg signature (the conformance test binary didn't catch it; cargo test -p fnp-python lib build failed).
close_reason: reshape now pass-through tracking installed numpy (shape/newshape/copy); fixed in 7440a4ab. Also hotfixed 3 nan_to_num lib-test call sites broken by 55de3f2f. conformance 22 passed.

title: [perf][fnp-ufunc][no-gaps] convolve/correlate short-kernel gather — kill the m-fold scatter write traffic
description: np.convolve/np.correlate were 6-28x SLOWER than numpy for SHORT kernels (e.g. n=50000,m=8: 2123us vs numpy 74us). Root: the f64 direct path used a SCATTER SAXPY (full[i..i+m] += a[i]*k) which writes every output cell m times — for a length-m kernel that is m-fold the memory traffic of numpy's gather (each output written once). Lever: GATHER form with pre-reversed kernel; visiting contributing inputs in ASCENDING order reproduces the scatter reference's accumulation order EXACTLY (bit-identical, golden + convolve_1d_parallel_matches_scatter_reference preserved), and vectorizes ACROSS independent outputs via portable_simd (each lane = one output, summed ascending-t, no FMA contraction). Gated to m<=96 (large kernels keep the autovectorized scatter); serial (parallel rayon dispatch lost on this bandwidth-bound op). Follow-up: large-N tail (>=1M elems) still ~10x vs numpy due to non-AVX-native cdylib codegen + cache cliff — needs a streaming/target-feature kernel.
close_reason: Shipped: HEAD 75bafa8d added the short-kernel gather; this commit makes it actually fast — serial-only (parallel rayon dispatch regressed it 4.4x: 50K/8 473us parallel vs 86us serial) + bounds-hoisted SIMD loads. 50K/8 now 86us vs numpy 69us (1.24x, was 28x slower under the old scatter, 5.5x faster than HEAD's parallel gather). nk=16-96 parity-or-faster. Large-N tail (>=1M) ~10x remains (non-AVX-native cdylib codegen + cache cliff) — separate follow-up.

title: [perf][fnp-ufunc] einsum full-contraction (ijk,ijk->, ij,ji->) 19-27x slower than numpy
description: einsum_native (UFuncArray::einsum / fnp-ufunc) is 19-27x slower than numpy for FULL-CONTRACTION patterns that reduce to a scalar via a general nested-loop path instead of a flat fused dot. Measured (f64, 6-rep min): 'ijk,ijk->' at 128^3 = np 775us vs fnp 20815us (26.9x); 'ij,ji->' at 1000x1000 = np 744us vs fnp 14067us (18.9x); 'i,j->ij' outer at 1000 = np 361us vs fnp 2846us (7.9x); 'ij,j->i' matvec 1000 = 2.77x. ROOT: full contraction (all indices summed, no output) should flatten/align and run ONE fused multiply-add reduction (like vdot), not a per-element odometer. Candidate fast routes measured: sum(multiply(A,B)) = 2254us (8.4x faster than current, ~1.6x numpy); for transpose case sum(multiply(A,B.T)) = 1174us (1.4x numpy). LEVER: recognize the no-output / full-contraction subscript shape in einsum_native and dispatch to the existing fused reduction kernel; transpose-aligned contractions need a strided fused multiply-sum. NOTE: einsum is the peer (cod) active lane (97ce9f5a einsum trace; conformance_einsum.rs modified) — coordinate / let owner take it. Parity: allclose holds for all candidate routes.
close_reason: Completed: routed identical-label two-operand full contractions (e.g. ij,ij-> and ijk,ijk->) to a flat left-to-right multiply-sum. Same-worker RCH vmi1152480 contraction/einsum_ij_ij_scalar/512 improved 367.47/375.48/384.40 us -> 164.55/173.20/181.87 us (~2.17x median). Proof: new bit-reference test passed; existing einsum golden SHA d4a2b5137fd48c9f2ebaae51760bb8ccd357c09ea3482eba833f750248946918 preserved; cargo check -p fnp-ufunc --lib passed. Transposed-label full contraction remains a separate strided lever.

title: [perf][no-gaps][fnp-linalg] Two-stage symmetric eig reduction (full->band BLAS-3 + band->tridiag bulge chase) for eigvalsh/eigh at common sizes
description: Profile-backed: np.linalg.eigvalsh is ~3.6x slower than numpy dsyevd at n=512 (numpy 23ms / fnp 86ms, STABLE). Shipped dlatrd matvec parallelization (50c2ec89) only helps n>=1100 (gate h>=1024) because per-column dlatrd matvecs are dispatch-bound at small h (a single n=512 column matvec is ~26us, can't amortize rayon dispatch across cores). The fundamental fix is the two-stage reduction: stage 1 full->band via blocked Householder with a SYMMETRIC rank-2k (dsyr2k) trailing update = cache-efficient parallel BLAS-3 (vs current memory-bound per-column matvecs); stage 2 band->tridiagonal via bulge chasing O(n^2*b). Wins at ALL sizes incl the common n=256-1024. Parity: eigenvalues match to tolerance (ascending order, ties). Confirmed NOT GEMM-microkernel-ceiling-bound at the algorithm level (the win is the data-movement reduction, not raw FLOPs). Substantial multi-hour build: band storage, WY block reflectors, bulge chase, golden/tolerance guards. Target Score >= 2.0 at n=512-1024.
notes: SnowySpire pass 1 baseline (2026-06-13): current HEAD ebae5a55 focused local RCH fallback baseline for eigvalsh_nxn/size/512 is 69.927/71.612/73.150 ms with RAYON_NUM_THREADS=16 and CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/data/tmp/fnp_deadlock9h2w9_eig_focus_target. RCH: no admissible workers critical_pressure=1,hard_preflight=11. No fnp-linalg source changes yet.
close_reason: SnowySpire no-ship closeout 2026-06-13: focused baseline eigvalsh_nxn/size/512 was 69.927/71.612/73.150 ms via RCH local fallback (critical_pressure=1,hard_preflight=11). perf record was blocked by perf_event_paranoid=4; stage helper passed and printed eig n=512 hessenberg=156.6ms qr_iter=379.8ms plus eigh n=1024 tridiag_reduce(Q)=626.8ms tridiag_qr(Q)=217.0ms, keeping the reduction/data-movement route profile-backed. Trialed one distinct bounded precursor, direct fused safe-Rust SYR2K-style A22 -= V*W^T + W*V^T in tridiag_reduce_blocked, not the prior one-GEMM transpose-elision family. Proof passed before rebench: cargo check -p fnp-linalg --all-targets; cargo test -p fnp-linalg blocked_tridiag --release -- --nocapture; cargo test -p fnp-linalg eigvalsh --release -- --nocapture; helper golden SHA during trial 485eadd5de10165d01484ebc9c302f351127571f3b5827887b3d8c998d4dbd4b. Comparable rebench regressed to 78.329/80.168/81.900 ms, Criterion +8.6517%/+11.947%/+15.650%, p=0.00, so Score < 2.0 and no source was kept. crates/fnp-linalg/src/lib.rs was unwound to empty diff. Next route: do not repeat one-GEMM rank2k elision or direct fused rank2k streaming; attack the actual full-to-band values-only reduction plus band-to-tridiagonal bulge chase with deterministic eigenvalue ordering/tie and golden tolerance guards.

title: [bug][fnp-python][parity] nan_to_num missing copy= kwarg (numpy mutates in place on copy=False)
description: fnp.nan_to_num(x, copy=True) raises TypeError: unexpected keyword argument 'copy'. numpy signature is nan_to_num(x, copy=True, nan=0.0, posinf=None, neginf=None) — copy is the 2nd positional param. With copy=False numpy performs the NaN/inf replacement IN PLACE on the input ndarray and returns it (verified: np.nan_to_num(np.array([nan,1.0]), copy=False) mutates the caller's array to [0.,1.]). fnp omits copy entirely. Found by cc 2026-06-15 via diff_parity_cc_0615b.py special-value sweep. FIX: add copy=true to the pyo3 signature; copy=True keeps the existing native fast paths (return new array); copy=False delegates to numpy.nan_to_num(x, copy=False, ...) for exact in-place semantics. Conformance tests use keyword nan= so positional copy slot is safe.
close_reason: Completed: direct nan_to_num unit tests now pass copy=true explicitly after the function signature gained NumPy's copy kwarg. Focused RCH validation passed on vmi1167313: RAYON_NUM_THREADS=16 CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/data/tmp/fnp_a3yjb_nan_tests rch exec -- cargo test -p fnp-python nan_to_num_matches_numpy --release -- --nocapture; 2 nan_to_num unit tests passed and the filtered fnp-python conformance shards compiled. copy=True native fast paths and copy=False NumPy delegation semantics are preserved.

description: Profile-backed successor from current einsum closeouts: after zero-copy two-operand full/partial/outer routes, remaining slow patterns include single-op ij reductions ('ij->', 'ij->i', 'ij->j') at roughly 10x/6.8x self gap in the latest cbour closeout. title: [perf][fnp-python] bitwise_count zero-copy POPCNT — 43x slow int64 -> 0.90x (beats numpy)
close_reason: Shipped. np.bitwise_count(int64) was 43x slower than numpy (90ms vs 2ms @4M): the native kernel already uses count_ones() (POPCNT) but fnp-python first extracted the input into its f64-values + i64/u64-sidecar dual representation (~16 bytes/elem copied) and rebuilt the output — pure data-movement overhead on a ~1-cycle op. Added try_zerocopy_bitwise_count: reads the integer ndarray buffer directly per dtype (i8/i16/i32/i64/u8/u16/u32/u64), maps each element through count_ones() (signed uses unsigned_abs, matching numpy + the native kernel), and writes straight into a uint8 numpy.empty output. Score @4M: int64 43.47x->0.90x (faster than numpy, ~48x self-speedup), uint32 0.61x. int8 1.28x (37us absolute, still far faster than the old extract path; numpy SIMDs narrow popcount). 0-d/non-contiguous/bool fall back. Parity verified (value+dtype) across all 8 int dtypes incl min/max/0 edges, negatives, 2d, scalar; 8 ufunc kernel tests green. Found via gap_sweep26.

title: perf(fnp-ufunc): integer-key sort + merge for f64 set ops (setdiff1d 2.0-2.6x, intersect1d 1.6-1.9x)
close_reason: Shipped. High-level sweep found set ops slow vs numpy (setdiff1d 3.19x, intersect1d 2.49x). ALGORITHMIC win (not delegate): the f64 paths sorted via sort_by(nan_last_cmp) — a stable comparator-closure sort — and setdiff1d additionally did per-element binary_search (O(n log n) membership). Replaced with (1) a monotonic f64->u64 total-order key + integer sort_unstable (branch-free, radix-friendly, no closure), and (2) setdiff1d binary-search -> linear two-pointer merge (intersect already merged). Score old/new: setdiff1d 2.62x[10k]/2.01[50k]/2.05[200k], intersect1d 1.90/1.62/1.61. Parity: 201 random cases (NaN/+-0.0/inf/empty/dup/large) byte-match numpy + 22 unit tests. 100% safe Rust, no-gaps compliant. union1d (1.53x via unique()) and setxor1d left for follow-up.

title: [perf][fnp-python][no-gaps] zero-copy 2-D partial einsum 'ij,ij->i'/'->j' — 23-66x self, beats numpy
close_reason: Shipped (follow-on to iknb6). einsum ij,ij keep-one-axis (out i or out j) was 28x/16x slower than numpy: extraction of both operands to UFuncArray dominates, then general Cartesian kernel. Added try_zerocopy_f64_einsum_pair_partial_2d (runs before extraction): keep-row = contiguous 8-accumulator row dot; keep-col = stream rows into a length-ncols accumulator (cache-resident, ascending-i). Zero-copy PyBuffer, 2-D same-distinct-labels equal-shape f64 contiguous. einsum parity is allclose so per-output order fine; close=True. Self-speedup 64-core 1000sq: keep-row 10553 to 159us 66.4x (0.46x vs numpy, FASTER); keep-col 4567 to 196us 23.3x (0.87x). Deferred and still correct via general kernel: transposed ij,ji partial and repeated-label ii,ii. Proof: einsum_full_contraction_fast_path_matches_numpy extended with both partial fast cases plus a transposed deferred case (allclose plus float64 dtype); all einsum unit and integration tests green. Remaining slow einsum left for follow-on: outer i,j (26x), single-op ij sum and ij to i (10x/6.8x), matvec ij,j, 3-D partial ijk,ijk to i. Found via einsum_patterns.py.

title: [perf][fnp-ufunc] interp_lr parallel threshold too low — medium-N (100k-1M) 4-6x slow, fnp WINS at 4M
close_reason: NO-SHIP (guess-search regresses, isomorphism proven but Score negative). Implemented numpy-style binary_search_with_guess (per-chunk carried guess, parallel via par_chunks_mut), gated to strictly-increasing xp, proven BIT-identical to the binary search (unit test interp_lr_guess_path_bit_identical_to_binary_search: sorted+shuffled, sizes straddling the 1<<12 parallel threshold, exact-grid+boundary). But a clean A/B (stash baseline, rebuild, bench) showed it REGRESSES large-N sorted: 4M sorted 8.3ms(old binary par_iter) -> 31-44ms(guess), 16M 1.5x slower. And it gave NO unsorted benefit: the OLD binary par_iter is ORDER-INDEPENDENT so it ALREADY beats numpy 4-19x on unsorted (numpy's serial guess THRASHES: unsorted 1M np=53ms vs fnp_old=4.2ms; 4M np=214ms vs fnp_old=11ms). The only apparent guess win was medium-N sorted (1M) but it was noise (re-runs showed ~old speed); the real medium-N sorted gap (100k 5.5x / 1M 3.9x vs numpy) is numpy's BEST case (perfect guess hits) + fnp's extract+build copy overhead — NOT the search. Closing the copy overhead needs a zero-copy PyBuffer path, blocked by PyBuffer cells being !Sync (can't par_iter), which ties into arch bead 8vdtg (relax forbid(unsafe) / SIMD). Net: old interp is already good (beats numpy on the general unsorted case, competitive at 4M sorted); guess-search is a regression. Discarded the change.

title: [perf][fnp-python][no-gaps] generalize zero-copy einsum partial to N-D prefix reduce ('ijk,ijk->i') — 19x self, beats numpy
close_reason: Shipped. Generalized try_zerocopy_f64_einsum_pair_partial_2d (renamed try_zerocopy_f64_einsum_pair_partial) from 2-D-only to N-D PREFIX reduction: same distinct labels both operands, output a non-empty STRICT prefix of the labels = keep leading k axes, sum the trailing CONTIGUOUS axes. n_groups=prod(leading dims), block=prod(trailing dims), out[g]=contiguous-block 8-accumulator dot of block g. Covers ij,ij to i (2-D, unchanged), ijk,ijk to i (3-D), ijk,ijk to ij, ijkl,ijkl to ij (any ndim). The 2-D suffix branch (ij,ij to j, strided keep-col) retained. Zero-copy PyBuffer before extraction; einsum parity allclose so block accumulation order fine; all close=True. Self-speedup 64-core 100cube: ijk,ijk to i 3385 to 173us 19.6x (fnp/np 0.46 FASTER). ij,ij to i/j unchanged (no regression). Deferred and still correct via general kernel: transposed ij,ji, repeated ii,ii. Proof: einsum_full_contraction_fast_path_matches_numpy extended with ijk,ijk to i + ijk,ijk to ij + ijkl,ijkl to ij (allclose plus dtype); all einsum unit and integration tests green; behavioral probe 5 shapes correct. Remaining einsum slow (follow-on): single-op ij to / ij to i (9x), matvec ij,j to i (2.66x) / ij,j to (11.86x), outer i,j to ij (shipped 153361bd but still 20x for large — not triggering, separate bug).

title: [perf][fnp-python] f16 zero-copy extract/build (uint16 view) + route f16 unary to numpy — kills 76-2555x extract gaps
close_reason: Shipped. float16 has no PyBuffer Element, so fnp read/wrote f16 arrays through PyList (one boxed Python float per element) — np.abs(f16 4M) was 2555x slower than numpy (425ms vs 166us), sqrt 23x, clip 12x, max 7.7x. Three fixes: (1) f16 INPUT extract (extract_precise_numeric_array + extract_storage_from_flat_array) now reads the raw 2-byte values via a zero-copy uint16 view -> f16::from_bits (f16/uint16 share a 2-byte layout so .view is valid on any layout). (2) f16 OUTPUT build (build_numpy_array_from_storage) writes the f16 bits through the uint16 buffer fast path -> .view(float16). (3) f16 unary elementwise ops (abs/negative/square/sign/floor/...) route to numpy in native_unary_elementwise (numpy's f16<->f32 ufuncs are the reference and faster than fnp's f16 dtype-bridge round-trip; bit-identical). Score @4M: abs 2555x->0.96x, negative 76x->1.00x, max 7.68x->0.46x (FASTER, via extract fix). Parity: f16 bit-exact (uint16 view) across abs/negative/sqrt/square/sign/exp/floor/ceil/rint + reductions; f32/f64 unaffected (route is f16-gated); 29 conformance tests green. RESIDUAL (filed mentally, diminishing): f16 sqrt 4.6x (separate special-error wrapper, not native_unary path) + f16 clip 2.7x (not a unary elementwise op) + f16 around decimals!=0 (allclose-level f64-intermediate rounding diff). Found via f16/complex extract sweep; complex was all clean.

title: [perf+fix][fnp-python] route f16 sqrt/clip/around to numpy — closes residual f16 gaps + around bit-parity
close_reason: Shipped, completes the f16 sweep (after dy679). Three remaining f16 paths not covered by native_unary_elementwise: (1) sqrt + other promoting unary ops go through native_unary_promoting — added the same f16->numpy route there (f16 sqrt 4.62x->1.02x). (2) clip is not a unary elementwise op — extended clip's bool-defer guard to also catch f16 (kind f + itemsize 2): 2.70x->1.00x. (3) around(f16) with decimals!=0 rounded via an f64 intermediate that diverged from numpy's f16-native rounding in the last bit (a real allclose-but-not-bitexact parity gap) — extended around's bool-defer to f16: 1.58x->1.02x AND now bit-exact vs numpy. Parity f16 bit-exact (uint16 view) for sqrt/clip/around0/around1; f64/int sqrt unaffected (f16-gated); 3 conformance tests green. f16 surface now fully at parity-or-better.

title: [perf][fnp-linalg][no-gaps] mid-size cholesky 2.4-3x slower than numpy dpotrf — needs recursive/larger-block reformulation (2 simple levers regressed)
notes: PROFILE (BlackThrush 2026-06-15, 64-core, load~12): cholesky_nxn reproducibly 2.4-3x slower than numpy across n=256..895 (clean numpy baseline, fnp uses own blocked dpotrf cholesky_blocked). n=256 1886us vs np 627 (3.0x), n=512 8228 vs 4983 (1.65x), n=768 12343 vs 4890 (2.52x), n=895 26081 vs 10677 (2.44x). KEY: the gap PERSISTS even in the fully-parallel large-n path (n=895 2.44x with the parallel column-tile SYRK active), so it is NOT a parallelism-gate bug — it is a per-flop/blocking-efficiency gap vs LAPACK dpotrf. ROOT: mid-size uses CHOL_MID_PANEL=32 (narrow), so n=512 has 16 panels each with a SMALL trailing GEMM (trail<384, k=bw=32) that never trips packed_gemm's k>=128 parallel gate. Many small per-panel GEMMs expose per-call overhead (packing, dispatch) that numpy's fused dpotrf avoids. TWO SIMPLE LEVERS REGRESSED (both reverted, byte-identical goldens held): (1) lower the parallel thin-SYRK trail threshold 384->128 (route mid trails to the scalar lower-tri par_chunks SYRK): REGRESSED (n=384 4799->8977) — the naive scalar dot is slower per-flop than cache-blocked packed_gemm, and parallel dispatch over few rows x many panels dominates. (2) parallelize the else-branch full-GEMM over disjoint trailing-row bands using packed_gemm_sub_assign_strided_serial per band: ALSO REGRESSED (n=256 1886->3854) — per-panel rayon dispatch x 16 panels dominates the small per-panel work. CONCLUSION: per-panel parallelization fails because mid-size panels are too small. DEEPER LEVER (no-ceiling, the real swing): RECURSIVE/cache-oblivious cholesky (LAPACK-style left/right-looking recursion) that aggregates the trailing updates into FEWER, LARGER GEMMs (one big trail x trail x panel GEMM per recursion level instead of N small ones), so the dominant work becomes a single large packed_gemm that hits the parallel + cache-blocked path. Alternatively try larger CHOL_MID_PANEL (64/96/128) to enlarge per-panel GEMMs (bw=128 trips packed_gemm k>=128 parallel) — but trades larger serial diagonal-block factorization. Needs a QUIET box (load<8; numpy times bounced 532-627us at n=256 here). Behavior is allclose (cholesky not bit-reproducible per code comment) but cholesky_mid_panel_256/512 goldens are byte-pinned — a reformulation that changes GEMM association would need re-pinning (verify recon<1e-7 + numpy allclose).
close_reason: Closed no-ship/routed deeper after five measured failed levers. Existing comments record thin-SYRK threshold regression, row-band SYRK regression, recursive potrf + naive TRSM flat result, recursive potrf + recursive TRSM slower result, and SnowySpire's same-worker vmi1293453 TRSM gate 384->128 rejection: 256 818.17 us -> 1.1144 ms, 512 4.5208 ms -> 4.6427 ms, 768 11.160 ms -> 11.365 ms. Source diff unwound to zero. Successor franken_numpy-ixs5y.205 now owns the deep primitive: allocation-free recursive Cholesky/TRSM with reusable scratch and triangular SYRK, not more threshold/per-panel rayon tuning.

comments.author: BlackThrush
comments.text: LEVER 3 (recursive divide-and-conquer cholesky) = FLAT, reverted (BlackThrush 2026-06-15, quiet box load~4.5). KEY DIAGNOSTIC FIRST: fnp's OWN packed_gemm BEATS numpy on the cholesky-SYRK shape (trail x 128 @ 128 x trail): fnp/np 0.30-0.71x. Yet cholesky stays 1.6-2.4x slower. So the gap is NOT the trailing SYRK GEMM (fnp wins there) and NOT a microkernel floor — it is the SERIAL parts: the unblocked diagonal-block factorization AND the triangular panel solve (TRSM). Implemented full divide-and-conquer recursive cholesky (split n/2: factor A11, block-TRSM L21, fat-k SYRK A22-=L21 L21^T via packed_gemm, recurse A22; base 64 unblocked). Goldens drifted (expected, allclose) but ALL reconstruction tests passed (correct). RESULT: FLAT vs blocked baseline (n=256 1924->2069, n=512 9030->9562, n=895 25165->24142) — still ~2-3x vs numpy. ROOT of the flatness: my block TRSM is a NAIVE column-forward-substitution = O(n^3/8) SCALAR work (per trailing row, sequential c-loop with growing inner dots), which became the NEW co-bottleneck, trading the thin-k SYRK problem for a naive-TRSM problem. Net wash. Also used FULL-GEMM SYRK (2x flops, computes discarded upper) not triangular. THE REAL FIX (next attempt): a winning recursive cholesky needs ALL THREE kernels fast — recursive dpotrf (done) + BLOCKED/GEMM-based dtrsm (solve panel vs small diagonal block, then GEMM-update trailing columns — the MISSING piece) + triangular dsyrk (half flops). That is a full LAPACK-class 3-kernel reimplementation = dedicated multi-turn project, NOT a one-lever change. Three levers now tried: thin-SYRK-threshold (regressed), row-band-SYRK (regressed), recursive-naive-TRSM (flat). All reverted, byte-correct.

comments.author: BlackThrush
comments.text: REFINED SCOPE (BlackThrush): the recursive cholesky's TRSM cannot be a simple blocked TRSM either — a column-panel-blocked TRSM has k=panel-width THIN GEMM updates (same packed_gemm serial gate at k<128). To get fat-k GEMM updates, the TRSM must ALSO be RECURSIVE (divide L11=[[L11a,0],[L11b,L11c]]: solve X1 vs L11a, then A21[:,sa:] -= X1·L11b^T [GEMM k=sa=fat at top level], solve X2 vs L11c). So the real fix = recursive potrf + recursive trsm, BOTH with fat-k GEMM updates (fnp GEMM beats numpy 0.3-0.7x on these shapes, so both would win). Plus triangular SYRK (half flops). This is a genuine LAPACK-class multi-kernel recursive reimplementation = dedicated multi-turn project, NOT a single lever. The KEY de-risking diagnostic stands: fnp's packed_gemm already BEATS numpy on the cholesky GEMM shapes, so the win is achievable — the work is purely structural (make potrf+trsm recursive so their updates become fat-k GEMMs). Next dedicated session should implement cholesky_recursive (have a working flat version) + a recursive trsm helper, re-pin cholesky_mid_panel goldens (allclose, recon<1e-7), target beating numpy across n=256..1536.

comments.author: BlackThrush
comments.text: LEVER 4 (recursive potrf + RECURSIVE trsm, both fat-k GEMM) = SLOWER, reverted (BlackThrush, quiet box load~6.5). Implemented full divide-and-conquer: recursive cholesky_rec_block (split n/2, fat-k SYRK) + recursive cholesky_rec_trsm (split L11 in half so the inter-panel A2-=X1 B21^T correction is a fat-k=sa GEMM). allclose=True all sizes (correct). RESULT: 2.4-7x vs numpy (n=256 4.97x, n=640 7.11x, n=2048 2.44x) — WORSE than the blocked baseline (~2-2.4x). ROOT: per-recursion-node overhead dominates — every node allocates+packs l21/l21t (SYRK) and x1/b21t/g (TRSM GEMM) Vecs, plus the SYRK is full-GEMM (2x flops, computes discarded upper). The clean recursive structure trades thin-k for allocation+packing+recursion overhead that LAPACK dpotrf (in-place, allocation-free, vectorized microkernels, decades-tuned) does not have. 4 LEVERS NOW FAILED: thin-SYRK-threshold (regressed), row-band-SYRK (regressed), recursive-naive-TRSM (flat), recursive-recursive-TRSM (slower). HONEST ASSESSMENT: matching dpotrf needs a HEAVILY-optimized allocation-free recursive impl (scratch reuse, triangular SYRK, tuned base cases, no per-node Vec churn) — research-grade, not an incremental lever. The de-risking diagnostic still holds (fnp packed_gemm BEATS numpy on the shapes), so it IS theoretically achievable, but requires a from-scratch allocation-disciplined kernel. Deferring; the blocked path (current, 2-2.4x) stays.

title: [perf] partition/argpartition native bridge 5-9x slower than numpy on large arrays — route to numpy passthrough (like sort/norm)
close_reason: Routed np.partition/argpartition to numpy passthrough (matching sort/norm). Root cause: native UFuncArray::partition (select_nth_unstable, introselect) matches numpy's algorithm AND per-compare cost (isolated Rust bench 9ms@4M == numpy), but the Py<->Rust bridge allocates several fresh full-size buffers per call (extract Vec + internal clone + exported buffer); the page-fault/copy tax dominates -> native export 5-9x slower on large inputs (4M f64: partition native 86ms vs numpy 16ms; argpartition 9x). An identical O(n) selection can at best TIE numpy, so passthrough = parity. Release after: partition 16.15ms vs np 15.94ms (1.01x); argpartition 17.36 vs 18.74 (0.93x). 19/19 conformance_partition + inline partition_argpartition_match_numpy green (passthrough = exact numpy parity). FOLLOW-UP (filed separately if pursued): a single-buffer native bridge (extract->in-place select->export without intermediate clones) could make native ~par; only then would native beat passthrough.

title: [perf][fnp-python][no-gaps] zero-copy masked_greater/less/equal/... family — 9-18x self, matches/beats numpy
close_reason: Shipped. The masked-scalar-compare family (np.ma.masked_greater/less/greater_equal/less_equal/equal/not_equal) was 9-24x slower than numpy (115-250ms@4M): masked_scalar_compare extracted x's data into an owned Vec, cloned it, and rebuilt = ~3 full-size copies. Added a fast path for a PLAIN f64 ndarray x + scalar float value: compute the mask zero-copy from x's buffer via the op's predicate (try_zerocopy_f64_predicate), then build np.ma.array(x, mask=mask, copy=copy) reusing the original x (a single copy, matching numpy). masked_equal also sets fill_value=value. Already-masked/non-f64/array-value defer to the generic mask-combination path. Score 4M: masked_greater 115ms->13.3ms 8.7x (0.70x vs numpy FASTER); masked_equal 250ms->14ms ~18x (1.40x, huge self gain). Correct across 6 ops x shapes/nan/copy (mask+filled+fill_value match); all 6 masked_* conformance tests green. Found via gap_sweep23. Remaining ma constructor follow-ons: masked_inside/outside (range, 13x), masked_invalid (isnan|isinf, 16x), fix_invalid (24x) — same extract-3-copies root, different mask computation.

close_reason: Shipped. (1) Added zero-copy fast paths for np.ma.masked_invalid (mask=~isfinite), masked_inside / masked_outside (range, lo=min(v1,v2) hi=max): plain f64 ndarray + scalar bounds -> compute mask zero-copy via predicate -> np.ma.masked_where(mask,x,copy). Score 4M: masked_invalid 176ms->3.7ms ~47x (parity), masked_inside 186ms->3.7ms ~50x (0.26x vs numpy FASTER), masked_outside ~250ms->3.7ms (0.28x FASTER). (2) FIX: the masked compare family fast path (shipped 0509c69d) used np.ma.array(x,mask=) which does NOT shrink an all-False mask -> diverged from numpy (full-False array vs nomask scalar) when nothing is masked (e.g. masked_greater(x,1e9)). Switched all masked-constructor fast paths to np.ma.masked_where(mask,x,copy) — numpy's OWN internal path for these wrappers — which shrinks all-False to nomask, byte-identical (repr). title: [perf][fnp-linalg] multi_dot 4-6x slow on skinny-intermediate chains — small-k GEMM path (DP already correct)
description: np.linalg.multi_dot is 6.18x slower than numpy on an order-sensitive chain (1500,2)(2,1500)(1500,2)(2,1500): numpy 2.30ms vs fnp 15.46ms; 4.62x on a 5-matrix chain. ROOT CAUSE: NOT the chain order — fnp's multi_dot (fnp-linalg:4717) ALREADY does the optimal-parenthesization DP correctly (verified: parity True, same FLOPs as numpy). The gap is mat_mul_rect for SKINNY matrices: the optimal order's dominant matmul is (1500x2)@(2x1500), k=2, and the cache-blocked packed GEMM's B-pack/A-pack + k-loop micro-kernel is pure overhead when k is tiny (output 2.25M, memory-bound). LEVER: add a small-k direct path (k <= ~8) — for each output row, out[i,j]=sum_{p<k} a[i,p]*b[p,j] as k SAXPYs over the contiguous j-row (autovectorizes), parallel over rows; skip packing. Bit-identical (same ascending-k accumulation) or tolerance. Isolate to multiply_range (or a mat_mul_smallk helper) to avoid touching the shared GEMM. CAUTION: fnp-linalg/src/lib.rs had a peer's UNCOMMITTED change in the working tree (git-trap risk) — do this in a quiet window or via isolated worktree per the disjoint-region-commit discipline. Target Score >= 2.0 on skinny chains; verify on typical (non-skinny) chains for no regression.
close_reason: Small-k direct-GEMM lever ATTEMPTED, NO-SHIP (1.1x). Confirmed fnp's multi_dot DP is correct (parity exact, same FLOPs as numpy). Added a parallel rank-k direct path to mat_mul_rect for k<=8 & m*n>=1<<14 (bit-identical, golden sha256 passed, 30 random chains match). BUT A/B on the skinny chain: 16.03->15.80ms (1.01x); isolated 2-matmul (1500x2)@(2x1500): OLD 15.61ms NEW 14.04ms (1.1x) vs numpy 0.47ms. So the parallel direct path did NOT materialize the expected ~10x — fnp is still ~30x behind numpy on this single skinny matmul. The gap is NOT packing overhead; it's deeper: the (1500x2)@(2x1500) op writes an 18MB output and fnp pays a build_numpy_array copy + cold page faults + the skinny-GEMM regime, all compounding. This is GEMM-microkernel/BLAS-class territory (8vdtg), not a quick kernel-regime patch. Tried via isolated git worktree (fnp-linalg had peer contention); reverted. DO NOT re-attempt the small-k mat_mul_rect patch.

title: Add fail-closed Phase2C porting ledger freshness verifier
description:
## What
Add a focused verifier in fnp-conformance that reads artifacts/contracts/PORTING_ESSENCE_EXTRACTION_LEDGER_V1.md plus artifacts/phase2c/FNP-P2C-*/packet_readiness_report.json and fails when a packet row claims stale proof status such as anchor-only, open, partial, missing packet artifacts, or E-I pending while the packet readiness report is ready with zero missing artifacts/fields/parse errors.

## Why
The current stale-evidence wave was found manually. A fail-closed verifier turns that class of drift into an automated signal for future swarms.

## How
Prefer a small Rust verifier/bin or test helper near the existing conformance control-plane validators. Parse the ledger conservatively enough to identify packet rows and status cells; avoid brittle global string bans that would flag legitimate historical words in unrelated sections. Emit structured diagnostics with packet_id, readiness status, stale phrase, and line number.

## Tests
Add unit tests for ready-vs-stale row rejection, non-ready row allowance, and clean ready row acceptance. Include a command that can be offloaded via rch.

## E2E/logging
Add an e2e or bin invocation that writes a JSON report with checked packet count, stale row count, and diagnostics. Success requires zero stale rows for ready packets.
close_reason: Added fail-closed Phase2C porting ledger freshness verifier with report output and validation coverage.

title: [perf][fnp-python][no-gaps] zero-copy 2-operand einsum full-contraction ('ij,ij->') — 13-58x self, beats numpy
close_reason: Shipped. einsum('ij,ij->',X,Y) and the 2-operand full-contraction family ('i,i->','abc,abc->') fell through to the general Cartesian einsum kernel AND first extracted both operands to UFuncArrays (extract_precise_numeric_array) — for a 1M-element pair the 16MB extraction alone dominated => 20-32x slower than numpy. Added try_zerocopy_f64_einsum_full_contraction: detects subscripts of the form 'L,L->' (same distinct alphabetic label string on both operands, empty output), reads both f64 buffers zero-copy via PyBuffer (contiguous, equal shape, ndim==labels), folds Σ_k a_flat[k]·b_flat[k] in ONE serial 8-accumulator multiply-reduce (PyBuffer cells are !Sync so serial; 8 indep accumulators break the add-latency chain => load-throughput bound, matching numpy's streaming bandwidth). Runs BEFORE the extraction loop. einsum parity is tolerance-based (einsum_multi_operand_values_match_numpy_within_tolerance is allclose), so the strided accumulation order is fine; all cases close=True, reldiff ~1e-14. Score (self-speedup, 64-core): ij,ij-> 1000x1000 9672->166us 58.2x (fnp/np 0.45 = FASTER than numpy); 2000x2000 43164->1972us 21.9x (0.78x); i,i-> 4M 69726->1849us 37.7x (0.72x); abc,abc-> 1970->153us 12.9x (0.42x). Deferred patterns correctly UNCHANGED + still correct: ij,ji-> (transpose contraction) and ii,ii-> (repeated-label diagonal) fall through to the general kernel (close=True). Proof: new lib unit test einsum_full_contraction_fast_path_matches_numpy (allclose + float64 scalar dtype across 3 fast-path + 2 deferred cases) passes; existing einsum_multi_operand_values_match_numpy_within_tolerance + einsum_path_matches_numpy + 3 integration einsum tests all green. cargo check + build + test clean; added code clippy-clean (pre-existing fnp-ufunc/fnp-python eq_op+drift warnings untouched). Found via gap_sweep19 (einsum_ij_ij 6.86x flagged).

title: [perf][fnp-linalg] eigvalsh residual ~3x: blocked tridiag_reduce_blocked still ~2.5x slower than numpy dsytrd (BLAS-3 fraction of symmetric two-sided update + FMA floor)
close_reason: Investigated, no-ship (residual is FMA-floor, not structural). The blocked tridiag_reduce_blocked is a proper dsytrd (BLAS-2 dlatrd panel + BLAS-3 rank-2k trailing update via packed_gemm). Tested the one candidate structural lever — lowering TRIDIAG_MATVEC_PAR_MIN (the panel symmetric-matvec u=A*v parallel gate, default 1024) — via clean same-binary env A/B at low load: parallelizing earlier REGRESSES (par-from-128: eigvalsh n256 9941->17416us, n512 56705->92361us; par-from-256 also worse at 384/512). The matvec is called nb*panels times with O(h) work/row, so rayon dispatch overhead dominates below ~1024 — the existing 1024 threshold is correctly tuned (the source comment was right). So the residual ~2.5x reduction gap vs numpy dsytrd is the per-core BLAS-2 panel-matvec efficiency: fnp's dot products autovectorize to AVX2 but WITHOUT FMA (.cargo disables +fma for bit-parity), ~2x behind numpy's AVX2+FMA dsymv. That is the FMA floor = arch bead 8vdtg (hardware FMA via target_feature/std::arch, human sign-off). Not a safe-Rust structural fix. The threshold win (a44dbead, eigvalsh 5x->3x) was the available lever.

title: [perf][fnp-python] make_mask_none/make_mask/mask_or -> numpy (560-1030x->1x)
close_reason: Shipped. Three pure boolean-mask utilities each stored masks as f64 UFuncArrays (8 bytes/elem) and rebuilt element-by-element, catastrophically slower than numpy's native bool ops: make_mask_none((4M,)) 68ms vs 66us (1030x), make_mask(int8 4M) 74ms vs 132us (564x), mask_or(bool,bool 4M) 101ms vs 113us (892x). All three are pure mask construction/combination with no arithmetic, so numpy is the parity reference. make_mask_none now defers to numpy.ma.make_mask_none (calloc np.zeros bool); make_mask defers after its O(1) nomask/dtype short-circuits; mask_or defers entirely (numpy logical_or). All now at parity (44/77/113us). Parity verified across bool/int/float inputs, all-false->nomask shrink, shrink=False, dtype, nomask operands, shape cases. 2 make_mask/mask_or conformance + 9 masked-constructor tests green. Found via gap_sweep24.

title: [perf][fnp-python] ma.allequal zero-copy early-exit fold (beats numpy 2.6x masked, ~1000x differ)
close_reason: Shipped. ma.allequal(a,b) extracted BOTH operands' data+mask into owned f64 UFuncArrays before folding (85ms @4M, 7.9x slower than numpy); because that extract is O(n) up front, the later early-exit never paid off. New zero-copy fast path for f64 (MaskedArray or plain ndarray, equal shape): folds straight over the data/mask PyBuffers with a genuine short-circuit. Masks fetched only for MaskedArray operands (plain ndarray has no mask; calling getmaskarray on it would alloc a needless all-False pass). No-mask case folds in 2048-elt SIMD-friendly chunks (inner AND-reduction autovectorizes; per-chunk break short-circuits). IEEE x==y is already False for any NaN so no explicit NaN test needed. Score @4M: masked-equal 0.39x (2.56x FASTER than numpy), plain-equal 0.90x (FASTER), differ cases ~1000x (early exit, 4us vs ~3800us). Value parity verified across plain/masked/nan/all-masked/fill_value T+F/2d/mixed ma-vs-plain. allequal conformance test green. Found via gap_sweep24.

title: [perf][fnp-ufunc][no-gaps] convolve/correlate large-N short-kernel tail ~10x — cache/profiling
description: After the short-kernel gather (8f1c9473), convolve/correlate match numpy at moderate N (50K-200K, parity-to-faster for m=8..96) but the LARGE-N tail (n>=1M, short m) is still ~10x slower. numpy scales LINEARLY (200K 273us -> 1M 1382us = 5x for 5x data); our gather is SUPERLINEAR = cache thrashing, despite +avx2 and single-write streaming. Needs perf stat cache-miss profiling on a QUIET box (load was 20+ when characterized, perf A/B unreliable). Candidate causes: (1) vec![0.0;full_len] zero-init is WASTED for the gather (each output written exactly once with =, not +=) — 2x write traffic + page faults at 8MB; safe-Rust uninit is the blocker. (2) overlapping-window redundant L1 loads (each input read ~m times). (3) write-allocate on output stores (no non-temporal). Likely fix: avoid the zero-init waste and/or a cache-blocked output tiling. See memory convolve-short-kernel-gather.
close_reason: Measured no-ship. Added a temporary Criterion row for production convolve large-N short-kernel gather, then removed it after rejection. Baseline on vmi1149989 with RAYON_NUM_THREADS=16: convprod_short_tail_1000000x8/prod_convolve [1.2476, 1.4414, 1.5998] ms. Trialed one source lever: replace the short-kernel gather output vec![0.0; full_len] plus slice writes with Vec::with_capacity(full_len) and ordered pushes/extend_from_slice, preserving the same scalar and SIMD accumulation order. Proof passed on vmi1149989: convolve_1d_parallel_matches_scatter_reference bit-reference test. Rebench on same worker rejected the lever: [1.3989, 1.6441, 1.9545] ms, about 14.1% slower by center with wider noise. Source and temporary benchmark hunks manually unwound; no fnp-ufunc code kept. Next route: do not retry allocation-construction for this tail; profile cache misses and attack true redundant input-window traffic with output tiling or a different streaming primitive.

title: [perf] convolve/correlate direct parallel scatter under-parallelizes large kernels (.max(m) block floor collapses to ~1-6 blocks)
close_reason: Dropped the .max(m) block-size floor in convolve_mode's direct parallel scatter (lib.rs ~19838): for a large kernel (m comparable to full_len) it forced chunk>=m, collapsing par_chunks_mut to ~1-6 blocks and idling most cores. Replaced with .max(64) (small floor only). Chunk size never affects the result (each output index owned by one block, ascending-i accumulation preserved) — proven by convolve_1d_parallel_matches_scatter_reference (bit-exact) + the bench's own assert_eq!(old,new). Same-run rch criterion A/B (load-robust, both arms equal load even at machine load 207): 5000x20000 (kernel>>signal) 18.94ms->4.82ms 3.93x; 20000x12000 30.94ms->10.55ms 2.93x; 200000x1000 small-m control 6.15->6.17ms 1.00x (IDENTICAL, zero regression — full_len/(threads*2) already exceeds small m so the floor never binds). Only the direct path with large m is affected; the FFT-gated huge cases and all small/medium convolves are unchanged. Continues the convolve FFT-gate fix (bead p8qdt). 17 convolve tests green.

title: [test-flake][fnp-python] inner_native_gemm_gate sha256 golden is build-non-deterministic
description: inner_native_gemm_gate_keeps_numpy_parity_across_sizes (fnp-python lib.rs ~64185) asserts allclose(inner,numpy) AND a byte-exact sha256 golden 33d7a135... over native-GEMM f64 output. The sha256 is NOT build-stable: identical source (commit 39f72475) PASSED in a fresh worktree build but FAILED in the main incremental build — the inlined/parallel GEMM kernel FP accumulation order drifts across compilations (incremental-cache / codegen-unit nondeterminism). allclose ALWAYS holds (parity intact). This false-fails any unrelated fnp-python edit (cost ~8 build cycles to exonerate a matrix_norm change). FIX: make the inner gate allclose-only, or compute the golden over build-invariant data, not parallel-GEMM FP bytes. Owner: GEMM ix117. title: [perf] fftshift/ifftshift native bridge 14-72x slower than numpy on large arrays — route to numpy passthrough
close_reason: Reduced fft_shift_impl to a numpy passthrough (np.fft.fftshift/ifftshift). Native roll-based path (UFuncArray::roll/roll_multi) matched numpy's algorithm (circular shift = pure data move) but paid the Py<->Rust bridge's per-call full-size allocations: 14-72x slower on non-trivial arrays (1M f64: native 13.1ms vs numpy 0.18ms; 50k 27.6x; even N=1000 1.24x — no regime where native wins). A circular shift can at best tie numpy, so passthrough = parity (same as sort/norm/partition). Release after: fftshift 50k 0.97x, 1M 0.94x; ifftshift 1.01x/1.09x. fftshift_and_ifftshift_match_numpy_across_axes_odd_even_and_nd green (passthrough = exact numpy). Same bridge-tax class as [[partition-bridge-tax-passthrough]] (bead efhtq).

title: Emit agent-readable Phase2C packet evidence convergence report
description:
## What
Add a report command or validator mode that summarizes each FNP-P2C packet's central-ledger status, packet readiness status, required artifact paths, residual parity-debt wording, and next actionable gap.

## Why
Agents currently rediscover stale packet truth by running rg/jq manually. A compact report helps large swarms choose real gaps without duplicate stale-evidence work.

## How
Reuse the same packet scan data as the freshness verifier. Output JSON for agents and a concise Markdown/table form for humans. Include packet_id, readiness_status, missing_artifacts_count, missing_fields_count, parse_errors_count, ledger_status_summary, residual_debt_summary, and suggested_next_action.

## Tests
Unit-test JSON shape and at least one stale/clean fixture. Add an e2e command that writes a report under artifacts/logs or a caller-provided --report-path.

## Validation
rch exec -- cargo test -p fnp-conformance <focused tests>; rch exec -- cargo run -p fnp-conformance --bin <report command> -- --report-path /tmp/phase2c-report.json; jq required fields.
close_reason: Added agent-readable Phase2C evidence convergence report with JSON/Markdown output, focused tests, and rch-validated live report command.

title: [perf][fnp-python][no-gaps] np.ma.getmaskarray O(1) passthrough — was O(n) extract+rebuild (~16000x self)
close_reason: Shipped. np.ma.getmaskarray on a 4M MaskedArray was 42678us (~235000x slower than numpy's 0.2us): the generic path extracted the bool mask into an f64 Vec then rebuilt it (bool->f64->bool numpy array) = O(n). numpy just returns arr.mask (the stored bool array) O(1). Added a passthrough fast path: for a MaskedArray whose .mask is already a bool ndarray of the data shape, return it directly. nomask/scalar/plain inputs fall through to the existing zeros-building path. Bit-identical (np.array_equal, same dtype/shape). Score 4M: 42678->2.65us ~16000x self (O(n)->O(1)); residual vs numpy 0.23us is constant Python-wrapper overhead (isinstance+getattr checks). Correct across masked/nomask/plain x 3 shapes; getmaskarray_matches_numpy + getmask tests green. Found via gap_sweep23. Same masked-array extract-3-copies/rebuild root as ma.filled/compressed (now done); masked_* CONSTRUCTOR family (masked_greater/invalid/inside/equal 10-24x) remains (constructor-bound, can match not beat numpy).

title: [test] FFT metamorphic invariants: Parseval + linearity over radix-2 and Bluestein paths
[perf][fnp-python][no-gaps] cov/corrcoef gram dot is single-accumulator latency-bound — register-block to dsyrk-class multi-accumulator
description:
np.cov/corrcoef on (50,1000) f64 are 4.3-4.8x SLOWER than numpy (cov 526us vs 123us); the gap is regime-dependent: (10,1000) 1.47x, (50,1000) 4.28x, (200,1000) 1.38x, (50,10000) 2.41x, (500,500) 0.86x (fnp already wins large).

ROOT: cov_gram_rowvar_f64 (lib.rs ~20717) computes each symmetric-Gram cell as a SINGLE-ACCUMULATOR dot `ci.iter().zip(cj).map(|(a,b)|a*b).sum()`. That is a length-n_obs DEPENDENT FP-add chain (~n_obs cycles latency-bound, no SIMD/ILP), where numpy's BLAS dsyrk uses multiple accumulators + register blocking. At m~50 there is too little row-parallelism to hide the per-dot latency; at m>=200 or m few-vars-many-obs the par_chunks_mut fan-out aggregates bandwidth and the gap shrinks.

LEVER (NO-CEILING, attack the primitive): replace the naive dot with a register-blocked MULTI-ACCUMULATOR dot (e.g. 4-8 partial sums unrolled, combined at the end) — classic dependency-chain break, the dsyrk microkernel structure. Optionally MR x NR register-tile the symmetric Gram (compute a small block of cells against shared centered rows) for reuse. Also fix the triangle LOAD IMBALANCE: par_chunks_mut(n_vars) gives row i only i+1 dots, so row m-1 does m x the work of row 0 — flatten the lower triangle into equal-work cell ranges.

TARGET: >=3x on the (50,1000) regime, no regression on the already-winning (500,500)/(50,10000) regimes. Score>=2.0.

BLOCKERS / OBLIGATIONS:
1. PARITY TEST IS EXACT-REPR. cov_matches_numpy_* and corrcoef_matches_numpy_* use assert_array_matches_numpy = exact tolist() repr compare. Multi-accumulator changes summation ORDER => last-ULP divergence from numpy's BLAS even on small inputs (4-5 obs test cases) once the combine order differs from serial. numpy's cov contract is allclose, so exact-repr is STRICTER than numpy requires. MUST relax cov/corrcoef matchers to np.allclose(rtol/atol) FIRST (or prove small-input bit-stability), as a deliberate parity-contract decision.
2. CLEAN A/B: needs box load < ~8 for reliable measurement (GEMM-microkernel ratios are bandwidth/scheduling sensitive; measured under load 18 here). Use rch criterion same-worker A/B.
3. Keep serial==parallel bit-identical within fnp (each cell still one independent dot); gate fan-out on work as today.

Found via gapsweep2 (2026-06-14); cov/corrcoef were the ONLY vs-numpy gap across ~50 ops swept (3 sweeps) — everything else parity-or-faster (unique/cumsum/searchsorted/median/percentile/nan*/char.*/triu/outer all fnp-faster). This is the next no-gaps lever after the set-op bitmap family (80305403/217d6f27/a6c7122e) shipped 6-65x.
notes:
REFUTED LEVER (2026-06-14, dot8 single-cell multi-accumulator): replaced the naive single-accumulator dot with an 8-lane multi-accumulator dot8() (chunks_exact(8) + 8 independent partial sums, autovectorizing; n<8 stays bit-identical so the exact-repr conformance tests — all <=5 obs — pass unchanged, parity allclose for n>=8). Measured NO improvement: cov (50,1000) 4.28x -> 4.16x (noise), (200,1000) 1.38->1.25x, (500,500) 0.86->0.82x. REVERTED.

WHY IT FAILED / SHARPENED DIAGNOSIS: the (50,1000) gram is MEMORY-TRAFFIC-bound, not dot-latency-bound. centered is row-major (n_vars x n_obs); the lower triangle rereads each cj row once per i>j, streaming ~sum_i (i+1)*n_obs*8 bytes (~10MB for 50x1000) from L2/L3 since the 400KB working set exceeds L2. The dot's dependency chain was never the bottleneck. numpy's dsyrk blocks an MR x NR tile of output cells and reuses each loaded panel of centered rows across the whole tile, cutting memory traffic ~MR-fold.

NEXT ATTEMPT (go straight here, skip dot accumulator tweaks): MR x NR register-blocked symmetric gram — load MR centered rows + NR centered rows into the tile, compute the MRxNR block of dots reusing them (panel-packing optional), only over the lower-triangular block range. Combine with the triangle load-balance fix (flatten to equal-work cell ranges, not per-row). Keep serial==parallel bit-identical. Conformance tests stay green only if the small-input (<8 obs / <tile) path remains the serial left-to-right sum. Needs quiet box for clean rch criterion A/B; target >=3x at (50,1000), no regression at (500,500)/(50,10000).
close_reason: Measured no-ship: MR=4 row-blocked cov_gram_rowvar_f64 candidate preserved left-to-right per-cell dot order but did not clear the Score>=2.0 keep gate. Same-worker RCH vmi1152480 Criterion row python_statistics_boundary/fnp_cov_rowvar_f64_200x500 baseline on clean ab8fe715 was 4.7898/5.6907/6.6160 ms; candidate was 4.1415/4.9610/5.7133 ms, only ~1.15x median with overlapping intervals. Source hunk unwound to zero diff. Route deeper: not another row micro-tile; next primitive should change memory layout/data movement, e.g. packed/transposed centered panels or a real symmetric rank-k panel kernel that reuses contiguous observation-major panels while preserving cov/corrcoef tolerance/golden proof and no regression on 500x500/50x10000.

title: Refresh central Phase2C porting ledger to ready packet evidence
description:
## What
Update artifacts/contracts/PORTING_ESSENCE_EXTRACTION_LEDGER_V1.md so FNP-P2C-001 through FNP-P2C-009 no longer report stale anchor-only/open/partial/missing packet-artifact status when their packet_readiness_report.json files are ready with zero missing artifacts, missing fields, and parse errors.

## Why
This file is the central semantic control plane for the clean-room parity wave. Agents use it to decide what proof is missing; stale rows waste swarm effort and can cause duplicate stale-evidence beads after packet-local evidence is already green.

## How
For each packet row, cite current packet-local evidence: legacy_anchor_map.md, behavior_extraction_ledger.md, contract_table.md, risk_note.md, implementation_plan.md, unit_property_evidence.json, differential/metamorphic/adversarial evidence where present, workflow replay artifacts, parity_report.json, RaptorQ/scrub/decode artifacts, final_evidence_pack.json, and packet_readiness_report.json. Keep true residual parity debt explicit instead of claiming full NumPy breadth closure.

## Risks
Do not convert packet readiness into full legacy parity. Ready means required packet artifacts are present and validator-clean; remaining edge breadth stays parity debt.

## Validation
Run: targeted stale-text rg on the central ledger, jq readiness summary for FNP-P2C-001..009, git diff --check, ubs on the ledger, and rch exec -- cargo run -p fnp-conformance --bin validate_phase2c_packet for a representative packet set or all packets if cheap enough.

title: [perf][fnp-linalg] det ~3x slower than numpy at n=512 (LU-driven determinant)
description: np.linalg.det(SPD) n=512: fnp 8.49ms vs numpy 2.88ms = 2.95x (perf_linalg 2026-06-14; n=1024 numpy LAPACK was thrashing so that ratio is confounded — the n=512 numpy 2.88ms is the clean non-thrash baseline). det = LU factorization then product of U's diagonal. The 3x suggests fnp's LU (the det path) lags numpy's dgetrf blocking/parallelism at this size, OR det does extra work (full L,U materialize vs in-place). Profile det_nxn / its LU; if the LU is the shared solve/inv kernel it may already be fine (solve/inv WIN here, though numpy thrashed those) — confirm det isn't doing a redundant copy/transpose. Lower priority (small absolute, partly numpy-noise-confounded). Verify on a quiet box. Found by cc 2026-06-14.
close_reason: Shipped fnp-linalg det/LU mid-size panel retune. Profile target was serial LU panel at n=512. One lever: LU_PANEL_NB 128 -> 64, reducing panel serial fraction while preserving pivot sequence. Same-worker RCH vmi1227854 det_nxn/size/512 baseline [5.8224, 6.0551, 6.3171] ms; post-change [4.3116, 4.3975, 4.4856] ms = 1.38x median. Adjacent sweep with nb=64: det 512 [4.3546, 4.4236, 4.4971] ms, 768 [13.407, 13.627, 13.857] ms, 1024 [31.473, 31.960, 32.466] ms. Behavior proof: new lu_panel_64_mid_size_keeps_pivots_logdet_and_golden_sha256 verifies serial-reference pivot order, determinant sign/log tolerance, and packed-LU golden sha256 0c94d84ca8e58ff7ce27babdac9d584397074a53770050be8151b9482ee586de. Validation: RCH cargo test -p fnp-linalg lu_panel_64_mid_size_keeps_pivots_logdet_and_golden_sha256 --release passed; RCH cargo check -p fnp-linalg --all-targets passed; RCH cargo clippy -p fnp-linalg --all-targets -- -D warnings passed; git diff --check clean. cargo fmt -p fnp-linalg --check and UBS still report broad pre-existing inventory unrelated to this two-hunk change.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: PROFILED (cc 2026-06-14): det = lu_decompose_for_det (blocked LU, n>=LU_BLOCK_MIN=512) then product of U diagonal. The blocked LU's TRAILING update (packed_gemm_sub_assign_strided, k=LU_PANEL_NB=128) is ALREADY parallel (k=128 trips the >=128 gate). The bottleneck is the SERIAL PANEL factorization (lib.rs ~592-643): partial-pivot column-max search + the rank-1 panel-column updates (~n²·bw serial flops) + the U12 triangular solve. For n=512,bw=128 the panel is 1/4 the matrix width = a large serial fraction. LEVER = RECURSIVE LU PANEL (Toledo recursive right-looking LU): factor the bw-wide panel by recursive halving so its level-2 rank-1 work becomes level-3 GEMM (reuses packed_gemm, parallel). Keeps the SAME partial-pivot sequence (recursive LU pivots identically) → matches within LU's allclose contract. Note det is P3: small absolute (numpy 2.88ms@512) and the n=1024 ratio was numpy-LAPACK-thrash-confounded. Lower value than eigvalsh SBR (0kqvd).

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: DEFER (cc 2026-06-15, box load ~13 so perf A/B unreliable per 'verify on quiet box'). Recursive-LU-panel lever BLOCKED as a clean win: det/inv/slogdet/solve locked BYTE-FOR-BYTE by golden tests (lib.rs:490); a recursive panel changes trailing-GEMM reassociation -> not byte-identical -> needs coordinated allclose-relaxation + golden re-pin across all 4 LU-family ops. High blast radius, P3 value (numpy 2.88ms@512, partly thrash-confounded). Pivoting to load-independent parity differentials.

title: [perf][fnp-python][no-gaps] zero-copy einsum matvec/vecmat ('ij,j->i','ij,i->j',full) — up to 81x self, beats numpy
close_reason: Shipped. einsum two-operand matrix-vector contractions fell to the general Cartesian kernel after extracting both operands: ij,j-> was 11.9x slower than numpy, ij,i->j 62x, ij,j->i 2.7x. Added try_zerocopy_f64_einsum_matvec (runs before extraction): the vector contracts one matrix axis (second/column label = contiguous matvec A dot v; first/row label = strided vecmat A-transpose dot v). Column-contraction: each output is an 8-accumulator dot of a contiguous matrix row against the once-copied vector. Row-contraction (keep-col): stream each row, fused multiply-add of row times v[i] into a length-ncols accumulator kept cache-resident, ascending-i per column (the strided keep-column reduction done as ONE contiguous pass over A instead of numpy strided column gather). Output keeps the surviving label or empty (scalar). Zero-copy PyBuffer, 2-D matrix + matching 1-D vector, f64, contiguous; PyBuffer cells !Sync so serial but 8 indep accumulators + single streaming pass match numpy bandwidth. einsum parity allclose; all close=True. Self-speedup 64-core 1000x1000: ij,j->i 952 to 144us (0.34x vs numpy FASTER); ij,j-> 5053 to 118us (0.35x FASTER); ij,i->j 12689 to 156us 81x (0.78x FASTER); ij,i-> 164us (1.28x, massive self-speedup over general kernel). Proof: einsum_full_contraction_fast_path_matches_numpy extended with all 4 matvec/vecmat cases (distinct matrix+vector shapes, allclose+dtype); all einsum unit+integration tests green; freshness behavioral probe correct. Found via einsum_patterns.py. Remaining einsum: i,j->ij outer still ~20x for large (build-copy floored, bead-tracked).

title: perf(fnp-python): delegate large f64 argmin/argmax/ptp to numpy SIMD (2-3.5x)
close_reason: Shipped. argmin/argmax zerocopy path copies the whole f64 buffer to a Vec then scans (2 passes vs numpy's 1 fused SIMD pass) = 2.5x slow; ptp does native max-min over an owned Vec = 3.8x slow. Gated both to delegate to numpy for f64 full-reductions >=4096 (argmin/argmax via try_zerocopy_f64_argextreme; ptp via size+dtype check before extract). A/B old/new: argmin 1.55x[16k]/2.49x[1M], argmax 1.43/2.52, ptp 2.10/3.50. Parity byte-exact (ties->first-index, NaN, int unchanged-and-faster, f64). conformance_argmax 10 + conformance_argmin 9 pass (local). Int argmin/argmax NOT delegated (fnp faster at large). Also cleaned 3 orphaned ufunc imports (copysign/fmod/heaviside) from last commit. Follow-up: all/nanmax/nanmin same lever.

title: [perf][fnp-python] 1-arg where + bool diff -> numpy (15-698x slow path eliminated)

Two extract-pattern gaps: (1) 1-arg np.where(cond) == np.nonzero(cond) ran extract_precise_numeric_array (f64 bridge) + native where_nonzero scan: 54x slow on a bool mask (83ms vs 1.5ms @4M) and 15x on f64 (132ms vs 8.7ms). numpy's nonzero is the parity reference and optimal for every dtype (fnp's own nonzero is already a numpy passthrough), so the whole 1-arg form now defers to numpy. (2) np.diff(bool) fell through the zero-copy f64/int/f32 diff paths to the f64-bridge extract: 698x slow (85ms vs 122us); numpy's diff(bool) (adjacent XOR, bool output) is optimal so bool defers to numpy (checked only on the fallthrough, f64/int/f32 unaffected). Score @4M: where(bool) 54x->0.99x, where(f64) 15x->0.97x, diff(bool) 698x->1.04x. Parity (dtype+value, tuple-aware) across where 1-arg bool/int/f64/2d/all-zero + 3-arg regression, diff bool/n=2/2d-axis/int8/f64. 17 where+diff conformance tests green. Found via bool-input gap sweep.

title: [bug][fnp-python][parity] clip rejects numpy-2.0 min=/max= kwargs (only took positional a_min/a_max)
description: np.clip in numpy 2.x is clip(a, min=None, max=None, ...) with each bound independently optional; legacy a_min/a_max still accepted but only as a pair and not mixable with min/max. fnp.clip required positional a_min/a_max and raised TypeError on clip(a, min=2, max=4) / clip(a, min=2) / clip(a, max=4) — the modern documented spelling. Found by cc 2026-06-15 via diff_kwargs_cc_0615.py. FIX: signature (a, a_min=None, a_max=None, out=None, min=None, max=None, **kwargs); legacy+new mix delegates to numpy (reproduces its TypeError); else collapse to effective lo=a_min.or(min)/hi=a_max.or(max). Verified all-match incl error-parity for mixed spellings.
close_reason: Added min=/max= kwarg aliases (each optional); mixed spellings delegate to numpy for exact error-parity. Fixed in 30418218, conformance green (17).

title: [perf] convolve/correlate FFT gate ~2000x too low — routes medium 1-D problems to slow FFT (5-9x vs direct); replace with cost-model gate
close_reason: Replaced convolve_mode's FFT gate (n.min(m)>=64 && n*m>=2^18) with a cost-model gate: FFT only when n*m >= 400*fft_len*log2(fft_len) (fft_len=next_pow2(n+m-1)). The old 2^18 threshold was ~2000x too low — it sent every medium 1-D convolve/correlate to the ~1ms-fixed-cost fft_linear_convolve when the PARALLEL direct SAXPY (which already beats numpy's always-direct kernel) is far faster. Same-run rch criterion A/B (direct vs FFT): 5000x200 -79% (4.7x), 50000x500 -90% (9.8x), 2000x2000 -47%, 100000x1000 -84%; 200000x4000 direct wins 2.6x (7.6 vs 20ms). FFT correctly RETAINED for huge kernels: 100000x20000 FFT 15ms beats direct 67ms (-79%). Crossover (n*m/fft_work) measured: 170=direct, 898=FFT -> factor 400 between them. Parity: convolve parity is tolerance-based (allclose); convolve_fft_matches_direct_within_tolerance + 17 convolve unit tests green; direct is numpy's own method so using it more = strictly closer to numpy. correlate inherits via correlate_mode. NOTE: machine load spiked to 172 mid-investigation; final absolute numbers are same-run A/Bs (both arms equal load), not cross-baseline. Follow-up: parallel direct chunk-sizing for huge m underutilizes cores (6 chunks at m=20000).

title: [bug][parity] Generator.integers rejects int8/int16/uint8/uint16 (numpy supports); needs buffered narrow-width Lemire sampler in fnp-random
description:
Generator.integers(dtype=int8/int16/uint8/uint16) raises 'Unsupported dtype dtype(...) for integers' (validate_random_integer_dtype_bounds in fnp-python lib.rs only handles I32/I64/U32/U64). numpy's Generator.integers supports all of these.

CANNOT be fixed by generating i64 and casting: numpy's narrow-width integers use a DIFFERENT, width-specific bounded-integer stream. Verified empirically: numpy int8 != numpy int64-cast-to-int8 (same seed). fnp.integers IS bit-exact vs numpy for int32/int64/uint32 (fnp-random has bounded_lemire_uint32/uint64 at lib.rs ~3535/3556), so the fix must add the matching narrow-width samplers.

Correct fix (fnp-random core, delicate): add bounded_lemire_uint8/uint16 matching numpy's _bounded_integers.pyx buffered_bounded_lemire_uint8/16 — numpy BUFFERS leftover bytes from a single uint32 draw across consecutive narrow samples, so bit-exact parity requires replicating that buffer state machine exactly. Then width-parameterize integers_shaped/integers_endpoint_shaped to dispatch by dtype width, add I8/I16/U8/U16 arms to validate_random_integer_dtype_bounds (fnp-python lib.rs ~3657) and confirm random_integer_storage handles them. Gate with an oracle differential (capture_numpy_oracle) across widths/ranges/endpoint. Found 2026-06-17 while sweeping Generator dtype parity (the choice/permutation/shuffle/permuted f64-dtype family, beads 4byse/id2c9, is already fixed).
close_reason: Implemented NumPy-width Generator.integers parity for int8/int16/uint8/uint16: fnp-random now has buffered uint8/uint16 Lemire samplers that consume leftover bytes/halfwords from uint32 draws, fnp-python dispatches narrow dtypes to typed storage instead of i64 generation/cast, dtype bounds/storage support narrow integer dtypes, and oracle tests cover signed/unsigned/bounded/endpoint/scalar cases. Validation: RCH cargo check -p fnp-random exit 0; RCH cargo check -p fnp-python exit 0 with pre-existing dead_code warnings; git diff --check clean; UBS ran on crates/fnp-random/src/lib.rs and crates/fnp-python/src/lib.rs, fmt/clippy/check/test-build sections clean, nonzero only from pre-existing broad inventory.

title: [perf][no-gaps][fnp-linalg] LU/det narrow-panel lever — NO-SHIP (gap is GEMM-microkernel ceiling, not serial panel)
close_reason: NO-SHIP, sub-2.0. Hypothesis: blocked-LU serial panel factorization (step 1) + serial U12 solve (step 2) dominate the det/slogdet/inv 2.1-2.27x-vs-numpy gap at n=512 (numpy det 3.5-3.9ms STABLE baseline; solve/inv/eigvals thrash to 400-1200ms under load = untrustworthy). Lever: LU_PANEL_NB 128->64 (halve serial panel work) + packed_gemm_sub_assign_strided_rowpar (band trailing GEMM over output rows since k=bw=64 < general k>=128 parallel gate). RESULT: det Score old-fnp/new-fnp = 0.96[512]/1.21[1024]/1.07[2048], n=512 REGRESSED. Narrowing the panel barely moved det => serial panel is NOT the bottleneck; the gap is GEMM-MICROKERNEL THROUGHPUT (fnp safe-Rust register-tiled ~5.7 GFLOP/s vs OpenBLAS getrf ~13; no FMA for bit-parity). Same no-FMA ceiling as matmul/dot, BLOCKED on franken_numpy-8vdtg (vetted std::arch AVX2/FMA). DO NOT re-attempt LU panel-width tuning or recursive dgetrf2 panel — wrong layer. Change stashed cc-noship-lu-narrow-panel-64-sub2.0-gemm-ceiling (2 inv golden digests re-blessed there, reconstruction <1e-7 held, valid but moot). 291 lib tests passed pre-revert.

title: [test][flaky] mr_sort_then_sum_equals_sum proptest tolerance too tight for ill-conditioned sums
description: metamorphic_proptest.rs:182 asserts sum(sort(x)) == sum(x) within rel_tol = sqrt(n)*1e-12*|sum|.max(1.0). Proptest discovered a failing case (persisted in crates/fnp-ufunc/tests/metamorphic_proptest.proptest-regressions) where reordering by sort changes the f64 accumulation enough to exceed this tolerance — classic catastrophic-cancellation: when sum_original.abs() << max|x_i|, the |sum|.max(1.0) factor floors rel_tol at ~sqrt(n)*1e-12 which is far smaller than the actual reordering error (~eps*sum|x_i|). PRE-EXISTING (reproduces on clean HEAD with the regressions file present; independent of the einsum Hadamard work). FIX: scale rel_tol by sum of magnitudes (sum|x_i|), not by |sum(x)| — e.g. rel_tol = n.sqrt()*1e-12*x.iter().map(|v|v.abs()).sum(). This is the correct conditioning bound for floating summation reordering.
close_reason: Fixed in 2a213e51: tolerance now scales by Sum|x_i| (summation conditioning) instead of |Sum(x)|.max(1). 22 proptests green, 6000 fresh cases pass.
