title: [perf][fnp-linalg] recursive (cache-oblivious) Cholesky to kill the scalar diagonal factor — residual 1.4-1.7x vs numpy after panel fix
close_reason: NO-SHIP (implemented + A/B-proven regression). Implemented in-place recursive (cache-oblivious) potrf: split sz->h+h, recurse L11, parallel TRSM (packed L11 + contiguous l21), then trailing SYRK via packed_gemm_sub_assign_strided. Tried both full-square SYRK (2x flops, lost badly) and lower-only tiled SYRK (half flops). Env-gated A/B (CHOL_REC=1 vs 0, same binary). Result: recursive REGRESSES or ties the existing cholesky_blocked at every size (n640 blocked 1.19x vs recursive 3.19x; n896 2.37 vs 2.57; n1024 2.10 vs 2.17; n1536 1.67 vs 1.63; n2048 1.71 vs 1.85). Parity was GOOD (allclose vs numpy, recon<1e-9). WHY: cholesky_blocked is already well-tuned — at panel=32 its trailing update is a hand-written PARALLEL lower-only rank-32 SYRK (cache-resident l21), not a skinny serial GEMM as I'd assumed; the recursion's per-level l21/l11/bblk allocations + transpose-pack + deeper call tree outweigh the larger-rank GEMM benefit. ROOT GAP IS FMA, NOT STRUCTURE: numpy=OpenBLAS 0.3.31 (Haswell AVX2+FMA); fnp packed_gemm at n=1024 = 76 GF/s vs OpenBLAS 97 GF/s = 1.28x, because .cargo/config disables +fma (for bit-exact parity; mul_add compiles to SOFTWARE fma w/o the feature). Cholesky's 1.4-1.7x residual is floor-limited by this same ~1.28x GEMM throughput gap. Closing it needs hardware FMA via #[target_feature]/std::arch (unsafe) OR loosening matmul/LU bit-exact conformance to allclose — both are the 8vdtg arch decision. Recursive potrf cannot beat the FMA floor and adds overhead, so it's not the lever. Reverted.

title: [perf][fnp-ufunc] SIMD-vectorize last-axis (inner==1) f64 ptp lane scan
description: gap_sweep2 (2026-06-13): np.ptp(M[1000,1000],axis=1) np=247us fnp=1783us = 7.2x (verified-ok). UFuncArray::ptp (lib.rs ~23357) last-axis branch uses a SCALAR single-pass lane_ptp (if mn<v{mn}else{v} per element) over each contiguous row; numpy uses SIMD vmaxpd/vminpd. The sibling inner>1 (non-last-axis, e.g. axis0) branch ALREADY has a register-blocked portable-SIMD reduction (LANES=8, simd_lt/simd_gt select + nan accumulator) — apply the SAME pattern to the last-axis case but as a HORIZONTAL reduction WITHIN each contiguous row (accumulate mn/mx/nan SIMD vectors over LANES-chunks of the row, then reduce_min/reduce_max + scalar tail). PARITY PROOF (already worked out — signed-zero is SAFE for ptp specifically): ptp value = mx - mn; the only signed-zero divergence would be a -0.0 vs +0.0 result, which requires mx=-0.0 AND mn=+0.0 simultaneously — impossible since reduce_max>=reduce_min (max prefers +0.0, min prefers -0.0). For all-equal/all-zero rows scalar keep-2nd makes mn==mx==last-element so ptp=+0.0; SIMD reduce gives mx-mn=+0.0 too. When mn/mx is zero but the other operand nonzero, x-(±0)=x and (±0)-x=-x so sign-of-zero never affects the result. => bit-identical to the scalar two-reduction reference for ALL inputs. Proof obligations: keep ptp_axis_single_pass_golden_sha256 + ptp_axis_single_pass_matches_two_reduction_reference + ptp_axis_parallel_matches_serial_reference_bits green; add a signed-zero/NaN/inf row differential. CAVEAT: measured under load 7-10 — the 7.2x is PARTLY rayon-starvation (parallel fnp vs single-threaded numpy SIMD), like einsum_outer; re-measure at low load to confirm the SIMD compute win is real (target the per-row scalar->SIMD ratio ~4x, NOT the raw 7.2x). Do NOT just delegate to numpy (NO-GAPS: build our own kernel). int ptp + full-reduction f64 (already delegated) out of scope.
close_reason: NO-SHIP (Score~1.0-1.2x, memory-bound). Implemented the SIMD horizontal-reduction last-axis lane_ptp (bit-identical: golden ptp_axis_single_pass_golden_sha256 + both differential refs stayed GREEN, confirming the signed-zero invariance proof). But A/B at low load (same rayon pool, both parallel): [1000,1000] par_scalar=0.327ms par_simd=0.273ms ser_simd=0.243ms; [15625,256] par_scalar=1.083 par_simd=0.898 ser_simd=1.928. SIMD is ~1.0-1.2x and NOISY — the per-row scan is MEMORY-BANDWIDTH-bound, not compute-bound, so vectorizing the inner loop barely moves it. ROOT CAUSE OF THE ORIGINAL 7.2x: it was a HIGH-LOAD (7-10) ARTIFACT — fnp's parallel scan gets starved while numpy's single-threaded SIMD streaming is unaffected. At normal load fnp KERNEL is at PARITY with numpy (Rust 327us vs numpy 329us @1000x1000); the python-boundary residual (3.13x@1000² shrinking to 1.25x@2000²) is PyO3 MARSHALLING overhead (array extract+construct ~700us), NOT the kernel — a cross-cutting concern, not ptp-specific. Reverted the SIMD change (no regression but fails Score>=2.0 + adds complexity). DON'T re-attempt last-axis ptp SIMD or de-parallelization (serial is worse: ser_scalar 0.95ms). Real residual = PyO3 small-array overhead, uniform across ops.

title: [perf][fnp-python] cumsum/cumprod bool zero-copy (uint8 view -> int64) — 4-15x slow -> 0.05-0.09x (beats numpy)
close_reason: Shipped. np.cumsum/cumprod on a BOOL array fell through the int zero-copy helpers (gated on kind i/u only) to the f64-bridge extract path: cumprod(bool) 15x slow (105ms vs 7.6ms @4M), cumsum(bool) 3.7x. numpy promotes bool->int64 for both. Added a bool branch to try_zerocopy_int_cumsum/_cumprod (+ their _axis variants): read the 0/1 bytes via a zero-copy uint8 view ('?' format rejects PyBuffer<u8>), feed the existing cumsum_typed core with an int64 accumulator (wrapping add/mul; never wraps for 0/1). Score @4M: cumprod 0.09x (11x faster, ~36x self), cumsum 0.05x (20x faster). Parity (dtype int64 + value array_equal) across 1d/all-true/with-zero/empty/scalar/2d-axis{0,1,None}. Found via gap_sweep (cumprod_bool flag). Sibling of the bitwise_count POPCNT extract fix.

title: [perf][fnp-linalg] eigvalsh values-only tridiagonal solve ~3-5x slow vs numpy — implement sqrt-free dsterf (Pal-Walker-Kahan)
close_reason: Partially shipped a44dbead. CORRECTED root-cause (the dsterf hypothesis in the title was WRONG): a load-robust ratio (fnp eigvalsh time ~= 0.5x fnp eigh time, i.e. eigvalsh ~= the shared Householder reduction since its values-only iteration is O(n^2) cheap) localized the gap to the TRIDIAGONAL REDUCTION, not the tridiagonal eigensolve. The blocked (BLAS-3) reduction only engaged at n>=384 (TRIDIAG_BLOCK_MIN); 64<=n<384 ran the unblocked BLAS-2 loop. Lowered the threshold 384->64 (clean same-binary low-load A/B confirmed blocked wins for BOTH values-only AND Q-accumulating paths from n>=64). Result: eigvalsh self-speedup 1.33-1.73x and eigh 1.34-1.92x for n in [64,384); eigvalsh vs numpy ~5x -> ~3x. Parity allclose (recon<1e-9, max err 3.8e-13), 2 goldens re-pinned, full suite green. RESIDUAL ~3x eigvalsh gap = fnp's blocked tridiag_reduce_blocked is still ~2.5x slower than numpy's dsytrd (BLAS-3 fraction of the symmetric two-sided update + the FMA floor, ~1.28x). Filed follow-up.

title: [idea-wizard] Phase2C control-ledger convergence wave
description:
## Background
The active tracker was empty after the Phase2C stale-evidence closeouts, but a fresh idea-wizard research pass found a non-duplicate control-plane gap: every packet readiness report from FNP-P2C-001 through FNP-P2C-009 is ready with zero missing artifacts/fields/parse errors, while artifacts/contracts/PORTING_ESSENCE_EXTRACTION_LEDGER_V1.md still describes several packet rows as anchor-only, partial, open, or missing packet artifacts.

## Goal
Converge the central Phase2C semantic ledger with the packet-local ready evidence, then add fail-closed checks and agent-readable reports so future swarms do not trust stale proof status.

## Non-goals
Do not weaken the full-parity doctrine, remove residual parity debt, or claim packet breadth beyond the packet-local evidence. This wave updates control-plane truth and creates validators; it does not change runtime NumPy behavior.

## Success criteria
- The central ledger rows reflect ready packet artifacts for FNP-P2C-001..009 while keeping real residual breadth debt explicit.
- A verifier can fail when the central ledger says stale open/partial/anchor-only claims for packets whose readiness reports are ready.
- The workflow has unit tests/e2e logging and rch-backed validation commands.
- bv reports no dependency cycles and at least one actionable bead after planning.
close_reason: Planning container completed: created Phase2C control-ledger convergence child beads deadlock-audit-nopu2, deadlock-audit-i65xx, deadlock-audit-lg4cg, deadlock-audit-ymhhg, and deadlock-audit-pn5d4 with dependency ordering and validation requirements.

title: [perf][fnp-linalg][no-gaps] mid-size cholesky (128<=n<896) runs trailing SYRK serially — panel=32 < parallel-GEMM gate
description:
np.linalg.cholesky is consistently SLOWER than numpy's dpotrf on SPD f64 (numpy NOT thrashing here, legit gap): cholesky 512 fnp 9.64ms vs numpy 3.14ms = 3.07x; cholesky 1024 fnp 26.45ms vs numpy 12.89ms = 2.05x (perf_linalg sweep 2026-06-14, box load ~6). cholesky_512 win would be larger relative gap than 1024.

ROOT (read of cholesky_blocked, fnp-linalg/src/lib.rs:1369): the size dispatch (cholesky_nxn:1531) uses CHOL_PANEL_NB=128 only for n>=CHOL_BLOCK_MIN=896; for CHOL_MID_MIN=128<=n<896 it uses CHOL_MID_PANEL=32. The trailing rank-bw SYRK update (step 3, the O(n^2*bw) BULK of cholesky flops) only takes the PARALLEL packed_gemm_sub_assign_strided path when 'bw >= MATMUL_PARALLEL_MIN_DIM(=128) && trail >= SYRK_COL_BLOCK(=256)' (line 1467). With mid-panel bw=32 < 128 that test is FALSE, so the dominant trailing update for ALL 128<=n<896 falls into the ELSE small-panel branch (line 1493): a single serial packed_gemm(&l21,&l21t,trail,bw,trail) + serial subtract. So mid-size cholesky does its bulk flops single-threaded => the 3x gap at n=512 (16 panels of 32, each with a serial 480x480-ish trailing GEMM). At n=1024 panel=128 hits the parallel SYRK => only 2.05x. The recent 'shrink mid cholesky panel' commit (1382622d) tuned diagonal/TRSM but left the mid-size trailing SYRK serial.

LEVER (no-ceiling, attack the bulk): make the mid-size trailing SYRK parallel. Options: (a) parallelize the else-branch small-panel SYRK over trailing output row-bands (rayon par_chunks_mut over work rows, each band subtracts its l21·l21t block — the lower-triangle-only tiling already in the bw>=128 path generalizes; just drop/lower the bw>=128 gate and instead gate on TOTAL trailing work trail*trail*bw), OR (b) raise CHOL_MID_PANEL so bw>=128 (but that enlarges the serial diagonal-block factor + may hurt the 128..256 range — measure). Prefer (a): gate the parallel SYRK on trail*trail*bw work, not on bw alone, so a thin (bw=32) but wide (trail~480) trailing update still parallelizes.

OBLIGATIONS: cholesky is allclose-contract in numpy but fnp likely has an EXACT-repr golden (check cholesky_matches_numpy_* + any golden sha256). Parallelizing the SYRK must keep each cell's subtraction order identical (each (i,j) cell still subtracts the same bw-term dot in the same order) => BIT-IDENTICAL, no parity relaxation (same discipline as the cov register-tile fallback on deadlock-audit-mtjkm). Prove with golden sha256 of L across SPD fixtures (incl n=129,255,512,895 to cover mid-range + boundaries) baseline-vs-new + the cholesky conformance tests. Clean rch criterion or pinned-thread A/B; target >=2x at n=512, no regression at n=1024/n<128/n>=896. NOTE perf_linalg also flagged eigvalsh 1024 4.2x and det512 2.95x as separate (numpy-thrash-confounded) candidates.

Found via cc perf_linalg sweep 2026-06-14 while deadlock-audit-mtjkm (cov) was peer-owned; cholesky is the cleanest uncontended consistent vs-numpy gap (2-3x both sizes, numpy not thrashing).
close_reason: Shipped ff302aa3: thin-panel cholesky trailing SYRK parallelized over the trailing-row dimension. BYTE-IDENTICAL (golden fbe05ee0 matches baseline across n=129..1024; all cholesky tests green; allclose vs numpy). Self-speedup n=895 2.3x, n=768 1.6-1.8x; n=512 flips from 3.07x-slower-than-numpy to 0.92x (beats numpy). No regression on n<416 / n>=896 (unchanged paths).

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: REFINEMENT (cc): lever (a) is NOT a one-line gate change — packed_gemm_sub_assign_strided ALSO gates its internal row-parallelism on k>=MATMUL_PARALLEL_MIN_DIM=128 (line 5979), and SYRK's k=bw=32 is small BY DESIGN, so merely lowering the line-1467 dispatch gate still runs serial inside the gemm. Need a SYRK-specific parallelization that fans out over the large trailing-row dimension (m~480) independent of the small k. Bit-identity is achievable: test packed_gemm_sub_assign_strided_matches_materialized_product_sha256 (line 8629) proves the tiled sub-assign path == the materialized packed_gemm product byte-for-byte, so routing n=512 through a row-parallel version of the SAME serial kernel (packed_gemm_sub_assign_strided_serial dispatched over disjoint row-bands, as the existing parallel path does — just without the k>=128 guard) stays byte-identical to today's else-branch output => no golden drift. Cleanest impl: add a work-gated (trail*trail*bw) row-band rayon fan-out in cholesky_blocked's trailing SYRK that calls packed_gemm_sub_assign_strided_serial per band, bypassing the inner k>=128 gate. Verify golden L sha256 unchanged at n=512 + conformance green.

title: [perf][fnp-python] cov/corrcoef 2.3-2.9x for tall-thin (non-rowvar-fastpath) inputs
description: gap_sweep3 (2026-06-13): np.cov(M[100,1000]) np=299us fnp=867us=2.90x; np.corrcoef(M[100,1000]) np=353us fnp=803us=2.27x (verified ok). try_zerocopy_cov_rowvar_f64 / corrcoef_rowvar_f64 EXIST but the [100,1000] case (100 vars x 1000 obs) apparently misses them or the fast path is sub-parity for this shape. Investigate which path [100,1000] hits and whether the GEMM-based covariance (X@X.T / (n-1)) is using the cache-blocked matmul. Lower priority (smaller absolute, GEMM-microkernel-bound likely).
close_reason: PARTIAL ship (6982271e): gated the cov/corrcoef Gram parallelism — small inputs were ~5x numpy purely from rayon dispatch on microseconds of work. cov([10,100]) 106us->8.1us=13x (now 2.4x FASTER than numpy), cov([20,200]) 3.4x. Bit-identical (serial/parallel dot order same per cell). MEDIUM/LARGE cov (e.g. [100,1000] 1.9x, [50,500] 3.6x, [10,20000] 2.7x) remains numpy-slower: profiled to ~7.3 GFLOP/s aggregate (memory-bandwidth + no-FMA; parallel barely helps — Gram re-streams centered rows). That residual is a cache-blocked SYRK microkernel + FMA = 8vdtg territory, NOT a quick lever. NO-SHIP attempted first: 8-accumulator dot (dot_f64_multiacc) was only 1.0-1.07x (LLVM already vectorized the .sum(); dot was not the bottleneck — fixed overhead/dispatch was). Reverted.

title: [perf][fnp-python] cov/corrcoef packed centered-panel Gram kernel
description: Profile-backed follow-up from deadlock-audit-mtjkm. Prior sweep found cov/corrcoef as the remaining vs-numpy gap: np.cov/corrcoef rowvar f64 (50,1000) about 4.3-4.8x slower than numpy; dot8 and MR=4 row-block levers were measured no-ships. Diagnosis from the closed bead: the Gram is memory-traffic-bound because row-major centered rows are reread across the lower triangle. One lever only: change data movement with packed/transposed centered observation panels or a symmetric rank-k panel kernel that reuses contiguous panels while preserving small-input left-to-right exact path and cov/corrcoef tolerance/golden proof. Baseline first with rch Criterion on the existing python_statistics_boundary cov/corrcoef rows, prove parity/golden, keep only Score>=2.0; otherwise close no-ship and route deeper.
close_reason: Measured no-ship. One lever tested: center rowvar f64 input directly into observation-major storage and accumulate the lower-triangular Gram by observation, preserving each cell's increasing-observation FP order. Proof before revert passed against NumPy allclose and golden c805a993cd05cd258efcc1af3680d64ccb775b82bc2de0046efee7496a9b94f7. Same-worker vmi1167313 Criterion regressed target python_statistics_boundary/fnp_cov_rowvar_f64_50x1000 from 1.2329/1.3409/1.4421 ms to 1.5697/1.6002/1.6683 ms; guard row 50x10000 also moved 4.6497/4.8708/5.1176 ms to 4.7980/5.3302/5.8767 ms. Source candidate and proof test manually unwound. Next primitive should avoid per-observation result-matrix RMW traffic, e.g. packed micro-panels or a different cov/corrcoef route with same-worker proof.

title: [bug][fnp-python] around(bool) returned bool but numpy promotes to float16
close_reason: Fixed. np.around(bool_array) PROMOTES to float16 ([1.0,0.0,...]) — a numpy quirk — but fnp's around returned a bool array (the bool input fell through the zero-copy f64/int/f32 paths to the native extract path, which kept bool). Value-equivalent but a dtype parity violation. Routed bool around to numpy (the parity reference) on the fallthrough; f64/int/f32 fast paths unaffected. Parity (dtype float16 + value) across basic/2d/all-true; f64/int/f32 + decimals regression green; 5 around/round conformance tests green. Found via bool-input gap sweep (was also ~1.4x slow). NOTE: clip/nan_to_num/min/max(bool) are correct (parity True) but slow on the f64-bridge extract — left as-is (uncommon bool ops, 80ms/4ms; filed mentally as low-priority).

title: [perf][fnp-ufunc] parallel bucket-select for global median/percentile (target 5-10x)
description: gap_sweep10: np.median/np.percentile (axis=None) on 4M f64 are fnp/np=1.69x/1.62x SLOW — fnp uses serial select_nth_unstable_by(total_cmp) (O(n) introselect) while numpy uses serial introselect too but with raw < compares. REJECTED 2026-06-13: naive parallel quickselect via rayon filter-collect per round REGRESSED to 0.13-0.57x (2-8x slower) — per-round Vec realloc + rayon dispatch dominate this memory-bound op (clip/bincount lesson). REAL LEVER: allocation-free parallel BUCKET-SELECT — narrow a value interval via COUNT-ONLY parallel passes (privatized B-bucket histograms over the live window, NO data movement), find the bucket containing ranks (n-1)/2 and n/2 from cumulative counts, narrow, repeat ~5-6 passes until <few distinct values then collect+sort the tiny survivor set. ~3 full parallel reads (~3ms) vs numpy serial ~29ms = target 5-10x. BIT-EXACT: median value = avg of the two unique order statistics by TOTAL order (handle -0.0<+0.0 via total_cmp consistently, matching select_median); duplicate-heavy + degenerate-range (all-equal) termination via tracking bucket value-range collapse; even-median lo_k/hi_k straddle. Same lever serves percentile (multi-q: one narrowing per q, or shared). Also speeds the per-axis median lanes if generalized. NOT 8vdtg-blocked (pure safe-Rust). Proof: par_select_median_matches_serial_across_distributions test pattern (random/sorted/dup/signed-zero/inf) already drafted.
notes: DONE 2026-06-13: median (2c2ff7b6 2.6-6.4x), scalar-q percentile/quantile (58899374 3-5x), array-q percentile/quantile axis=None (315e3e29 2.0-2.8x [25,50,75]@4M). All via parallel radix-select (count-only privatized histograms, no realloc). Remaining tail: shared multi-target radix narrowing for many-q (5+ q's currently 1.82-1.92x via N separate selects; one narrowing tree for all q's would lift it). Per-axis median/percentile already parallel (across lanes). Closing core; many-q sharing is a minor follow-up.
close_reason: Completed: shared multi-rank radix narrowing for vector-q axis=None percentile/quantile. Proof: percentiles_axis_none_matches_repeated_scalar_and_golden_sha256 passes with golden sha256=233d772184799df965a4237b5eea7ac3951ed90f9c6336e42c3b313871ab839f. Perf RCH vmi1227854: n=4000000 q_count=11 shared=27.644ms vs independent_scalar=90.830ms, speedup=3.29x, output sha256=0e2d63055712d98ff48b1e1df8c9685c2a0a7803b58fb154dcd574a6d26457dc. Python parity: percentile_matches_numpy_across_q_axis_method_and_dtype and quantile_matches_numpy_across_q_axis_method_and_dtype pass. Checks: cargo check -p fnp-ufunc --all-targets clean; cargo check -p fnp-python --all-targets clean. cargo fmt --check and cargo clippy -p fnp-ufunc --all-targets -- -D warnings remain blocked by pre-existing workspace/crate lint debt outside this lever.

title: Wire Phase2C ledger freshness into validation recipes
description:
## What
Expose the Phase2C porting-ledger freshness verifier through the repo's agent-readable validation recipe flow so future agents can discover and run it without remembering the bin name.

## Why
The repo already has validation recipe tooling for agent ergonomics. A verifier that is not discoverable in that flow will be missed during queue-drain sessions.

## How
Add a recipe entry that names the purpose, expected runtime, required artifacts, rch command, output report path, and failure interpretation. Keep it scoped: this is a control-ledger freshness check, not a full workspace test.

## Tests
Update recipe-selector tests to assert the new recipe exists and includes the verifier command plus report path. Include an e2e run of the recipe selector with JSON output.

## Validation
rch exec -- cargo test -p fnp-conformance validation_recipe; rch exec -- cargo run -p fnp-conformance --bin run_validation_recipe_selector -- --format json and jq the new recipe entry.
close_reason: Completed: validation recipe selector now emits the Phase2C porting-ledger freshness verifier for central ledger and packet evidence changes. Validation: rustfmt check on selector passed; git diff --check clean; ubs selector exit 0; remote rch cargo test -p fnp-conformance validation_recipe_selector passed 10 tests exit=0; selector JSON probe emitted phase2c-porting-ledger-freshness with validate_phase2c_porting_ledger command/report; remote rch cargo check -p fnp-conformance --all-targets exit=0; remote rch cargo clippy -p fnp-conformance --all-targets -- -D warnings exit=0.

title: [perf][fnp-python] masked_where plain-ndarray fast path -> numpy (11.8x->1.03x)
close_reason: Shipped. np.ma.masked_where(cond, a) on a PLAIN f64 ndarray ran the generic extract -> combine -> rebuild path: it copies BOTH the condition and the data into owned UFuncArrays then reconstructs the MaskedArray, ~12x slower than numpy (159ms vs 13.5ms @4M f64). For a plain ndarray a (get_type().is(ndarray) excludes MaskedArray subclasses, so a is never already-masked) numpy's own masked_where is the parity reference and far faster, and handles scalar-broadcast conditions, shape-mismatch IndexErrors, and all-False mask shrinking natively. Added an early route to numpy for that case. Score @4M: 11.76x->1.03x. Parity verified (getdata+getmaskarray repr+fill_value) across bool cond, all-false/all-true shrink, scalar True/False cond, int data, int cond array, copy=False, and shape-mismatch IndexError. 2 masked_where conformance tests + 9 masked-constructor tests green. Found via gap_sweep22.

title: [perf][fnp-linalg] cholesky n>=1024 ~3x gap vs numpy (45ms vs 14ms @1024; n=512 at parity) — needs low-load confirm + blocked-potrf block-size/recursive lever
close_reason: Shipped 12178cb9. Root cause was a mis-tuned panel width: cholesky_blocked factors each nb-wide diagonal block with an UNBLOCKED scalar kernel (O(nb^3/3) scalar flops/panel), and the fixed CHOL_PANEL_NB=128 for n>=896 serialized far more scalar work than the parallel trailing GEMM saved. n<896 already used a 32/64 mid panel near parity. Unified into cholesky_panel_width (32 for [640,1280)+small, 64 for >=1280 + [384,640) band). Controlled same-binary env A/B vs numpy: n=896 1.62x->1.04x (gap eliminated), n=1024 2.19x->1.73x, n=1536 1.53x->1.35x, n=2048 1.54x->1.40x; nb=128 was slowest of {32,48,64,96,128} at every size. allclose-parity preserved (recon<1e-9), 378 fnp-linalg tests green. Residual gap (1.73x@1024, ~1.4x large) is the scalar diagonal factor itself -> deeper recursive-Cholesky lever filed separately.

title: perf(fnp-ufunc): merge-based f64 setxor1d (2.6-4.0x)
close_reason: Shipped. f64 setxor1d was the worst set op (~5x vs numpy): it did THREE comparator-closure sorts (a, b, then the result) plus TWO per-element binary-search passes. Replaced with the integer-key sort-unique (sorted_dedup_float_set, from the prior set-op commit) + a single linear two-pointer merge that emits the symmetric difference already sorted. Each side's NaN kept once (NaN!=NaN; np.setxor1d([nan],[nan])==[nan,nan]); +-0.0 equal. Score old/new: 3.98x[20k]/2.64x[200k]. Parity: 600 randomized cases (NaN/+-0/inf/empty/dup) + 1203-case unique/union/setxor sweep byte-match numpy; 54 unit tests. NOTE: a unique()/union1d f64 fast-path was tried+REVERTED — the key-transform's 2 extra passes (build keys, map back) LOSE to those ops' existing PARALLEL in-place f64 sort at large n (union1d regressed to 0.76x@200k). setxor wins because its old path had no parallel sort, just 3 serial sorts.

title: [perf] fnp-ufunc::cov/corrcoef naive path SIMD-vectorized — 2.09x (vs-numpy gap closed)
close_reason: Shipped: cov/corrcoef naive path (nvars<128) now uses portable-SIMD f64x4 lane accumulators (j blocked by 4 for xi reuse). rch Criterion bench cov: cov_96x4000 2.4012ms->1.1486ms (2.09x), cov_50x5000 1.4238ms->0.778ms (1.83x), cov_50x1000 368.6us->237.9us (1.55x). corrcoef inherits (calls cov). Reassociation isomorphism: cov_parallel_matches_serial_mirror_reference now asserts <=1e-9 rel vs serial ascending-k ref + exact symmetry; 26/26 fnp-python cov/corrcoef numpy allclose conformance green; public_api golden byte-unchanged; clippy clean.

title: [perf][fnp-python] argwhere 4.5x slower than numpy (UFuncArray round-trip copies)
description: gap_sweep3 (2026-06-13): np.argwhere(M[1000,1000]>0) np=6140us fnp=27874us=4.54x (verified ok). argwhere() routes input through extract_numeric_array (copies/widens the 1M bool input) -> UFuncArray::argwhere -> build_numpy_array_from_ufunc (copies ~500kx2 index array out). fnp's OWN np.nonzero is already at PARITY (1.00x) so the kernel is fine — the 4.5x is the UFuncArray extract+build round-trip (argwhere == transpose(nonzero)). LEVER: zero-copy argwhere reading the borrowed PyBuffer (bool/numeric), one pass counting nonzeros, write (count,ndim) i64 indices into numpy.empty directly (like try_zerocopy_flatnonzero but 2-D row-major coords). Parity = byte-equal vs numpy.argwhere (deterministic row-major index order). Same PYO3 INPUT-COPY ELIMINATION class as diag(36cefdb2)/ptp(940cc430)/extract(72cef858).
close_reason: Shipped zero-copy try_zerocopy_argwhere: 27874us->4115us=6.8x, now 3x FASTER than numpy. Byte-identical golden 0x9c25806588f3a004.

title: bug: np.cross on int32/uint32 loses precision (f64 products) — wrong wraparound
description: np.cross computes a×b in f64; for int32/uint32 inputs the component products (a[i]*b[j]) exceed 2^53 and the f64 result wraps WRONG when cast back to the narrow int dtype. Repro: cross(uint32[max,1,2], uint32[3,max,1]) -> numpy [3,7,4294967294] but fnp [3,7,0] (the max*max term loses low bits in f64). numpy computes cross in the input integer dtype with two's-complement wraparound. FIX: when UFuncArray::cross inputs carry/synthesize an integer sidecar (incl narrow ints promoted to i64/u64), compute the 2x2 determinant components (a1*b2 - a2*b1 etc.) in i64/u64 with wrapping_mul/wrapping_sub and carry an exact sidecar — same pattern as the trace/prod fixes. Found via fnp-vs-numpy tobytes differential (cross 56/300, int32/uint32 BYTES + float32 ULP-expected). Part of the f64-accumulate/product-then-cast bug class (cf. trace, vdot, prod-axis).
close_reason: Fixed in 8eb265aa. Integer cross fast path (wrapping i64/u64 + native ArrayStorage); 0 divergences vs numpy across 800 cases incl mixed dtype + overflow; unit test added; lib green 2191.

title: [PORT-EPIC] numpy.polynomial 100% parity to fnp_python.polynomial
description:
Reach 100% API parity between fnp_python and numpy.polynomial.

Current state (2026-04-24):
- 59 flat-namespace polynomial passthrough wrappers (chebadd, hermadd,
  lagadd, legadd, polyadd, and siblings for sub/mul/val/roots/fromroots/
  der/int/line/mulx/trim/pow/div across 5 bases).
- 0 class exposures (Polynomial, Chebyshev, Legendre, Hermite, HermiteE,
  Laguerre) — all gate-checked import errors.
- 0 HermiteE (herme*) functions — complete gap. numpy.polynomial.hermite_e
  exports ~13 herme* spellings missing from fnp_python.
- No nested submodule structure — users can't do
  `fnp_python.polynomial.chebyshev.chebadd`; must use flat
  fnp_python.chebadd. Violates numpy's public import path.

Sub-beads:
1. HermiteE (herme*) passthrough wrappers + parity oracle.
2. Polynomial class re-exports (6 classes lazy-loaded from numpy).
3. Nested submodule structure fnp_python.polynomial.{polynomial,
   chebyshev, hermite, hermite_e, laguerre, legendre} exposing the
   existing flat-namespace functions under canonical numpy paths.

Plus: polynomial compliance matrix generator (scripts/fnp_polynomial_
compliance_matrix.py) that reports coverage vs numpy.polynomial.*
public API with MUST/SHOULD/MAY classification.

title: PROFILE: baseline benchmarks captured + geterr inline
description:
## Summary
Established baseline performance metrics for core operations and added profiling infrastructure.

## Changes
1. Added `[profile.release-perf]` to workspace Cargo.toml for profiling with debug symbols
2. Added `#[inline]` to `geterr()` - called at start of every fast path to check error state

## Baseline Benchmarks (ts2 remote)
| Operation | Time | Elements | ns/elem |
|-----------|------|----------|---------|
| ufunc_add_broadcast 1024x1024_by_1024 | 14.7-15.2ms | 1M | ~15 |
| reduce_sum_axis1_1024x1024 | 666-674µs | 1M | ~0.67 |
| matmul_256x256_by_256x256 | 2.58-2.60ms | 16M ops | - |
| sort_quicksort_1m | 4.27-4.47ms | 1M | ~4.3 |
| fft_65536 | 2.47-2.49ms | 64K | ~38 |
| astype_f64_to_i32 1024x1024 | 750-794µs | 1M | ~0.77 |
| reshape_1024x1024_to_2048x512 | 159-165µs | - | - |
| io_npy_save_load_512x512_f64 | 117-122µs | 256K | ~0.46 |

## Analysis
- Hot paths already have dedicated fast paths (same-shape, tail-contiguous broadcast)
- Fast paths skip error checking when `geterr().is_all_ignore()` is true
- `is_all_ignore` already had `#[inline]` hint, `geterr` was missing it
- Broadcast ops are memory-bound (~15ns/elem includes allocation)
- Reduction is extremely fast at <1ns/elem (likely cache-friendly)

## Next Steps
- Monitor for regressions using these baselines
- Profile with samply/flamegraph to identify deeper hotspots if needed
close_reason: done

title: [perf][no-gaps] Buffer-protocol input extraction — extract_numeric_array/extract_precise_numeric_array tolist() round-trip makes native unary path 100x slower than numpy (sqrt 1M 100ms vs 1ms)
description: Python-surface bench (vmi, fresh .so @ fa3704db): fnp.sqrt(1M f64)=100.8ms vs np.sqrt=1.03ms (98x); fnp.cumsum 35x; fnp.outer 64x; fnp.unique 13x; fnp.exp=1.01x (exp passes through to numpy, sqrt uses native path). Root cause: extract_precise_numeric_array (lib.rs:4096) and extract_numeric_array (lib.rs:4056) read input via flat.astype(t).tolist().extract::<Vec<T>>() — per-element PyList boxing/unboxing (~100ns/elem). Output side already buffer-optimized (numpy_array_from_slice, fa3704db). Lever: symmetric PyBuffer<T>::to_vec memcpy read for i8/16/32/64,u8/16/32/64,f32/f64 (bool/f16 keep tolist — no buffer Element). Bit-exact (same f64 values copied as raw bytes). Acceptance: golden sha256 unchanged, Score>=2.0.

title: Centralize QR iteration limit coefficients and document rationale
description:
## Problem

Two different iteration limit coefficients exist for QR iteration:
- SVD bidiagonal QR: `100 * n * n` (used in svd_nxn)
- Symmetric eigenvalue QR: `60 * n * n` (used in tridiag_eig_qr AND hessenberg QR for general eigenvalues)

Named constants were already created in the audit:
- `SVD_QR_ITERATION_COEFF = 100`
- `EIGEN_QR_ITERATION_COEFF = 60`

But the actual loop code still uses the literal expressions `100 * n * n` and `60 * n * n` rather than the named constants. The constants exist but aren't wired in yet.

## Why Different Coefficients?

SVD uses a larger coefficient (100) because bidiagonal QR can require more iterations for convergence than symmetric tridiagonal QR (60). LAPACK uses similar ratios. The 60 coefficient for eigenvalue QR appears in both the symmetric (tridiag_eig_qr) and non-symmetric (Hessenberg QR) paths, which is correct — both use implicit shifted QR on similar matrix structures.

## Proposed Fix

1. Find all `100 * n * n` occurrences in SVD code → replace with `SVD_QR_ITERATION_COEFF * n * n`
2. Find all `60 * n * n` occurrences in eigenvalue code → replace with `EIGEN_QR_ITERATION_COEFF * n * n`
3. Add doc comments explaining why the coefficients differ
4. Verify no other hard-coded iteration limits exist in QR loops

## Validation

- All linalg tests must pass
- All conformance tests must pass
- The actual iteration counts should be unchanged (this is a naming refactor, not a behavioral change)

## Files to Modify

- `crates/fnp-linalg/src/lib.rs` — SVD bidiagonal QR loop, tridiag_eig_qr loop, Hessenberg QR loop
close_reason: Wired SVD_QR_ITERATION_COEFF (100) and EIGEN_QR_ITERATION_COEFF (60) into all 3 QR iteration loops. Constants documented at top of file.

title: [BUG] check_compliance_matrices.sh aborts on first generator failure
description:
scripts/check_compliance_matrices.sh (commit 01e8f1f) iterates the
three generators in a for-loop with `set -e`. If generator N fails
its own gate (e.g. MUST coverage < 95%), the loop aborts and the
remaining generators never run. Result: partial visibility into
matrix regressions when multiple subtrees drift in the same PR.

Self-review on commit 01e8f1f, found 2026-04-25.

Fix: collect generator exit codes + any drift in a flag variable,
keep running, then surface a combined failure at the end. Engineers
investigating a regression see the full surface picture in one run.

Acceptance:
- All 3 generators run regardless of intermediate exit codes.
- Final exit reflects ANY failure (gate or drift).
- The diff dump still shows per-output drift so the engineer can
  attribute regressions correctly.

title: Fast fft_pow2 kernel (split-radix/SIMD) + transpose-based N-D FFT — unblocks 2-D FFT-conv + speeds all FFT ops
description:
fnp's radix-2 fft_pow2 (the core behind fft/ifft/rfft/fft2/fftn/Bluestein + the 1-D FFT convolution 8fd90e76) is a SLOW SCALAR kernel: per-butterfly twiddle recurrence (serial dep, no vectorize) + fft_mul branch per multiply. It is the bottleneck behind two REJECTED FFT levers this session:

1. TWIDDLE TABLE (reverted): precompute tw[j]=exp(i·TAU·j/n), index tw[k·(n/len)] per pass = 0.52x (2x SLOWER) at 2^20. The table (n/2 = 8MB) exceeds cache and tw[k·step] is a STRIDED cache-missing gather; the recurrence keeps w in registers (cache-optimal). So a naive table is WRONG.

2. 2-D FFT convolve2d (reverted): fft_linear_convolve_2d via fftn_along_axis row+col passes = 0.46x (2x SLOWER) at [256,256]*[64,64] vs the parallel-direct gather. The COLUMN pass (axis 0) is STRIDED (fftn_along_axis gathers/scatters each column per line) + slow fft_pow2. 1-D FFT-conv WORKS (4.6x) because it is contiguous; 2-D loses to strided columns.

THE REAL LEVERS (advanced-math / alien-artifact zone, target ~2-3x on fft_pow2 → unblocks 2-D FFT-conv + speeds every FFT op):
- SPLIT-RADIX or RADIX-4 butterflies: ~25-33% fewer real multiplies than radix-2, fewer passes. Keeps the cache-friendly recurrence. Intricate bit-correct impl with inf/NaN (fft_mul) handling.
- SIMD butterflies (portable_simd): vectorize the butterfly inner loop. The twiddle recurrence blocks it; reorganize as `for k {w=twiddle(k); for block {butterfly}}` so the inner block loop has CONSTANT twiddle and vectorizes (helps outer passes), or use a per-pass CONTIGUOUS small twiddle table (len/2 entries, cache-fits) indexed contiguously.
- TRANSPOSE-BASED N-D FFT: replace fftn_along_axis's strided column gather with an explicit transpose between passes so every 1-D FFT is on CONTIGUOUS data. This is what makes 2-D FFT-conv viable (re-enable fft_linear_convolve_2d, gated, after this lands).
- FINITE FAST PATH: if all data finite (one O(n) scan), use plain `*` instead of fft_mul in the butterfly (bit-identical for finite data) to drop 4 branches/butterfly.

PARITY: all fft tests are TOLERANCE-based (1e-9..1e-15, no golden), so a kernel swap is permitted as long as it stays within tolerance AND is actually faster (the twiddle table was within tolerance but slower).

PROOF: existing fft tolerance suite (fft_ifft_roundtrip, fft_non_power_of_two/Bluestein, fft_inf_propagation_matches_numpy, fftn cross-checks, convolve_fft_matches_direct) + a direct fft_pow2 A/B bench (2^20-point, both with shared bit-reversal). Needs a focused multi-hour profiled session, not a rushed hour.
notes: Closeout (BlackThrush, 2026-06-08): SIMD radix-4 FFT work was already landed and recorded in the bead notes. Remaining candidate tested in this handoff was re-enabling fft_linear_convolve_2d for finite f64 2-D convolve2d after radix-4/transpose work. A crate-scoped RCH invocation fell back local because active_project_exclusion had two same-project RCH jobs running; the advisory Criterion row still showed the candidate regressed the profile-backed target: old parallel-direct reference input256_kernel64 10.224/10.657/10.995 ms, FFT candidate input256_kernel64 18.640/19.230/19.877 ms. Ordering/tie/RNG surfaces unchanged in the candidate design and tolerance golden scaffolding was drafted, but performance failed Score>=2.0, so all uncommitted source/bench/test hunks were removed. No tracked source diff remains. Next route: do not repeat naive full-frame 2-D FFT convolution; use an algorithmically different primitive such as overlap-add/overlap-save tiled convolution or separable/kernel-structure detection with a fresh RCH baseline and proof.
close_reason: Closed current in-flight FFT bead: shipped radix-4 FFT work already recorded; remaining 2-D fft_linear_convolve_2d re-enable was rejected after advisory RCH-local fallback Criterion showed regression vs old parallel-direct path (10.657 ms direct vs 19.230 ms FFT candidate). Source/bench/test hunks removed; no tracked source diff remains. Route deeper to overlap-add/overlap-save tiled convolution or other algorithmically different primitive.

description: Systematic follow-up to the einsum (4beba578, 3.8x) + histogram2d (5922695a, 5.3x) routing wins. 134 core_numpy_passthrough sites in fnp-python; many delegate to numpy where numpy is SLOW (serial loop, not BLAS) and we have a fast parallel kernel. RECIPE (proven): grep passthrough -> bench numpy speed in .probe -> route common case (extract_precise_numeric_array + sidecar/complex/kwargs gate + UFuncArray kernel + build_numpy_array_from_ufunc + numpy fallback) -> verify parity via cargo test -p fnp-python --test conformance_<op> LOCALLY (host has numpy 2.4.3; rch doesn't) -> bench release .so locally. NEXT: (1) histogramdd (N-D, same family, numpy serial); (2) cumulative_sum/cumulative_prod on large AXIS = parallel-across-lanes, EXACT parity (each lane sequential); (3) sort/argsort on multi-lane 2-D = parallel-across-rows, exact parity; (4) correlate. SKIP elementwise (numpy SIMD) + matmul/dot (OpenBLAS large-n). Parity keys: integer counts => array_equal-safe parallel; edges/float => allclose tolerance. Each is a clean Score>=2.0 user-facing win that also reduces numpy delegation per no-gaps.
notes: PROGRESS: np.histogramdd DONE (dcea3c9d, 1.9-3.6x: d=2 3.64x, d=3 1.91x, parity=True, 17/17 conformance). Joins einsum (3.8x) + histogram2d (5.3x). REMAINING campaign ops (numpy-slow + kernel exists + parity-safe): cumulative_sum/prod on large AXIS (parallel-across-lanes, exact parity), sort/argsort multi-lane 2D, correlate. NOTE IndigoFrog now also routing np.matmul (size-gated 320-896 window, sidesteps the +fma block 3q3pe).

title: [perf][no-gaps] SIMD polynomial native transcendentals (sin/log/sqrt/tanh) — target 2-4x per-core, compute-bound
description:
CONTEXT (2026-06-09, cc analysis after the parallelize-a-serial-op well ran dry):
The recent perf wins all had real per-element COMPUTE (binary search, division, decode) or a complexity-class reduction (pairwise einsum O(n^4)->O(n^3)). Three levers attempted-and-REJECTED this turn all hit the MEMORY-BANDWIDTH wall (sub-2.0):
 - bincount privatized-fold: 0.5-0.6x (cheap cast+increment, serial scatter already at bandwidth floor; per-thread alloc+merge dominates).
 - set-op (intersect/union/setdiff/setxor) parallel sort: ~1.3x (dominated by linear clone/dedup/merge/f64-convert, not the sort).
 - einsum single-operand reduction routing to reduce_sum_axes: ~1.5-1.8x, 'ijk->i' possibly regresses (both general loop and reduce read the full input once = memory-bound).

NEXT PRIMITIVE (compute-bound, real gap vs numpy's SIMD ufuncs):
fnp's NATIVE unary transcendentals (UnaryOp::Sin/Log/Sqrt/Tanh in native_unary_promoting_or_passthrough) call scalar Rust libm per element + rayon across cores. numpy uses per-core SIMD polynomial ufuncs. For transcendentals the per-element compute (tens of cycles) dominates over memory, so a portable-SIMD (std::simd f64x4/f64x8) polynomial approximation with range reduction is a genuine 2-4x PER CORE win and closes a real gap. NOTE: np.exp is a pure numpy passthrough (core_numpy_passthrough) — NO gap there; only the native ops qualify.

BLOCKER TO RESOLVE FIRST: determine whether native transcendental parity is BIT-EXACT (tobytes/array_equal vs numpy) or TOLERANCE (allclose). A SIMD poly approx is ~1-2 ULP, so:
 - if tolerance-based -> implement SIMD poly (Cephes/Sleef-style minimax coeffs), target 2-4x, prove allclose + golden sha256 of fnp's own bits.
 - if bit-exact vs numpy/libm -> a poly approx WILL break parity; would need to match numpy's exact SIMD algorithm (hard) -> likely a no-ship, document and move to the next primitive.

OTHER BIG PRIMITIVES ON THE SHELF (for reference): SIMD complex FFT butterfly across blocks (FFT is tolerance-based but fft_pow2 radix-4 is already register-blocked + finite-fast-path = near-optimal per prior analysis); Strassen GEMM (shelved, needs an architectural tolerance-relaxation decision per bead ix117); randomized SVD (fnp-linalg, contended by the SVD agent).
close_reason: NO-SHIP: investigated with 2 de-risking experiments. (1) Branchless autovec ln is accurate (1.06e-13 rel err) but 0.53x SPEED vs libm — i32->f64 exponent convert + 4 edge selects block clean 4-wide avx2-no-fma autovec, and libm ln is already ~10-15cyc. (2) NO GAP vs numpy: fnp transcendentals already rayon-parallel across ~10 cores while numpy SIMD ufuncs are single-threaded, so fnp already comparable-or-faster. (3) Hand-intrinsic wide SIMD needs std::arch=unsafe, blocked by forbid(unsafe_code). Also refuted: naive LSD radix sort 0.35-0.64x vs sort_unstable (scatter cache-thrash; pdqsort excellent). Next genuine primitives all need architectural decisions: relax forbid-unsafe for vetted SIMD module, cache-aware parallel radix, Strassen (shelved ix117), randomized SVD (linalg agent).

Consider: (a) adding Rust-style aliases (int8, uint8 already work), (b) documenting the gotcha, (c) adding i1/u1 for int8/uint8 (already supported). title: feat(fnp-io): extended metamorphic tests for I/O roundtrip invariants
description:
## Summary
Extended metamorphic test suite for fnp-io with 13 new tests covering
additional I/O invariants that must hold regardless of input values.

## New metamorphic relations tested (13 tests)
- **Double roundtrip idempotence**: write->read->write->read = identity
- **Payload size invariant**: len(payload) = product(shape) * item_size
- **Shape interpretation invariance**: same bytes preserve across reshape
- **NPZ all entries recoverable**: all N entries accessible after roundtrip
- **Header parse determinism**: repeated parsing yields identical results
- **Large array roundtrip**: 100K element stress test
- **Integer boundary values**: i8/i16/i32/u8/u16/u32 MIN/MAX roundtrip
- **Float special values**: 0.0, -0.0, INF, -INF, NaN, MAX, MIN roundtrip

## Test count
Before: 18 tests
After: 31 tests (+13 new metamorphic relations)

## Files
- crates/fnp-io/tests/metamorphic_io.rs (extended)

title: [BUG] testing_assert_string_equal lines_keepends incomplete separator set
description:
testing_assert_string_equal native port (commit dc2fcb6) added a free
fn lines_keepends in fnp-python/src/lib.rs that mirrors Python's
str.splitlines(keepends=True) for \n, \r, \r\n only. Python actually
recognizes a wider separator set: \v (\x0b), \f (\x0c), \x1c, \x1d,
\x1e, \x85,  ,  .

Self-review found in last-3-commits sweep on 2026-04-25.

For typical test input (newline-terminated lines) the divergence is
invisible, but a string containing e.g. \v will tokenise differently
than numpy's reference and produce a non-matching diff message,
breaking the exact-format parity guarantee documented in the commit.

Fix: drop lines_keepends and route through Python's stdlib via
`actual.into_pyobject(py)?.call_method1("splitlines", (true,))?` so
the canonical separator set is used. Cleaner, smaller, and correct.

Acceptance:
- lines_keepends helper deleted.
- testing_assert_string_equal calls actual.splitlines(True) /
  desired.splitlines(True) via pyo3.
- Existing testing_assert_string_equal parity test passes.
- Optional: extra parity oracle exercising \v and \r-only inputs to
  prove the broader separator set is honored (gated on
  numpy_available).

close_reason: Completed: fnp-python diagnostic coverage now matches NumPy warning category/order for divide/remainder/mod/fmod zero divisors, empty mean/var, and all-NaN nanmean/nanstd via native fast-path guards and NumPy fallback; PD-2F6L4 removed from active divergence ledger.

title: [perf] fnp-ufunc: parallelize last-axis f64 sort across independent lanes (bit-exact)
description: np.sort(axis=-1) on a 2D+ f64 array sorts each row independently; fnp does it serially. Lanes along the last axis (inner==1) are contiguous and independent. LEVER: rayon par_chunks_mut(axis_len) calling the existing sort_slice_by_kind per lane (bit-exact: same per-lane function, identical output regardless of thread count). Gate on enough lanes/elements. numpy sort is single-threaded -> big win on many-row sorts. Proof: golden bit-identical vs serial + re-bench.
notes: Shipped: f64 sort axis=-1 (inner==1) parallelizes across contiguous lanes via par_chunks_mut + existing sort_slice_by_kind per lane (gate len>=16384, >=2 lanes). Bit-exact (same per-lane fn). 2048x2048: serial 87.2ms -> parallel 26.8ms = 3.26x. Test sort_last_axis_parallel_matches_serial_reference (incl NaN-last). 2092 lib tests pass, clippy clean.

title: perf(fnp-ufunc): parallel axis-map gather for pad edge/wrap/reflect/symmetric
description: The 4 gather-based pad modes (edge/wrap/reflect/symmetric) each ran a fully-serial per-element coordinate-decomposition gather over the full output (ndim divisions/elem + the mode's branchy index logic per element). Added shared pad_gather_by_axis_maps: precompute each mode's per-axis source-index map ONCE (small 1-D tables, branch logic hoisted out of the hot loop), then fill the output row by row — each row decomposes its outer index once (amortized over the contiguous last axis) and gathers the inner run via the reused last-axis map; rows are independent contiguous slices => parallel across rayon. Values + i64/u64 sidecar from same index map. Pure gather => bit-identical. Score 3.99x [4096,4096] / 5.50x [256,256,256] (pad_reflect; same helper serves all 4 modes). Proof pad_gather_modes_match_naive_and_golden (4 modes x to_bits vs naive across 2-D/3-D asymmetric pad incl NaN/inf/-0.0 + i64 sidecar, sha256 11f3f36f).
close_reason: Shipped: parallel axis-map pad gather (edge/wrap/reflect/symmetric), 3.99x/5.50x, bit-exact golden 11f3f36f.

title: [perf][no-gaps][fnp-python] zero-copy parallel-Gram corrcoef (apply the cov d53dee38 treatment)
description: Filed by cc 2026-06-09 after shipping cov zero-copy parallel-Gram (d53dee38, 2.6x). corrcoef is still ~8x slower than numpy (1.9ms vs 0.24ms @50x2000) — it does NOT route through the new fast cov path (it computes its own native cov + normalization, paying the same cold-mmap alloc chain: extract/center/transpose/full). Fix: route corrcoef's rowvar=True/no-y/f64 case through try_zerocopy_cov_rowvar_f64 (or a shared gram helper), then normalize: c[i][j] / sqrt(c[i][i]*c[j][j]), clip to [-1,1] like numpy. Parity tolerance (np.allclose). Target ~2.5x like cov. Residual vs numpy is the AVX2-no-FMA dot ceiling (8vdtg). Also note a SEPARATE pre-existing bug found: np.cov of a (1,N) array with rowvar=False returns (N,N) but fnp returns a 0-d scalar (native_cov_unweighted's '!rowvar && shape[0]!=1' guard mishandles the single-row case) — file/fix separately.
close_reason: Shipped in a6858af3: zero-copy parallel-Gram corrcoef rowvar=True/no-y contiguous f64 fast path. Clean source commit reports corrcoef(50,2000) 1.9 ms -> 0.85 ms (2.2x) with parity across shapes, scalar 1-D, zero-variance/rowvar=False/y defer paths. Post-commit statistics benchmark row: fnp_corrcoef_rowvar_f64_200x500 1.1526/1.1691/1.1876 ms vs numpy 595.53/610.19/634.82 us; fnp_cov_rowvar_f64_200x500 1.1369/1.1569/1.1818 ms. Residual routed to deeper dot/FMA work, not accepted as a ceiling.

title: Expand public API golden test with statistical functions
description:
## Finding

public_api_golden.rs was missing coverage for statistical analysis, nan-aware reductions, and histogram/binning functions.

## New golden test coverage

### Statistical analysis
- diff (n=1, n=2)
- ptp (peak-to-peak range)
- cov (covariance matrix)
- corrcoef (correlation coefficients)

### NaN-aware reductions
- nansum (all, axis)
- nanmean (all, axis)

### Histogram and binning
- histogram (uniform bins, custom edges)
- bincount
- digitize (default, right)

## Files

- fnp-ufunc/tests/public_api_golden.rs (+47 lines)
- fnp-ufunc/tests/golden/public_api.golden (+46 lines)
close_reason: Complete: 14 new golden snapshots for statistical/binning functions

README doesn't surface this anywhere. Add a one-line callout in the Parity Status table or API Surface to flag the project tracks NumPy 2.x.

title: [idea-wizard] Diagnostic parity and upstream drift wave
description:
Context:
- The prior idea-wizard many-core conformance/performance wave is closed, including sharded fnp-python conformance, API coverage gates, benchmark telemetry, RaptorQ stress/parallelism, and evidence-link validation.
- Current live work is concentrated on fnp-python I/O wrappers and a recfunctions structured-dtype bug, so this wave avoids duplicating that active implementation surface.
- FrankenNumPy's stated goal is drop-in NumPy compatibility. Value/shape parity is well represented, but exception classes, warning behavior, version-specific upstream drift, and intentional divergence documentation are still comparatively informal.

Phase 2 summary: generated 30 candidate improvements and winnowed to the best five: (1) exception/warning parity harness, (2) structured dtype/record-array oracle corpus, (3) cross-version NumPy drift matrix, (4) view/stride mutation error parity, and (5) agent-readable validation recipes. Phase 3 next-best ideas included IO parser diagnostic goldens, oracle environment capsules, masked/object dtype diagnostic matrices, adversarial NPY/NPZ fuzz interop, and generated divergence docs.

Goal:
- Make diagnostic behavior a first-class compatibility contract: capture NumPy exception class/warning/category/message-shape behavior, gate high-risk surfaces, track upstream-version drift, and document intentional divergences so future agents do not silently normalize away observable NumPy behavior.

Non-negotiables:
- Preserve strict NumPy observable behavior unless a divergence is explicitly documented and gated.
- Keep heavy validation behind rch.
- Add deterministic JSON/JSONL reports suitable for bv/br triage and agent handoff.
- Do not duplicate the closed many-core/performance wave or the active fnp-python I/O wrapper beads.

Done when:
- Child beads form an acyclic graph, each with clear acceptance criteria and validation commands.
- New gates distinguish code failure from oracle/environment drift.
- Any discovered behavior gaps are filed as follow-up beads instead of hidden in prose.
close_reason: Completed diagnostic parity/upstream drift wave: all child beads are closed and dependency graph has no cycles.

title: Build exception and warning oracle capture schema
description:
Context:
- Many conformance tests compare returned values, shapes, or dtypes, but drop-in compatibility also depends on exception class, warning category, warning count/order, and stable message fragments.
- This is the foundation bead for the diagnostic parity wave; downstream surface-specific gates should reuse its schema instead of each inventing local formats.

Scope:
- Add a deterministic fnp-conformance schema for diagnostic cases: input id, API/surface, strict/hardened mode if applicable, expected NumPy exception class or warning category, message fragment policy, version guards, and whether a divergence is intentional.
- Add a small oracle runner that executes Python snippets under NumPy and emits JSON/JSONL observations for success, exception, and warning paths.
- Include environment metadata: FNP_ORACLE_PYTHON, numpy.__version__, platform, and interpreter path, so agent handoffs can separate oracle drift from code regressions.

Acceptance criteria:
- Unit tests cover success, exception, single-warning, multi-warning, and version-guarded diagnostic cases.
- The report is deterministic and stable under repeated runs.
- Missing/unknown diagnostic expectations fail closed unless the case is explicitly marked exploratory.
- Validation commands include: rch exec -- cargo test -p fnp-conformance diagnostic_oracle; rch exec -- cargo clippy -p fnp-conformance --all-targets -- -D warnings; cargo fmt --check for touched files or a documented unrelated fmt blocker.
close_reason: Completed: added diagnostic oracle schema, Python-backed warning/exception capture runner, smoke CLI, and validation tests.

title: Gate dtype and shape diagnostic parity
description:
Context:
- Dtype promotion/casting and shape/stride legality are crown-jewel compatibility surfaces. A wrong exception class or warning path can break downstream NumPy callers even when successful cases match.
- This task should build on the diagnostic oracle schema from franken_numpy-33vtd.1.

Scope:
- Add diagnostic conformance cases for fnp-dtype and fnp-ndarray high-risk paths: invalid casts, unsafe casts, reshape -1 errors, broadcast failures, overflowed element counts, invalid strides/views, and unsupported dtype descriptors.
- Compare NumPy exception class, warning category, and stable message fragments where practical. Use version guards for known NumPy 1.x/2.x differences.
- Emit a report section that future agents can map directly to the dtype/SCE contract surface.

Acceptance criteria:
- At least 20 diagnostic cases across dtype and ndarray surfaces.
- Tests include empty shape, overflow, multi -1 reshape, incompatible broadcast, and invalid cast policy examples.
- Failures distinguish target mismatch from oracle execution failure.
- Validation commands include: rch exec -- cargo test -p fnp-conformance dtype_shape_diagnostics; rch exec -- cargo test -p fnp-dtype; rch exec -- cargo test -p fnp-ndarray; rch exec -- cargo clippy -p fnp-conformance -p fnp-dtype -p fnp-ndarray --all-targets -- -D warnings.
close_reason: Completed: added 22 NumPy-backed dtype and ndarray diagnostic cases with surface summary and oracle evaluation tests.

title: Gate IO parser and NPZ diagnostic parity
description:
Context:
- fnp-io is a security and compatibility boundary. Existing tests cover many successful NPY/NPZ/text paths, but malformed input behavior needs diagnostic parity so strict mode does not mask user-visible NumPy behavior.
- This extends the new diagnostic schema into parser and archive surfaces.

Scope:
- Add diagnostic cases for malformed NPY headers, unsupported object/pickle settings, truncated payloads, invalid descriptors, EOCD/ZIP central-directory defects, duplicate/invalid NPZ member names, and text parsing failures for loadtxt/genfromtxt/fromfile.
- Compare exception class and stable message fragments against NumPy where a public NumPy API has a matching path; otherwise classify the case as hardened-only with explicit rationale.
- Emit JSONL suitable for later fuzz/minimization work.

Acceptance criteria:
- At least 20 IO diagnostic cases, with both NPY and NPZ represented.
- Include at least one compressed NPZ and one uncompressed NPZ malformed-path case.
- Strict-vs-hardened divergence is explicit; no unknown diagnostic path passes silently.
- Validation commands include: rch exec -- cargo test -p fnp-io npy_npz_diagnostic; rch exec -- cargo test -p fnp-conformance io_diagnostics; rch exec -- cargo clippy -p fnp-io -p fnp-conformance --all-targets -- -D warnings.
close_reason: Completed: added IO/NPZ diagnostic oracle runner, 22 NumPy diagnostic cases, JSON/JSONL output, and fnp-io malformed-boundary reason-code tests.

title: Add fnp-python warnings and exceptions conformance shard
description:
Context:
- fnp-python is the user-facing drop-in surface. Many wrappers delegate to NumPy for unsupported paths, but wrappers still need to preserve exception class, warning category, and argument validation order.
- This task should wait for active fnp-python I/O and recfunctions work to settle before editing shared files.

Scope:
- Add a new shard or extend the fnp-python conformance shard manifest with warning/exception-focused cases.
- Cover representative wrappers from array creation, dtype utilities, IO, recfunctions, masked/object helpers, and deprecation paths such as aliases that should warn.
- Use the diagnostic oracle schema from franken_numpy-33vtd.1, and mark intentional divergences explicitly instead of hiding them in test code.

Acceptance criteria:
- At least 30 fnp-python diagnostic cases across 5+ API families.
- Tests verify warning category/count/order and exception class for selected cases.
- The shard has a deterministic rch runner entry and JSON/JSONL output.
- Validation commands include: rch exec -- cargo test -p fnp-python --test <diagnostic shard> -- --nocapture; rch exec -- cargo run -p fnp-conformance --bin run_fnp_python_conformance_shards -- --list-shards; rch exec -- cargo clippy -p fnp-python --tests -- -D warnings.
close_reason: Completed: added fnp-python diagnostic conformance shard covering warnings and exception classes across API families; filed follow-up beads for discovered diagnostic gaps.

title: Add cross-version NumPy oracle drift matrix
description:
Context:
- Prior work added NumPy version detection for specific tests, but the project still lacks a first-class matrix that records how diagnostic/value expectations differ across supported NumPy versions.
- Without this, agents can mistake upstream NumPy changes for FrankenNumPy regressions or accidentally bake one local oracle version into long-lived fixtures.

Scope:
- Add a conformance runner that can execute a selected fixture subset against multiple configured NumPy interpreters: repo default, optional NumPy 1.x, optional NumPy 2.x, and optional nightly/dev when available.
- Emit a drift matrix showing stable expectations, version-gated differences, new upstream warnings/errors, and unsupported/missing oracle environments.
- The runner must degrade clearly when an interpreter is unavailable: environment missing is not a code failure, but a missing required matrix lane should fail if the gate is configured as required.

Acceptance criteria:
- Unit tests cover matrix merge logic, missing interpreter handling, stable case, and version-divergent case.
- A smoke e2e command runs against the available repo oracle and writes deterministic JSON.
- Existing version-detection tests are integrated or referenced, not duplicated.
- Validation commands include: rch exec -- cargo test -p fnp-conformance oracle_drift_matrix; rch exec -- cargo run -p fnp-conformance --bin <matrix runner> -- --smoke --report-path target/oracle_drift_matrix.json; rch exec -- cargo clippy -p fnp-conformance --all-targets -- -D warnings.
close_reason: Completed in 0c86f14: added oracle drift matrix runner with remote validation and smoke JSON report evidence.

title: Document intentional divergences from diagnostic gates
description:
Context:
- The project currently has scattered comments and README limitations, but no single generated/checked divergence ledger that tells future agents whether a diagnostic mismatch is intentional, temporary parity debt, or an upstream-version split.
- This task should use outputs from the diagnostic oracle and cross-version matrix instead of hand-written speculation.

Scope:
- Add or generate a docs artifact such as docs/DIVERGENCES.md or an artifacts/compatibility divergence report that records intentional diagnostic/value/warning divergences with rationale, affected NumPy versions, strict/hardened behavior, and follow-up bead IDs.
- Add a checker that fails when a diagnostic gate marks a divergence as intentional but the divergence ledger lacks an entry.
- Link the report from the relevant README/FEATURE_PARITY location only if that avoids stale manual claims.

Acceptance criteria:
- At least one synthetic/unit fixture proves missing-ledger entries fail closed.
- The report distinguishes intentional divergence from unimplemented parity debt.
- The checker emits deterministic JSON or Markdown and is usable by future agents without reading old conversations.
- Validation commands include: rch exec -- cargo test -p fnp-conformance divergence_ledger; rch exec -- cargo run -p fnp-conformance --bin <divergence checker> -- --fail-on-missing; ubs on touched docs/code files.
close_reason: Completed: divergence ledger docs, parser/checker, fail-closed missing-ledger diagnostics, and run_divergence_ledger validation landed in d5bf59d; closeout was waiting on tracker reservation.

title: Expand structured dtype and recfunctions oracle corpus
description:
Context:
- The active franken_numpy-86iws bug shows that structured dtype and recfunctions behavior is fragile: flatten=False promotion/wrapping semantics differ for single-field inputs.
- This task should start after that bug is resolved, then broaden coverage so the same class of regression does not recur.

Scope:
- Add a structured dtype and recfunctions oracle corpus covering merge_arrays, unstructured_to_structured, repack_fields, rename/drop/append fields, nested fields, duplicate names, object fields, byte order, and empty records.
- Include diagnostic expectations for invalid field layouts and duplicate-name behavior using the diagnostic schema when available.
- Produce a report that maps each corpus case to the fnp-python/fnp-dtype/fnp-io surface it protects.

Acceptance criteria:
- At least 25 structured dtype/recfunctions cases with live NumPy oracle comparison.
- Includes single-field, multi-field, nested-field, duplicate-name, and flatten=True/False scenarios.
- The active franken_numpy-86iws regression is represented as a permanent conformance case after it is fixed.
- Validation commands include: rch exec -- cargo test -p fnp-python --test conformance_dtype_utils; rch exec -- cargo test -p fnp-conformance structured_dtype_corpus; rch exec -- cargo clippy -p fnp-python -p fnp-conformance --tests -- -D warnings.
close_reason: Completed: added 32-case live NumPy structured dtype/recfunctions oracle corpus in fnp-python conformance tests.

title: Add agent-readable validation recipe selector
description:
Context:
- Agents repeatedly need to choose the smallest trustworthy rch validation set for a changed surface. The closed many-core wave created shards and coverage reports, but there is still no single command that maps changed files or bead labels to the recommended validation recipe.
- This should be a thin selector over existing manifests, not a new test framework.

Scope:
- Add a deterministic recipe selector that accepts changed paths, bead labels, or crate names and emits the recommended rch commands, expected reports, and known environment prerequisites.
- Integrate existing fnp-python shard manifest, API coverage gate, performance/packet gates, and the new diagnostic parity reports when available.
- Emit JSON suitable for agent mail handoffs and markdown suitable for final session summaries.

Acceptance criteria:
- Unit tests cover path-to-crate mapping, fnp-python conformance shard selection, fnp-io parser selection, docs-only changes, and unknown paths.
- The selector never claims workspace-wide proof is required when a narrower scoped proof is sufficient, and never suppresses required gates for high-risk surfaces.
- Validation commands include: rch exec -- cargo test -p fnp-conformance validation_recipe_selector; rch exec -- cargo run -p fnp-conformance --bin <recipe selector> -- --changed crates/fnp-io/src/lib.rs --format json; ubs on touched files.
close_reason: Completed: added self-contained validation recipe selector with JSON/Markdown output and focused tests

title: [perf] fnp-ufunc::fft radix-2 first butterfly uses generic twiddle path
description: Continuation pass-3 profile on rch worker vmi1149989 after 30a6f57c: fft_65536 is the second-largest remaining core_ops hotspot at center 4.5750 ms, CI [4.2350 ms, 4.9701 ms], after sort_quicksort_1m 6.1723 ms. Sort's next obvious zero-based pre-scan was rejected under proof rules, so this bead targets the next high-score profiler-evident hotspot. Source trace: fft_pow2 starts at len=2 even though w=(1,0) for every butterfly in that pass; the generic loop still calls fft_mul four times per pair and updates an unused twiddle recurrence. One-lever candidate: handle len=2 pairs directly before the generic len>=4 loop, preserving Inf/NaN zero-twiddle semantics because w=(1,0) makes the current generic equations identical to direct pair sum/difference. Required proof: rch before/after fft_65536, perf_fft_golden FNV/current-vs-reference equality, focused fft tests, public API golden sha256, check/clippy/fmt, and isomorphism note for ordering/floating-point/RNG.
close_reason: Measured no-ship: FFT first-butterfly specialization did not confirm a Criterion win. Baseline fft_65536 4.5750 ms CI [4.2350, 4.9701] on vmi1149989; post-close independent after-run 5.1367 ms CI [4.7235, 5.7594] on vmi1227854. Source reverted; no optimization kept.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Reopened: Independent post-close rch Criterion run regressed fft_65536 to 5.1367 ms center, CI [4.7235, 5.7594] on vmi1227854; reverting unproven optimization under perf campaign keep rules.

title: Return NaN matrices for non-finite expm/sqrtm inputs
description:
`expm_nxn` and `sqrtm_nxn` currently fail closed on any non-finite input, but SciPy returns all-NaN matrices for these cases. `logm_nxn` still raises, so only expm/sqrtm should change.

Evidence:
- `scipy.linalg.expm([[inf,1],[2,3]]) -> [[nan,nan],[nan,nan]]`
- `scipy.linalg.sqrtm([[nan,1],[2,3]]) -> [[nan,nan],[nan,nan]]`

Goal: preserve current `logm_nxn` error behavior, but make `expm_nxn` and `sqrtm_nxn` return all-NaN outputs on non-finite input with regression tests.
close_reason: Completed

title: binary ufuncs lose dtype for narrow/int inputs: fmax fmin fmod mod true_divide hypot arctan2 logaddexp(2) copysign heaviside nextafter
description: F64-only kernels: these binary ufuncs return float64 regardless of input dtype, diverging from NumPy. fmax/fmin/fmod/mod must preserve input dtype (int32->int32, float32->float32); true_divide/hypot/arctan2/logaddexp/logaddexp2/copysign/heaviside/nextafter are float-output and must derive the narrow float (int16->float32, uint8->float16, float32->float32). Native f64 path is correct only when both inputs are float64. Fix: gate each wrapper to native path only when both operands are numpy_dtype_is_f64, else passthrough to the real numpy ufunc. Sibling add/sub/mul/maximum/minimum/floor_divide/remainder/divide already correct (dtype-aware or numpy-registered). Found via differential dtype probe vs numpy 2.4.3 (46 dtype diffs). Related: xstsw (divmod, same class).
close_reason: Fixed: gated 11 f64-only binary ufunc wrappers (fmax/fmin/fmod/mod/true_divide/hypot/arctan2/logaddexp/logaddexp2/copysign/heaviside/nextafter) to run the native f64 kernel only when both operands are float64 (numpy_dtype_is_f64); narrow floats and integers now passthrough to numpy, preserving NumPy's result dtype (int32->int32 for fmax/fmin/fmod/mod; int16->float32/uint8->float16 for the float-output ufuncs). Verified 224/224 dtype+value cases vs numpy 2.4.3 + new test binary_ufuncs_preserve_input_dtype_like_numpy; probe3 0 mismatches.

title: numpy.tril_indices_from python wrapper parity
description: Add fnp_python.tril_indices_from passthrough wrapper around numpy.tril_indices_from(arr, k=0). Returns indices for the lower triangle of arr. Include oracle test covering: square matrix k=0, k=1, k=-1, rectangular (non-square raises ValueError matching numpy), 1-D input raising ValueError, and verifying the indices select only lower-triangular positions.

title: fnp-dtype: can_cast(complex128, f64, same_kind) diverges from numpy
description: Conformance test discovered: fnp_dtype::can_cast(Complex128, F64, "same_kind") returns false but numpy.can_cast(np.complex128, np.float64, casting='same_kind') returns True. NumPy considers complex→float same_kind because the float component is representable. fnp-dtype rejects it. Oracle: python3 -c "import numpy as np; print(np.can_cast(np.complex128, np.float64, casting='same_kind'))" → True

title: [no-gaps][POLICY][P1] Enable +fma (with tolerance-parity for non-reproducible float ops) to unlock BLAS-class GEMM speed
description: ROOT-CAUSE of the matmul/dot-vs-OpenBLAS gap (and a ceiling on ALL float-op perf vs numpy). numpy's matmul/exp/etc. use FMA+AVX (OpenBLAS/SVML). Our kernels CANNOT use hardware FMA because: (1) .cargo/config enables +avx2 but NOT +fma — enabling +fma is known to break ~16 conformance tests that assert array_equal (BIT-EXACT) on inherently-non-bit-reproducible float ops (matmul, einsum, transcendentals); (2) #[target_feature(enable=fma)] needs unsafe, forbidden by forbid(unsafe_code) in all crates except fnp-python. So our GEMM stays ~4-5x slower than OpenBLAS at n=512 (FMA ~2x + microkernel/packing ~2x; B-packing alone REGRESSED 2.3x, NC-panel already L2-resident). THE UNLOCK: (a) AUDIT the ~16 float conformance tests using array_equal -> relax to allclose where numpy itself is non-reproducible (matmul/einsum/dot/exp/log/trig/...); numpy's BLAS results already vary by thread count, so bit-exact-vs-numpy is the WRONG contract for these. (b) Enable +fma in .cargo/config (or x86-64-v3). (c) Re-audit kernels that NEED bit-exact (integer ops, exact reductions) to ensure they're unaffected or use explicit non-FMA paths. PAYOFF: ~2x across every FMA-able float kernel (GEMM, transcendentals, dot, conv, cov), enabling np.matmul/dot routing to BEAT numpy. RISK: project-wide; needs orchestrator/multi-agent sign-off (BlackThrush/IndigoFrog maintain many goldens). This is the single highest-leverage no-gaps lever — gated on a parity-policy decision, not engineering. Found by cc-numpy 2026-06-04 while closing 7put0 (einsum/histogram2d routing shipped; matmul blocked here).
close_reason: Measured no-ship: env-only +fma no longer reproduces the old fnp-linalg parity blocker (rch ts1 RUSTFLAGS=+avx2,+fma: 270 unit + 37 conformance + 19 golden + 19 metamorphic + 4 solve perf tests passed), but speed did not clear Score>=2.0. rch ts1 core_ops/matmul_256x256_by_256x256 AVX2 baselines 540.33us and 646.60us; +fma 561.41us (noise/no clear win). rch ts1 matrix_power_nxn/size/512 AVX2 5.4689ms; +fma 5.2990ms (1.03x). No .cargo/config change kept. Next primitive should be algorithmic: recursive/blocked safe-Rust GEMM microkernel/packing or communication-avoiding decomposition, not FMA policy alone.

title: [parity] histogram_full uses floor-division binning (FP-edge divergence vs numpy/basic histogram)
description: Reality-check finding. UFuncArray::histogram (basic, bins-only) bins via partition_point over the edge array — the code comment explicitly states this matches numpy and that naive ((v-min)/width) as usize mis-bins values on internal edges under FP rounding (e.g. (1.2-1.0)/0.2=0.9999..->bin0 instead of bin1). BUT UFuncArray::histogram_full (the range/weights/density path) still uses the buggy floor: idx=((v-lo)/width) as usize. So the two histogram implementations can disagree at FP-edge values, and histogram_full diverges from numpy there. Low user-facing impact today because fnp-python np.histogram fast-paths ONLY the simple integer-bins/no-range/no-weights/no-density case (-> basic histogram, correct) and FALLS BACK to real numpy for range/weights/density/edges (lib.rs ~10685-10720). So the bug is reachable only via the direct Rust histogram_full API. FIX: build edges first, bin via edges.partition_point(|e| *e<=v)-1 clamped to [0,bins-1], keeping the range filter (exclude v<lo||v>hi) and weights/density. Validate against numpy.histogram with explicit range + weights at exact internal-edge values. Lock with a test that feeds values exactly on internal edges and asserts basic histogram == histogram_full (no-range) bin-for-bin.
close_reason: Fixed in 8616fd48: histogram_full now bins via edge partition_point matching basic histogram + numpy. Locked by histogram_full_edge_binning_matches_basic_histogram. All existing histogram_full tests + 2117 lib tests pass, clippy clean.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Extended the edge-binning fix to histogram2d + histogramdd in 253d0e20 (both used the same floor-division bug). Whole histogram family now bins via partition_point over edges, consistent + numpy-correct. Committed via selective git-apply--cached patch because BlackThrush was concurrently editing lib.rs (fft_pow2 + a test block) — only my 3 histogram hunks were staged, their uncommitted work left untouched. 35 histogram tests pass.

title: README Quick Example z-score 'broadcast_to(&[5])' is a no-op (array is already shape [5]) — simplify
description: Lines 100-102 of Quick Example: 'data' is UFuncArray::new(vec![5], ...), then z-score uses '.broadcast_to(&[5])'. Since the operand is already shape [5], the broadcast is identity. NumPy users would expect mean/std reductions to return scalars (shape []) which would then auto-broadcast. Either keep the broadcast and explain why (mean is shape [] in NumPy but here it returns a scalar that needs explicit broadcast?), or simplify the example to skip the redundant call.
close_reason: Premise wrong — verified at fnp-ufunc/src/lib.rs:8303: reduce_mean(None, false) returns shape Vec::new() (0-dim scalar), so broadcast_to(&[5]) is NOT a no-op — it's the required step before elementwise subtraction. Substituting bead for a comment explaining this in the example.

title: [perf] Enable +avx2 codegen for 4-wide f64 autovectorization (bit-exact, workspace-wide)
description: SHIPPED. Default build was generic x86-64 (SSE2, 2-wide f64), capping all autovectorized elementwise/AXPY kernels. Added .cargo/config.toml rustflags=-C target-feature=+avx2. PROOF (rch, AMD Threadripper 5975WX, single-thread): serial LU 1024x1024 271.99ms (2.63 GFLOP/s, SSE2) -> 54.05ms (13.24 GFLOP/s, +avx2) = 5.0x, via perf_lu_parallel example. Bit-exact: LU FNV-1a 0xceef03f1375eb26f IDENTICAL across SSE2 and +avx2. Parity verified: fnp-linalg 252 unit + 37 numpy-oracle conformance pass; fnp-ufunc 2087 lib pass. CRITICAL: full x86-64-v3 (which bundles FMA) is NOT bit-exact — LLVM contracts a*d-b*c (det_2x2 etc.) into single-rounding fmsub, regressing 16 linalg conformance tests. We enable +avx2 ONLY (no +fma) to keep IEEE per-op rounding. Score ~5. Foundational for all subsequent SIMD/GEMM/LU work.
notes: Shipped in commit (see below). .cargo/config.toml committed.

title: Emit swarm-ready proof backlog reports from bv and conformance manifests
description:
Context:
- Massive agent swarms need actionable slices with exact files, commands, and proof expectations. bv ranks beads, while conformance manifests know test surfaces; combining those signals can reduce idle time and duplicate validation work.
- This bead is intentionally tooling-oriented and should not block correctness work.

Scope:
- Add a lightweight report generator that reads open Beads, bv robot output, and conformance shard metadata to produce a swarm handoff report.
- For each ready bead, include suggested reservation globs, rch validation commands, related conformance shards, and likely artifact outputs.
- Keep the report deterministic and terminal-friendly, with optional JSON for automation.

Acceptance criteria:
- Tests cover report generation with ready, blocked, and closed sample beads plus conformance shard metadata.
- The generator never runs bare bv; it uses robot-mode output or a provided JSON fixture.
- Validation commands include: rch exec -- cargo test -p fnp-conformance <swarm report tests>; bv --robot-triage; rch exec -- cargo run -p fnp-conformance --bin <new report generator> -- --output target/swarm_handoff.json.
- Closeout confirms the report points at the new idea-wizard graph and does not create or mutate beads itself.
close_reason: Completed: added deterministic swarm handoff report generator over Beads, bv robot triage, and conformance manifest metadata without mutating beads

title: perf(fnp-ufunc): einsum generalized-outer fast path shipped (2.4-4.2x); general-path per-cell alloc remains
description: SHIPPED ea175000. np.einsum('i,j->ij'/outer + 2-operand no-contraction tensor products fell to the general scatter (per-cell label_vals Vec alloc + mod/div decode, ~80x numpy). Added bit-identical fast path out[ia*sizeB+ib]=a[ia]*b[ib], parallel over rows (gate 4096). Same-process A/B vs old-path replica: 1000^2=2.39x, 2000^2=4.17x. title: AUDIT: deadlock-finder static analysis - codebase safe
description:
## Summary
Full deadlock-finder audit of franken_numpy concurrency primitives found NO deadlock hazards.

## Scope
- fnp-ufunc: UFuncArrayView (SharedBuffer, SharedSidecar)
- fnp-python: PY_TEST_MUTEX (test serialization only)
- fnp-conformance: FILE_LOG_MUTEX (log serialization only)

## Findings

### UFuncArrayView Lock Ordering (SAFE)
Two Arc<RwLock<...>> fields: buffer and integer_sidecar

Contract documented at line 2228-2234:
> When acquiring both `buffer` and `integer_sidecar` locks, always acquire
> `integer_sidecar` first, then `buffer`.

Analysis of all lock acquisitions in impl block:
- `itemset()` (line 2437): sidecar.write() FIRST, then buffer.write() SECOND ✓
- `itemset_cow()` (line 2488): buffer.read() → drop → sidecar.read() → drop (sequential, not held simultaneously) ✓
- All other methods acquire only one lock ✓

### No AB-BA Pattern Possible
The only function holding both write locks (`itemset`) follows the documented ordering.
Read-only paths (`itemset_cow`) release each lock before acquiring the next.

## Action Taken
Added concurrent regression test: `shared_view_concurrent_itemset_no_deadlock`
- 4 threads × 25 itemset calls = 100 concurrent writes
- Would deadlock within seconds if ordering violated
- Passes in <1s

## Verdict
✅ CODEBASE SAFE - consistent lock ordering, no AB-BA deadlock possible
