title: [perf][no-gaps][fnp-linalg] sub-cubic Strassen-Winograd GEMM for large n (helps matmul/matrix_power/factorization trailing updates)
description: Filed by cc 2026-06-09 after the LU/cholesky/GEMM SCHEDULING lever family was exhausted (bead xz111 closed no-ship: recursive getrf2 ~= blocked, cholesky nb=128 regression, gemm thin-k gate-relax regression). All those share the cubic packed_gemm (AVX2, NO FMA per bit-parity policy), which is ~2x/flop behind scipy-OpenBLAS getrf/potrf (AVX2+FMA). Two genuine 2.0+ unlocks remain: (A) bead 8vdtg — a vetted std::arch SIMD+FMA microkernel (needs USER sign-off; this is THE blocker for closing the f64 factorization gap at n<=2048, where Strassen does NOT yet pay). (B) THIS bead — Strassen-Winograd recursive GEMM: 7 instead of 8 (n/2)^3 multiplies per level, O(n^2.807). Theoretical (7/8)^levels: 1.30x@2 levels, 1.95x@5 levels (n/32 base). Real win net of the 18 (n/2)^2 add/sub temporaries + less-efficient small base GEMMs => crossover for a clean Score>=2.0 is ~n>=4096 (sub-2.0 at the current 512..2048 bench sizes, so bench MUST extend to 4096/8192). Tolerance-equivalent (matmul/LU conformance is allclose, not bit-exact — Strassen reassociates, slightly worse conditioned but within envelope). Plan: recursive 2x2 block Strassen with a crossover threshold to the existing packed_gemm; preserve NaN/Inf propagation and the all-finite contract; one large scratch arena reused across levels (NOT per-node Vec like the failed recursive LU); prove vs packed_gemm within tolerance + golden sha on a fixed seed; bench mat_mul_flat / matmul / matrix_power at n=2048,4096,8192 via rch same-load A/B; keep only Score>=2.0 else unwind. Wiring it into the factorization trailing updates (lu_decompose_blocked/cholesky_blocked) then propagates the win. Bench infra prepped: det/inv now bench to 1024, cholesky to 768 (commit this session).
notes: BlackThrush pass-3 start (2026-06-09): claimed after .130/.131 no-ships. Focused existing-bench baseline via RCH/local fallback, RAYON_NUM_THREADS=16: cargo bench -p fnp-linalg --bench criterion_linalg -- matrix_power_nxn/size/1024 --measurement-time 3 --warm-up-time 1 => [28.835,29.432,30.043] ms. This is below the bead's stated Strassen crossover; next step must either extend matrix_power/matmul bench rows to >=2048/4096 as measurement infrastructure or prototype Strassen behind a high threshold and prove only on large rows.
close_reason: Measured no-ship (cc, 2026-06-09). Implemented classic Strassen-Winograd recursive GEMM (7 half-size products/level, crossover to packed_gemm at STRASSEN_MIN; quadrant sums formed directly from source to limit alloc). Correctness PROVEN: vs packed_gemm max rel err 8e-11 < 1e-10 (tolerance, reassociated) + golden sha256 87704a84... + sub-threshold bit-exact fallthrough. PERF = 5x REGRESSION: same-process best-of-N (RAYON=16) n=2048 packed 51.1ms vs strassen 260.0ms (0.197x), n=4096 packed 398.2ms vs strassen 2215ms (0.180x). ROOT CAUSE — packed_gemm is ALREADY near-optimal for the ISA: 2048^3*2/0.051s = 337 Gflop/s = ~88% of AVX2-no-FMA peak (8 flops/cyc/core x 16 cores x 3GHz = 384 Gflop/s). Strassen's 1/8-multiply saving cannot overcome the O(n^2) combine + per-node allocation + many-small-packed_gemm-call overhead when the base GEMM is at 88% of peak. Even a perfectly-tuned Strassen tops out at the 8/7=1.14x/level ratio minus overhead — never 2.0x at benchable n. Source fully discarded (worktree). DEFINITIVE CONCLUSION: the fnp GEMM (and thus every blocked factorization: LU/cholesky/qr trailing updates, matrix_power, multi_dot) is FMA-INSTRUCTION-bound. The 2x gap to scipy-OpenBLAS = exactly the FMA factor (OpenBLAS AVX2+FMA = 16 flops/cyc/core = 768 Gflop/s peak). NO safe-Rust algorithm (Strassen, recursive blocked LU [xz111], threshold/gate tuning) closes an instruction-level gap. The SOLE remaining lever for the entire linalg/GEMM perf frontier is bead 8vdtg (relax forbid(unsafe_code) for a vetted+fuzzed std::arch AVX2+FMA microkernel — still 100% Rust toolchain, no C/BLAS). Do not re-attempt Strassen or any GEMM-scheduling lever; the answer is 8vdtg and it needs user sign-off.

title: Reject infinite negative_binomial n like NumPy
description: Upstream NumPy Generator.negative_binomial rejects n=+inf with ValueError ('n too large or p too small'). fnp-random::Generator::negative_binomial currently rejects n <= 0 and NaN but not positive infinity, so infinite n can enter gamma/poisson sampling instead of fail-closing like NumPy. Add explicit finite-n validation and regression coverage without changing valid finite sampling.
close_reason: Implemented negative_binomial infinite n rejection parity

title: Match NumPy beta tiny-shape branch
description: Upstream NumPy random_beta special-cases a < 3e-103 and b < 3e-103 by drawing one uniform and returning 1.0 when (a+b)*U < a, otherwise 0.0. fnp-random::Generator::beta currently routes tiny shapes through gamma sampling, which underflows and consumes the wrong RNG stream. Add the tiny-shape branch and deterministic stream-parity regression coverage in fnp-random.

title: [perf][no-gaps][SHIPPED] blocked right-looking Cholesky via packed GEMM — 2.4-5.2x
description: SHIPPED b07396f3. Same blocked-factorization template as blocked LU (54c28e3c), applied to Cholesky. Unblocked dot-product cholesky_nxn (behind np.linalg.cholesky + SPD solve) -> blocked LAPACK-dpotrf: width-128 panels, factor 128x128 diagonal block, solve panel L21=A21*L11^-T, trailing A22 -= L21*L21^T via packed_gemm (k=128 clears parallel gate). Tolerance parity (re-association; not bit-exact). n=12 bit-exact golden stays unblocked (gated n>=896). Proof: L*L^T=A <1e-9 + blocked==unblocked <1e-9 (n=160/200/256), suite 263/263, SAME-PROCESS A/B 2.42x@1024 4.53x@1536 5.24x@2048. Campaign: blocked LU (6x) + blocked Cholesky (5.2x) shipped. NEXT: blocked QR (Householder WY representation: accumulate panel reflectors into a block W*Y, apply to trailing as GEMM) — bigger lift than LU/chol; complex LU also blockable. REUSABLE TEMPLATE: gate n>=896, NB=128, panel-factor + triangular-solve + trailing-GEMM-via-packed_gemm, same-process A/B, tolerance test vs unblocked ref.

title: [perf][no-gaps][SHIPPED] blocked right-looking LU via packed GEMM trailing update — up to 6x
description: SHIPPED 54c28e3c. Replaces the memory-bound unblocked LU rank-1 sweep (behind solve/inv/det/lstsq) with LAPACK-dgetrf-shape blocked LU: width-128 column panels, panel factorization (full-col pivot + whole-matrix row swaps + in-panel rank-1), U12 triangular solve vs unit-lower L11, then trailing A22 -= L21*U12 via the cache-blocked packed_gemm (compute-bound, parallel). Same pivot sequence as unblocked -> numerically equivalent up to GEMM re-association = TOLERANCE parity (LU not bit-reproducible; conformance tolerance-based like numpy LAPACK). NaN/Inf det path keeps unblocked (LAPACK NaN-pivot passthrough). Proof: blocked_lu_reconstructs_pivoted_matrix (P*A=L*U <1e-9, pivots match unblocked, n=160/200/256), full suite 262/262, SAME-PROCESS A/B 1.28x@1024 3.12x@1536 5.98x@2048. Gated n>=896 (crossover ~768, margin vs noise, no small-n regression). NB=128 chosen so trailing GEMM k>=128 clears packed_gemm parallel gate. ALL same-process measured (methodology fix e8b5723f). NEXT: same blocked-via-GEMM approach for Cholesky (right-looking, trailing -= L21*L21^T via syrk/GEMM) + QR (blocked Householder WY representation -> GEMM). complex LU could also block.

title: [perf][no-gaps][SHIPPED] blocked Householder QR via compact-WY block reflectors — 8-17.5x
description: SHIPPED e188fc8e. Blocked QR (LAPACK dgeqrf/dlarft/dlarfb): width-128 panels, factor panel unblocked storing V+tau, build nb×nb upper-tri T with H_jb..H_{pend-1}=I-V·T·V^T, apply block reflector to trailing R and to Q as GEMMs. DIRECTION KEY: Q right-applied forward order uses T; R left-applied increasing-k = H_{pend-1}..H_jb = (I-VTV^T)^T = I-V·T^T·V^T uses T^T (initial bug: used T for R -> Q·R err ~2.0; fixed with T^T). Tolerance parity. n=8 golden stays unblocked (gated 896). HUGE win because unblocked QR's explicit per-reflector Q accumulation is O(n^3) brutally slow (2048=53s -> 3s). Proof: Q·R=A + Q^T·Q=I + blocked==unblocked all <1e-9, suite 265/265, same-process A/B 8.25x@1024 12.93x@1536 17.51x@2048. Powers np.linalg.qr + lstsq. CAMPAIGN COMPLETE for 4 major factorizations: LU 6x + Cholesky 5.2x + TRSM 4.9x + QR 17.5x, all via the packed GEMM, all tolerance-parity, all same-process-verified. NEXT: eig/svd Householder reductions (tridiag/bidiag - blockable dsytrd/dgebrd, harder); or complex packed GEMM -> blocked complex LU.

title: [perf][no-gaps][SHIPPED] blocked symmetric tridiagonalization (dsytrd) — 2.1-2.8x, faster eigvalsh
description: SHIPPED 2d2acb18. np.linalg.eigvalsh routes native all-sizes; unblocked Householder tridiagonalization re-streams the trailing matrix per column (left+right sweep) -> DRAM-bound ~0.8 GFLOP/s (2048 eigvalsh ~14s). Blocked dsytrd/dlatrd (values-only): width-64 panels reduce nb cols producing reflectors V + vectors W (w=tau·A·v-(tau²·vᵀAv/2)·v) via symmetric matvecs vs panel-start trailing + rank-2 corrections from prior panel reflectors, then trailing A22 -= V·Wᵀ+W·Vᵀ (symmetric rank-2k) via packed GEMM. Halves trailing memory traffic + GEMM-izes the bulk. Tolerance parity. Q-path (eigh w/ vectors) keeps unblocked; gated n>=384. LANDED FIRST TRY. Proof: d,e match unblocked <1e-8 (n=128/160/200), suite 266/266, same-process A/B 2.09x@512 2.32x@1024 2.80x@2048. 🏁 CAMPAIGN: LU 6x + Cholesky 5.2x + TRSM 4.9x + QR 17.5x + tridiag(dsytrd) 2.8x — all via packed GEMM, all tolerance-parity, all same-process-verified. NEXT: block the eigh-WITH-VECTORS path (Q accumulation via compact-WY like QR + this dsytrd) for np.linalg.eigh; Hessenberg reduction (dgehrd) for eigvals(non-symmetric).

title: [perf][no-gaps][SHIPPED] blocked tridiagonalization-with-Q (dsytrd+dorgtr) — 2.75-3.22x, faster eigh
description: SHIPPED 61bd7aa0. eigh-with-vectors used unblocked two-sided tridiag reduction + per-column Q accumulation (DRAM-bound), the dominant eigh cost (~2.7s/3.8s@1024) after the values dsytrd shipped. Extended blocked dsytrd to accumulate Q: each panel's reflectors -> compact-WY block reflector I-V·T·V^T (forward T), applied to Q via GEMMs (Q:=Q·(I-VTV^T)) = dorgtr, same technique as blocked QR's 17.5x. Tolerance parity. Gated n>=384. Proof: A=Q·T·Q^T + Q^TQ=I <1e-9 (n=400/512), suite 268/268, same-process A/B 2.75x@512 3.22x@1024, end-to-end eigh@1024 3830->2206ms. eigh reduction NOW FULLY BLOCKED (dsytrd reduction + dorgtr Q-build both via packed GEMM; QR-iter fast via last turn's transpose). THIS TURN ALSO: range-restriction of hessenberg_qr_iter left rotation was a NO-SHIP (same-process A/B 1.05-1.14x — the eig bottleneck is SWEEP COUNT/single-shift convergence, not per-sweep column range). REMAINING: eig (nonsym) qr_iter single-shift convergence ~14x suboptimal -> needs double-shift Francis (hard); Hessenberg reduction unblocked (block via dgehrd).

title: [perf][no-gaps][SHIPPED] blocked multi-RHS TRSM via packed GEMM — up to 4.9x (fast inv)

Blocked TRSM (dtrsm shape): forward top-down row-blocks (nb diagonal solve + trailing X[be..] -= L21*X[block] via packed_gemm), back bottom-up (X[..ib] -= U12*X[block]). Same template as blocked LU/Cholesky. Tolerance parity (re-association). Gated n>=896 && m>=128 (single/few-RHS keep row-by-row). Proof: blocked==unblocked <1e-9 + A*X=I <1e-7 (n=160/200/256), suite 264/264, SAME-PROCESS A/B 1.21x@1024 3.40x@1536 4.94x@2048. inv now fully BLAS-class (blocked LU + blocked TRSM both via GEMM). CAMPAIGN: blocked LU 6x + Cholesky 5.2x + TRSM 4.9x shipped. NEXT: blocked QR (Householder WY, bigger lift) — qr_nxn golden n=8 stays unblocked; or complex LU/cholesky (need complex packed GEMM first).

title: Replace absolute convergence thresholds in sqrtm and logm with relative checks
description:
## Problem

Matrix square root (sqrtm_nxn) and matrix logarithm (logm_nxn) use absolute convergence thresholds:

### sqrtm_nxn (Denman-Beavers iteration)
- Line ~2626: convergence check `1e-14` — compares max element difference between successive iterates
- Problem: For matrices with large entries, convergence to 1e-14 absolute difference may never occur or may be meaningless. For matrices with small entries, 1e-14 is too loose.

### logm_nxn
- Line ~2666: scaling threshold `0.5` — checks ||M - I|| < 0.5 to decide when to stop scaling
- This is actually reasonable (it's a relative-to-identity check) and standard in the algorithm
- Line ~2693: Taylor convergence `1e-15` — absolute check on term size
- Problem: Same scale-sensitivity issue as sqrtm

## What SciPy Does

SciPy's sqrtm uses Schur decomposition followed by a triangular square root (Björck-Hammarling algorithm), not Denman-Beavers. But if using iterative methods, they check relative convergence: `||Y_{k+1} - Y_k|| / ||Y_k|| < eps`.

## Proposed Fix

### sqrtm_nxn
1. At each iteration, compute `rel_change = max_diff / max(||Y_k||, 1.0)`
2. Converge when `rel_change < f64::EPSILON * 100` (few ULPs of relative change)
3. Keep SQRTM_MAX_ITERATIONS as safety cap

### logm_nxn
1. The `0.5` scaling threshold is fine (it's relative to the identity matrix)
2. Replace `1e-15` Taylor convergence with adaptive stopping (covered by the Taylor adaptive stopping bead)

## Validation

- sqrtm tests must pass (especially the A = sqrtm(A)² roundtrip identity)
- logm tests must pass
- funm tests must pass (funm uses sqrtm and logm internally)
- Test with both well-conditioned and poorly-scaled matrices

## Files to Modify

- `crates/fnp-linalg/src/lib.rs` — sqrtm_nxn convergence check
close_reason: sqrtm convergence now relative (eps*64*||Y||), logm Taylor uses adaptive term-vs-result comparison, eigvector normalization guard uses f64::MIN_POSITIVE. All 201 tests pass.

title: [deadlock-audit] [HIGH] shared-view itemset can partially mutate after sidecar lock failure
description: crates/fnp-ufunc/src/lib.rs:2422-2458 — UFuncArrayView::itemset acquires the buffer write lock, validates, writes data[offset] = value, then attempts to acquire the integer sidecar write lock. If the sidecar RwLock is poisoned or otherwise returns Err, itemset returns an error after the shared buffer was already mutated, leaving the float bridge and exact integer sidecar out of sync. This is a lock/cancellation-safety defect on the public SharedSidecar/UFuncArrayView construction path; acquire/validate the sidecar before mutating the buffer or rollback atomically on sidecar lock failure.
close_reason: Fixed in c87c66b - lock acquisition reordered for atomicity

title: [perf][no-gaps][SHIPPED] eliminate redundant PyO3 bridge copies for f64 — cumsum/cumprod ~8x
description: SHIPPED 16718455. Bridge did multiple O(n) passes per native call. INPUT: astype(target) always copied even when dtype matched -> astype(copy=False) (returns as-is when already correct). OUTPUT: f64-no-sidecar went through to_storage() (clone + validity scan + cast_to(F64) pass) before buffer copy -> copy array.values() straight into numpy buffer (f64 holds NaN/inf, no gate needed). Pure copy-elision, bit-identical (9/9 vs numpy: diff/cumsum/cumprod/clip/roll/outer in f64/i64/f32). Benefits EVERY extract_precise consumer + every f64-returning op. cumsum 43.3->5.44ms (now beats numpy), cumprod 43.2->5.33ms, clip 2.3x, roll 1.8x, diff 1.6x, outer 1.6x. Residual diff/roll/outer/clip gap = remaining input to_vec + output copy_from_slice (1 pass each, unavoidable without true zero-copy view-borrow). LESSON: the PyO3 bridge had 4 copies (astype + to_vec + to_storage-clone/cast + copy_from_slice) vs numpy's 1; eliminate the 2 redundant ones (astype-copy, to_storage-clone) for huge wins on memory-bound ops.

title: Parallelize RaptorQ sidecar encode and decode configuration
description:
Context:
- RaptorQ sidecars protect conformance and benchmark artifacts, but raptorq_artifacts.rs currently records encoding_parallelism and decoding_parallelism as 1. Large artifact bundles are a natural place to use available cores without changing NumPy semantics.
- The implementation must preserve deterministic artifact hashes and symbol ordering.

Scope:
- Add a bounded parallelism configuration for RaptorQ sidecar generation and decode proof paths, using existing asupersync primitives where appropriate.
- Make the configured worker count explicit in sidecar metadata and reports.
- Preserve deterministic output ordering, authentication, scrub, and decode proof behavior across worker counts.

Acceptance criteria:
- Unit tests prove sidecars generated with worker counts 1 and N decode to the same source hash and stable report metadata.
- Tests cover invalid worker counts and fail-closed behavior for corrupted symbols.
- Validation commands include: rch exec -- cargo test -p fnp-conformance raptorq; rch exec -- cargo run -p fnp-conformance --bin generate_raptorq_sidecars -- --parallelism <n> or equivalent; rch exec -- cargo run -p fnp-conformance --bin run_raptorq_gate -- --report-path target/raptorq_parallel_report.json.
- Closeout includes before/after timing on at least one large local artifact or explains why no speedup was expected.
close_reason: Completed: added bounded RaptorQ parallelism config, deterministic metadata in sidecar/scrub/proof artifacts, fail-closed symbol hash checks, CLI/script flags, and raptorq tests/gates. Local default-bundle timing showed no expected speedup on small committed artifacts: parallelism=1 7.07s, parallelism=2 7.07s, final parallelism=1 regen 6.99s.

title: Add convergence-adaptive stopping to matrix exponential and logarithm Taylor series
description:
## Problem

Matrix exponential (expm_nxn) and matrix logarithm (logm_nxn) both use a fixed number of Taylor series terms (now centralized as MATRIX_FUNC_TAYLOR_TERMS = 30). This is wasteful for well-conditioned matrices (converges in 5-10 terms) and potentially insufficient for ill-conditioned ones.

## Current Behavior

### expm_nxn
- Computes exp(A) = I + A + A²/2! + A³/3! + ... (30 terms)
- No early termination when terms become negligible
- For a matrix with small norm, this computes 20+ unnecessary terms

### logm_nxn
- First scales A to be near identity via repeated square roots (up to LOGM_MAX_SCALING_ITERATIONS = 20)
- Then computes log(I+X) = X - X²/2 + X³/3 - ... (30 terms)
- The scaling step ensures X is small, so the Taylor series should converge fast
- But no early termination check

## What LAPACK/SciPy Does

- SciPy's expm uses Padé approximation (not Taylor), which is more numerically stable
- SciPy's logm uses inverse scaling and squaring with Padé, checking convergence at each step
- For our Taylor approach, we should at minimum check `||term|| / ||result|| < eps` to stop early

## Proposed Fix

1. In both Taylor loops, compute the Frobenius norm of the current term
2. If `term_norm < f64::EPSILON * result_norm`, break early
3. Keep MATRIX_FUNC_TAYLOR_TERMS as the maximum cap (safety net)
4. This is backward-compatible: results within f64 precision should be identical

## Considerations

- The early termination adds a norm computation per iteration (~n² operations), but the Taylor multiplication is already O(n³) per term, so the overhead is negligible
- For the matrix logarithm, the scaling step already ensures fast convergence, so this is mainly a performance improvement
- For the matrix exponential, this could also improve accuracy by not accumulating floating-point errors from unnecessary terms

## Validation

- All linalg tests must pass (especially expm, logm, funm tests)
- Results should be numerically identical within f64 precision
- Test with both small-norm and large-norm matrices

## Files to Modify

- `crates/fnp-linalg/src/lib.rs` — expm_nxn and logm_nxn Taylor loops
close_reason: Both expm and logm Taylor series now use adaptive convergence (term_norm < eps * result_norm) instead of absolute thresholds. MATRIX_FUNC_TAYLOR_TERMS remains as safety cap. All 201 tests pass.

title: perf(fnp-linalg): blocked bidiagonalization (dgebrd-style) for SVD — serial bidiag is the last O(n^3) gap, target ~2x at n>=512
description:
PROFILE (n=512, local loaded): svd_mxn_full 112ms > eigvalsh 83ms > qr 73ms > svd_nxn 65ms. QR/LU/tridiag all have blocked+parallel paths (QR engaged at n>=768 via cde6e1a8; LU blocked at 896 but only ~1.3x there, memory-bound, no >=2x lever; tridiag_reduce_blocked at n>=384 uses packed_gemm for rank-2k update + Q accum). The ONE remaining O(n^3) serial hot path is SVD's Golub-Kahan BIDIAGONALIZATION (svd_bidiag_qr_values ~line 2786, svd_bidiag_qr_full ~line 2441): per-column left Householder zeros col j below diag (d[j]) + right Householder zeros row j past superdiag (e[j]), both applied to work as cache-friendly two-pass but FULLY SERIAL over j. 

LEVER: blocked bidiagonalization (LAPACK dgebrd). Reduce a panel of nb columns maintaining X(m x nb), Y(n x nb) so the trailing update is A22 -= V*Y^T + X*W^T via TWO packed_gemm calls (BLAS-3, parallel). TEMPLATE: tridiag_reduce_blocked (line 3242) is the symmetric one-sided analog — same compact-WY + packed_gemm structure; bidiag is the non-symmetric TWO-sided version (separate left v_house and right w_house per column, X and Y instead of just V and W). 

GATE: add BIDIAG_BLOCK_MIN, engage where blocked beats serial >=2x (measure same-process like QR; expect crossover ~384-512 given tridiag's is 384). Wire into svd_bidiag_qr_values (values: svd_nxn/svd_mxn/matrix_rank) FIRST (no U/Vt accumulation = simplest), then svd_bidiag_qr_full (with-vectors). 

PROOF: singular values are UNIQUE so blocked must match serial svd to ~1e-9 rel (existing svd golden/reconstruct tests cover this) + new golden sha256 of svd_nxn(512) singular values + same-process blocked-vs-serial bidiag A/B report (>=2x). Parity is tolerance-based (SVD never bit-reproducible vs LAPACK). 

FOLLOWUP after bidiag: bidiagonal divide-and-conquer (dbdsdc) replaces the O(n^3) Golub-Reinsch QR sweep in the WITH-VECTORS path (svd_bidiag_qr_full) with O(n^2 log n).
notes: REJECTED ALTERNATIVE (cc, this window): one-sided JACOBI SVD (values, column-major contiguous columns, the fundamentally-different algorithm vs DLABRD bidiag). CORRECTNESS PROVEN: jacobi_svd_values_match_golub_kahan passes (sigma match svd_mxn to 1e-9 across 8x8/16x11/40x40/64x50; Jacobi is >= as accurate, singular values unique). But SERIAL SPEED: n=256 0.08x, n=512 0.06x (12-17x SLOWER than Golub-Kahan). Root cause: Jacobi does ~10x the flops of Golub-Kahan (O(sweeps*n^2*m), ~8 sweeps). Column-major made it cache-friendly (no strided gather) but the flop disadvantage dominates. PARALLEL round-robin (n/2 disjoint column-pairs per round) is the only handle but capped ~1.5-2x: n-1 sequential rounds => ~4000 fork/join barriers per SVD (511 rounds x 8 sweeps), overhead-limited, and safe disjoint-mutable-column access is intricate. Estimate parallel Jacobi ~1.8x (borderline, NOT clean >=2x). This is WHY LAPACK uses Jacobi (dgesvj) for ACCURACY not speed; dgesdd (Golub-Kahan) is the speed path. CONCLUSION for cjpas: BOTH alternatives to serial Golub-Kahan fail >=2x — blocked-bidiag (row-major GEMV friction, 0.24-0.32x) AND Jacobi (10x flops). The serial cache-friendly Golub-Kahan reducer may be at the parallel-safe-Rust ceiling for the VALUES path; the real remaining lever is the WITH-VECTORS path's O(n^3) Givens accumulation or bidiagonal divide-and-conquer (dbdsdc). Jacobi code stashed.
close_reason: Completed current SVD values-path pass: shipped exact-order right-Householder row-slice keep in commit 98885ba3 (svd_nxn/512 62.459ms -> 57.777ms mean on ovh-a, SHA256 4c7c7aa6cdf4721a02c213c87c18eec2f977fef96cb9ca52b6b79a873f413f7a preserved). Rejected row-major blocked bidiag, Jacobi values path, row-parallel Householder, and direct-index follow-up on same-process/RCH evidence. Not a ceiling: next deeper route is a different primitive, either panel-major/communication-avoiding DLABRD/DGEBRD for with-vectors or the open Strassen/Winograd matmul bead franken_numpy-qr6h2.

title: Expanded metamorphic test coverage: 18 new MRs across 5 categories
description:
## Finding

metamorphic_math.rs had 10 MR categories (20 tests). Expanded to 15 categories (38 tests).

## New metamorphic relations added

### Category 11: Inverse Trigonometric
- arcsin(sin(x)) = x for x in [-π/2, π/2]
- arccos(cos(x)) = x for x in [0, π]
- arctan(tan(x)) = x for x in (-π/2, π/2)
- arcsinh(sinh(x)) = x
- arctanh(tanh(x)) = x

### Category 12: Statistical Invariants
- mean(x + c) = mean(x) + c (translation)
- mean(k*x) = k * mean(x) (scaling)
- var(x + c) = var(x) (translation invariance)
- var(k*x) = k² * var(x) (scaling)
- std(k*x) = |k| * std(x) (scaling)
- sum(k*x) = k * sum(x) (scaling)

### Category 13: Tanh Odd Function
- tanh(-x) = -tanh(x)

### Category 14: Cumulative/Differential
- diff(cumsum(x)) = x[1:]
- cumsum(diff(x)) + x[0] = x[1:]

### Category 15: FFT Inverse
- ifft(fft(x)) real parts = x, imaginary parts ≈ 0
- irfft(rfft(x), n) = x

## Files

- fnp-ufunc/tests/metamorphic_math.rs (+18 MRs)
close_reason: Complete: 18 new MRs validate math correctness

comments.text:
REVISION (plan-space refinement round 1):

WHERE: Same fixture infrastructure as franken_numpy-xfdw. Add to artifacts/fixtures/ and test in crates/fnp-conformance/src/lib.rs.

EXACT TEST CASES TO ADD (oracle-captured):
1. np.add(np.float64(5.0), np.arange(6).reshape(2,3)) → 0-d + 2-d broadcast
2. np.multiply(np.array([1,2,3]), np.array([[10],[20]])) → (3,) + (2,1) → (2,3)
3. np.add(np.zeros((1,3,1)), np.zeros((2,1,4))) → (1,3,1) + (2,1,4) → (2,3,4) shape-only
4. np.subtract(np.zeros((0,3)), np.zeros((3,))) → empty dim broadcast → shape (0,3)
5. np.add(np.array([1.0]), np.array([2.0, 3.0, 4.0])) → (1,) + (3,) → (3,)
6. np.multiply(np.ones((1,1,1)), np.ones((5,))) → triple-1 + (5,) → (1,1,5)
7. np.add(np.float64(1.0), np.float64(2.0)) → scalar + scalar → scalar
8. np.power(np.arange(4).reshape(2,2), np.array([2])) → (2,2) + (1,) broadcast
9. np.divide(np.ones((3,1)), np.ones((1,4))) → (3,1) / (1,4) → (3,4) outer-product style
10. np.add(np.zeros((2,1,3)), np.zeros((4,1))) → (2,1,3) + (4,1) → incompatible! should error

VALIDATION: Shape, dtype, and value comparison against NumPy oracle. Case 10 must produce an error in both NumPy and FrankenNumPy. Tolerance 1e-12 for float64.

LOGGING: Print fixture ID + shapes + pass/fail for each case.

ACCEPTANCE: All 10+ pass. Zero regressions.

title: Fail performance budget gate on uninstrumented workload paths
description:
Context:
- run_performance_budget_gate already computes uninstrumented_budget_paths and warns when candidate reports omit SLO-covered workloads. For a project with performance governance, missing instrumentation should be a hard failure in strict gate mode.
- This is a small, high-leverage gate hardening bead that protects later optimization work from measuring only the easy paths.

Scope:
- Add a strict option or default gate behavior that fails when any budgeted workload lacks candidate telemetry.
- Preserve useful diagnostics: workload name, path family, expected measurement fields, and remediation guidance.
- Make the behavior visible in test-contract gate output so CI and agents see the missing evidence.

Acceptance criteria:
- Unit tests cover pass with full instrumentation, fail with one missing workload, and report contents for missing workload metadata.
- An e2e or binary-level test proves the CLI exits nonzero in strict mode and writes the expected JSON report.
- Validation commands include: rch exec -- cargo test -p fnp-conformance run_performance_budget_gate; rch exec -- cargo run -p fnp-conformance --bin run_performance_budget_gate -- --candidate-path <fixture missing path> --report-path target/perf_budget_missing_path.json --coverage-floor 1.0.
- Any compatibility choice between warning and failure is documented in the report schema, not hidden in code comments.
close_reason: Completed: performance budget gate now fail-closes on uninstrumented SLO paths, reports fail_closed policy/diagnostics, and G7 contract requires strict budget outputs. Validated with RCH focused tests, clippy, binary gate failure proof, rustfmt --check on touched files, git diff --check, and UBS triage.

title: [perf][no-gaps][SHIPPED] 2-D np.diag/np.diagonal read only the diagonal — ~100x bit-exact
description: SHIPPED 1873a371. Siblings of trace fast path (b27f4e62). np.diag(2-D)/np.diagonal returned N values from N×N matrix but copied whole matrix across PyO3 (O(n²)). Read np.diagonal VIEW (O(1) strided) + extract N elems. Element-copy (no sum) so BIT-FOR-BIT == numpy: verified 777 cases. ~0.7ms->0.007ms ~100x; 9-12x residual vs numpy zero-copy view = PyO3 overhead. 1-D diag (constructs matrix) + swapped/N-D axes keep generic path.

title: [bug][parity] native diagonal/trace_axis swapped axes (axis1>axis2) + nonzero offset gives wrong values
description: PRE-EXISTING (not introduced by perf work). fnp.diagonal(M, offset=k, axis1=1, axis2=0) and trace with swapped axes+offset diverge from numpy: e.g. diagonal axes=(1,0) k=2 returns the k=-2 diagonal. Root: native UFuncArray::diagonal/trace_axis for 2-D ignore axis order (trace_axis calls self.trace(offset) regardless; diagonal mis-signs offset under swap). Found via diag_check.py (4/777) and trace_check.py (axes=(1,0) off=2). numpy: trace(a,1,0,offset=k)=transpose diagonal = offset -k. Fix in fnp-ufunc diagonal/trace_axis axis normalization; add swapped-axis+offset conformance cases.
close_reason: Fixed in 55b18007: negate offset when a1>a2 in 2-D diagonal/trace_axis shortcuts. diag/diagonal now 777/777 bit-exact vs numpy; trace swapped matches to 1e-14 (residual = pre-existing summation ULP). Regression test diagonal_2d_swapped_axes_negates_offset.

title: [bug][parity] np.diff float32 with n>=2 diverges from numpy (internal f64 accumulation)
description: PRE-EXISTING (not from diff-noclone; n>=2 uses untouched generic path). diff_check.py: float32 inputs with n>=2 (e.g. shape (1000,2000) n=2 ax=1; (500,400,7) n=2/3 all axes) diverge from numpy bit-pattern. 12/54 fail, ALL float32 n>=2 (n==1 float32 + all f64/i64 bit-exact). Root: UFuncArray stores f32 inputs widened to f64 and computes successive diffs in f64, then narrows to f32 at the end — numpy computes each pass in float32, so intermediate rounding differs and compounds over n passes. Fix: for float32 dtype, round to f32 between passes (or compute in f32). Low user impact (n>=2 float32 rare) but real parity gap.
close_reason: Fixed in ef099f3e: round each diff pass to f32/f16 precision before feeding the next pass (n>=2). round_to_f32(f64_sub)==f32_sub exactly for f32-representable inputs. diff differential now 54/54 bit-exact vs numpy (was 12 float32 n>=2 fails). 23 tests pass.

title: [perf][no-gaps][SHIPPED] np.diff n=1 no-clone fast path — 1.9-3.5x bit-exact
description: SHIPPED 8336c4c0. diff() cloned the whole input before its loop even for n==1 (read-only first pass) — wasted 16MB copy making diff ~1.94x slower than sibling cumsum. n==1 fast path computes from self; inner==1 uses tight windows(2) (vectorized). Bit-exact (same v1-v0 order); n>1 path unchanged. Same-process A/B (load-independent): 1.91x@1M / 3.48x@2M (clone grows with N, fast flat ~5.5ms). Brings np.diff to cumsum parity (diff/cumsum 1.94->1.05). Proof diff_n1_noclone_vs_clone_speedup. 22 diff tests pass.

title: Build failures in fnp-python and fnp-ufunc from custom reduce implementation
description:
The recent custom reduce implementation for frompyfunc introduces several compilation errors and warnings:
1. fnp-python: tuple.len() uses question mark operator on a usize (E0277).
2. fnp-python: reduce_frompyfunc_values requires T: Clone, but Py_PyAny does not implement Clone natively in PyO3 0.21+ (E0277).
3. fnp-python: use of moved value callable when calling callable.bind(py) after moving it into the struct (E0382).
4. fnp-ufunc tests: Type annotations needed for E in Ok(lhs * rhs) (E0282).
5. fnp-ufunc: FromPyFuncReduceIdentity Default impl should be derived (warning).
6. fnp-ufunc: reduce_frompyfunc_values takes 9 arguments (warning: too many arguments).
7. fnp-ufunc: collapsible if statement in where_mask check (warning).
Please fix these compilation errors and warnings.
close_reason: Fixed custom reduce build failures in fnp-python/fnp-ufunc

title: fnp-iter: add 19 metamorphic tests for iterator operations
description:
Finding: fnp-iter had ZERO metamorphic tests despite containing complex index manipulation logic (ndindex, linear_index_to_multi_index, multi_index_to_linear_index) that has rich invariant properties.

Fix: Created crates/fnp-iter/tests/metamorphic_iter.rs with 19 tests covering 8 metamorphic relations:
- MR1: Index roundtrip (linear → multi → linear = identity) for 1D/2D/3D/4D
- MR2: Coverage completeness (|ndindex(shape)| = product(shape)) for various shapes
- MR3: Uniqueness (no duplicate indices in iteration)
- MR4: Bounds (all indices within shape dimensions)
- MR5: Order independence (C vs F order covers same indices)
- MR6: Multi-index determinism (same input → same output)
- MR7: Boundary values (first and last indices correct)
- MR8: Element count matches shape product

Applied: /testing-metamorphic skill
close_reason: done

title: Fix dtype fixture u8 naming: u8 means uint64, not uint8
description:
The dtype_metamorphic_cases.json fixture has a bug:

```json
{
  "id": "dtype002_lossless_cast_destination_u8_u32",
  "lhs": "u8",  // BUG: parsed as uint64, not uint8
  "rhs": "u32"
}
```

In NumPy's byte-width naming convention:
- u1 = uint8 (1 byte)
- u2 = uint16 (2 bytes)  
- u4 = uint32 (4 bytes)
- u8 = uint64 (8 bytes)

Fix: Change "u8" to "uint8" or "u1" to test actual uint8 behavior.

File: crates/fnp-conformance/fixtures/packet002_dtype/dtype_metamorphic_cases.json:236
notes: Fixed: changed lhs from 'u8' (uint64) to 'uint8' (actual uint8). dtype_packet002_f_suites_are_green passes.
close_reason: done

title: [PORT] PyRandomState missing 15 distributions (binomial/poisson/multinomial/…)
description:
PyRandomState exposes 34 methods. numpy.random.RandomState exposes ~49.
The 15 missing methods block the t44q module-level legacy aliases from
landing for these names (they're gated by hasattr(_rand, name)):

  binomial, poisson, negative_binomial, hypergeometric, logseries,
  vonmises, wald, multinomial, dirichlet, multivariate_normal,
  noncentral_chisquare, noncentral_f,
  choice, shuffle, permutation

Port each to PyRandomState via the fnp_random::RandomState core. Some
may need core RandomState additions (binomial, poisson, etc. currently
live on Generator only — legacy RandomState has its own algorithms).

Acceptance: scripts/fnp_random_compliance_matrix.py reports MUST
coverage ≥ 95%; new PyRandomState methods pass matches_numpy parity
tests.

Part of franken_numpy-lxmb.

title: numpy.vander python wrapper parity

title: [perf][no-gaps] QR 512 compact-WY threshold probe
description: Profile-backed follow-up after SVD left-update no-ship. Fresh RCH profile on vmi1149989 at fcfb84f2/7076d5ac showed qr_nxn/size/512 = 55.213/63.288/70.763 ms, the next large linalg surface after SVD and matrix_power. Existing fnp-linalg has a compact-WY blocked QR primitive (QR_BLOCK_MIN=896) while n=512 still uses the unblocked Householder path. One lever: test lowering the compact-WY gate to engage the blocked algorithm at 512 if same-worker RCH baseline/rebench proves Score>=2.0. Isomorphism: QR sign convention/tie-breaking unchanged within existing tolerance contract; no RNG; finite-input error policy unchanged; golden/QR tests must pass. If the threshold probe regresses, record no-ship and route to a deeper blocked SVD/CA-bidiagonalization primitive.
close_reason: Measured no-ship. Target was profile-backed QR 512: RCH vmi1149989 baseline qr_nxn/size/512 = 54.832/57.313/59.673 ms. One lever tested: lower QR_BLOCK_MIN 896->512 so 512x512 routes through the existing compact-WY blocked QR primitive. Behavior proof passed via RCH cargo test -p fnp-linalg qr_ -- --nocapture: 19 unit + 2 conformance + 2 golden + 1 metamorphic QR tests passed; QR ordering/sign policy remains tolerance-based, RNG N/A, finite-input error policy unchanged. Same-worker rebench = 131.84/133.33/136.34 ms, 0.43x center speed, Score < 2.0. Source hunk removed; not shipped. Next route: deeper SVD blocked/compact-WY or communication-avoiding bidiagonalization, not threshold probing.

title: [perf][no-gaps] Parallelize histogram2d/histogramdd binning (compute-bound after partition_point fix)
description:
UNBLOCKED by 253d0e20: histogram2d and histogramdd now bin each element via partition_point over the per-axis edge arrays (O(log bins) per element per dim) instead of O(1) floor division. That makes the per-element bin lookup the dominant cost and independent per observation, so they are now genuine compute-bound parallelization targets -- same shape as the shipped 1-D histogram lever (0b7f1d5d).

RECIPE (mirror 0b7f1d5d exactly): the bin lookup is the expensive independent part; the count accumulation must stay serial because counts are EXACT INTEGERS (order-independent, so the tally is bit-identical regardless of evaluation order).
- histogram2d (~lib.rs:17917): par_iter over the (xv,yv) pairs producing a Vec of flat indices xi*ybins+yi (each via the existing bin_of partition_point closure), then serial accumulate hist[idx] += 1.0. Gate elems>=8192 and (xbins*ybins)>=2 and rayon::current_num_threads()>=2.
- histogramdd (~lib.rs:18061): par_iter over observations producing Option flat_idx (None when the existing valid early-exit trips), collect, serial accumulate the Some values. Same gate on n_obs.

BIT-EXACTNESS: integer counts are order-independent so the result is bit-for-bit identical to serial; the par_iter map is order-preserving anyway. Lock with histogram2d_parallel_matches_serial_reference / histogramdd_parallel_matches_serial_reference comparing bin counts to_bits() vs a serial reference on above-threshold input (place tests near the existing histogram2d tests ~lib.rs:47016, NOT near ~41759).

COORDINATION: lib.rs is BlackThrush-reserved and they are actively editing fft_pow2 (~28141, bead ixs5y.6) plus a test block (~41759). Use the selective-commit technique (see memory feedback note: git apply --cached with awk-filtered hunks) OR wait until lib.rs is uncontended. Production hunk (~17900) and test hunk (~47100) are both disjoint from the peer's regions.

VALUE: moderate (histogram2d/dd less common than 1-D histogram) but completes the histogram-family perf parity and is bit-exact + low-risk. Part of epic franken_numpy-ixs5y.

title: Preserve non-finite matrix norm parity
description:
## Problem
`fnp_linalg::matrix_norm_nxn` and `matrix_norm_2x2` currently reject any non-finite matrix entries. The public 2-D `UFuncArray::norm()` bridge routes into `matrix_norm_nxn`, so calls analogous to `np.linalg.norm([[nan, 1.0], [2.0, 3.0]])` fail instead of following NumPy behavior.

## Evidence
- `matrix_norm_nxn` currently returns `NormDetRankPolicyViolation` if any entry is non-finite
- NumPy returns `nan` for Frobenius/1/inf-style norms on NaN matrices
- NumPy returns `inf`, finite values, or `nan` for non-spectral norms with `inf` entries depending on the norm order

## Goal
Match NumPy’s non-finite semantics for matrix norm orders exposed through the public API, while preserving explicit error behavior where SVD-backed spectral/nuclear norms genuinely fail on NaN input.

## Files
- `crates/fnp-linalg/src/lib.rs`
close_reason: Completed

title: [perf][no-ship] Parallel LU trailing-update is memory-bound, does not scale
description: REJECTED. Tried rayon par_chunks_mut over independent trailing rows in lu_decompose_inner (bit-exact: FNV identical). At 1024x1024 on 32-core Threadripper: 0.67x (SLOWER). The right-looking rank-1 trailing update is memory-bandwidth-bound (serial only 2.63 GFLOP/s on SSE2), so threads add dispatch overhead + L3 bandwidth contention with no compute headroom. The real path to BLAS-class large-LU is BLOCKED LU (panel factorization + cache-blocked GEMM trailing update), but that reorders FP accumulation and is NOT bit-exact vs the current serial kernel -> blocked by the absolute-parity constraint. Reverted; no rayon dep kept.
notes: No-ship documented. Reverted cleanly.

title: [perf][bug][SHIPPED] deflate complex 2x2 blocks in QR iteration — eig 14.7x (was pathological)
description: SHIPPED 686fedb8. np.linalg.eigvals -> fnp-linalg eig_nxn (Hessenberg + single-shift QR). Iteration only deflated 1x1 blocks; isolated COMPLEX 2x2 blocks never reduce under a real single shift -> spun to max_iter=60*n^2. Random real matrices generically have complex pairs -> PATHOLOGICAL: n=512 eigvals = 53.7 SECONDS, and on non-convergence the spectrum could be WRONG (perf AND correctness bug). Fix: standard Francis deflation — when active block is exactly 2x2 with disc<0 (complex), deflate directly (extract_schur_eigenvalues resolves the conjugate pair); real 2x2 left to normal QR (splits to 1x1 for the eigenvector path). Proof: blockdiag(2x2 rotation, 2) -> {±i,2} correct+instant; suite 266/266 incl eig_nxn_full eigenvectors; n=512 53702ms->3650ms (14.7x). LESSON: always MEASURE candidate ops — found this by timing eig (53s@512 jumped out). SIMPLE 1-criterion fix beat the full Francis double-shift rewrite I was scoping. NEXT: eig still 3.65s@512 (single-shift convergence still suboptimal; full double-shift -> <0.5s) + unblocked Hessenberg; eigh-WITH-VECTORS still 14-16s@1024 (tridiag-with-Q + QR-iter eigenvector accumulation).

title: [perf][no-gaps][SHIPPED] eigenvector accumulation into Q^T (contiguous) — 54-63x accum, eigh 4.3-4.8x
description: SHIPPED 72005e8b. np.linalg.eigh routes native. tridiag QR iteration accumulated Givens rotations into Q COLUMNS (q[row*n+kk], stride-n down rows) -> cache-thrash ~83 MFLOP/s, the dominant eigh-with-vectors cost (~11s@1024 for accumulation alone). Fix: accumulate into Q-TRANSPOSED so each rotation updates two CONTIGUOUS rows (split_at_mut), transpose back once. Bit-identical (same c·t1+s·t2 per element). Same-process A/B (Q matches <1e-12): 54.4x@512 63.5x@1024 on the accumulation; end-to-end eigh 4.8x@512 4.3x@1024; suite 267/267. SAME INSIGHT as GEMM packing: stride-n column walk -> contiguous = huge cache win. New eigh bottleneck = unblocked tridiag-reduce-WITH-Q (block it: dsytrd reduction + compact-WY Q like QR). REUSABLE: scan for stride-n column-walk accumulation loops (q[row*n+col]) — transpose to contiguous, bit-exact.

title: [perf][no-gaps] Zero-copy single-pass np.select for all-f64 condlist/choicelist
description: np.select all-f64 path ran a chained where_select fold (K full-array passes + K intermediate Vecs) plus cold extract/build Vecs (lglck), 5.5x slower than numpy at 4M. try_zerocopy_f64_select does a single pass: bool conditions viewed as uint8, f64 choices, scalar default; for each element walk conditions in order and copy first match (verbatim=bit-exact incl -0.0/inf/nan), else default. First-match-wins == numpy semantics. Self-speedup 6.77x@1M (41.24->6.09ms), 7.68x@4M (185.79->24.18ms); now beats numpy (24.18 vs 28.05). Golden sha256==numpy. Broadcasting/non-contiguous/non-scalar-default/non-f64 fall through. Lock test select_zerocopy_f64_bit_exact_matches_numpy.
close_reason: Shipped: try_zerocopy_f64_select, 6.77x@1M / 7.68x@4M, beats numpy, bit-exact golden sha256, lock test select_zerocopy_f64_bit_exact_matches_numpy

title: [perf] fnp-ufunc::matmul — naive 256^3 triple loop 2.76ms (~12 GFLOP/s); cache-blocking / B-transpose candidate
description:
## Scenario
core_ops baseline, run tests/artifacts/perf/2026-06-01_perf_baseline. matmul 256x256 . 256x256 (f64).

## Baseline (criterion n=30, Threadripper 5975WX, taskset-pinned)
- matmul_256x256_by_256x256: 2.759 ms  CI[2.753, 2.762], MAD 0.011 ms (~12.2 GFLOP/s).
For reference NumPy w/ OpenBLAS does this in well under 1 ms; the gap is the naive kernel.

## Evidence
10_baseline.md row matmul; criterion core_ops/matmul_256x256_by_256x256/new/estimates.json. Source: UFuncArray::matmul in fnp-ufunc/src/lib.rs (naive i-j-k triple loop, row-major, no cache blocking / no transpose of B / no SIMD-friendly accumulation).

## Hypothesized fix (one lever at a time)
Profile-confirm with a flamegraph once perf symbolization is available, then: (a) transpose/pack B so the inner loop is contiguous (kills the column-stride cache misses), or (b) register/cache-block (e.g. 8x8 micro-kernel) accumulation. Either is a one-lever change; prove unchanged via differential/conformance + numeric tolerance, then re-baseline against this artifact. Lower priority than franken_numpy-71n7p (the construction tax is systemic; matmul is one op).

## Owner
Filed by BlackThrush profiling pass (measurement only).
close_reason: Profiled two matmul loop unroll candidates and rejected both; golden output was unchanged but rch Criterion showed no acceptable win, so no code change was kept.

title: Phase pivot decision: declare parity 'done enough' and unlock disclaimed limitations
description:
## Why this exists

Per 2026-04-09 reality check, FrankenNumPy has cleared every architectural milestone in its README: SCE, dual-mode runtime, RaptorQ-everywhere durability, 9 Phase2C extraction packets, oracle-driven differential testing, 2,894 tests passing. The active bead pattern (last 17 closed beads, all NobleSnow on 2026-04-07/08) is uniformly small parity-debt grinding: cross-dtype binops, LeftShift/RightShift, comparison ops, multi-axis reductions, bitwise AND/OR/XOR. Each closed bead now adds smaller and smaller fractions of the legacy NumPy surface.

Meanwhile, the README's Limitations section explicitly disclaims FOUR strategic capabilities that were deferred when foundations were uncertain: (1) Python FFI / pip-installable, (2) BLAS/LAPACK backend, (3) multi-threading, (4) i64/u64 > 2^53 native arithmetic. Foundations are no longer uncertain. This bead is the explicit analysis-and-decision artifact that asks: at what point do we declare the parity wave "done enough" and pivot to the four deferred items?

**Critical: the agent produces the analysis, but the USER makes the final pivot decision.** This bead outputs a draft ADR for human review, not a unilateral architectural shift.

## Background / context for future-self

- 318 beads have been closed to date. The project velocity is ~68 beads/week over the last 7 days.
- The parity-debt pattern shows diminishing returns: early beads added entire op-families (masked arrays, datetime, polynomial families); recent beads add 15-24 fixture cases each for narrow edge-case coverage.
- The four disclaimed limitations are ALL listed in the README's Limitations section and FAQ, meaning users read them and form expectations around them. Every day they remain unaddressed is a day where the README's "planned but not implemented" stays true.
- commit 27f27f2 (2026-04-08) shows active investment in performance governance (memory/allocation SLOs) — the swarm is ALREADY drifting toward performance work organically. This bead formalizes that drift into an explicit decision.

## Goals

1. **Quantify remaining parity debt:** Scan every fnp-conformance fixture directory and the oracle capture manifest. Produce a list of every fixture that has: (a) no corresponding FrankenNumPy test, or (b) a known divergence (tolerance failure, dtype mismatch, shape mismatch). Group by op-family. Rough effort estimate per family (small/medium/large based on complexity of the divergence).

2. **Quantify cross-engine performance gap:** Consume the output of franken_numpy-azql. Summarize: median ratio, worst ratio, count of workloads in each band (green/yellow/red), and which op-families cluster in which bands.

3. **Define pivot criteria:** Propose a specific, measurable threshold. Suggested template:
   > "Pivot when: (a) fewer than N fixture families have uncovered divergences, AND (b) median cross-engine ratio is under M, AND (c) no single workload exceeds ratio R."
   The agent drafts this with concrete numbers informed by goals 1 and 2. The user approves, modifies, or rejects.

4. **Produce a draft ADR (Architecture Decision Record):** File at `docs/adr/ADR-001-parity-pivot.md` using the standard ADR format:
   ```markdown
   # ADR-001: Pivot from parity grinding to Phase 3 (FFI/BLAS/threading)

   ## Status
   PROPOSED (awaiting user decision)

   ## Context
   [Summary of goals 1-2 findings]

   ## Decision
   [User fills in after review]

   ## Criteria
   [The measurable threshold from goal 3]

   ## Consequences
   - Parity debt remaining: [list]
   - New beads to file for FFI/BLAS/threading/i64: [list]
   - Swarm reassignment plan: [which agents shift to what]
   ```

5. **Pre-draft beads for the four disclaimed limitations:** For each of (Python FFI, BLAS backend, multi-threading, native i64 arithmetic), produce a DRAFT bead with title, priority, rough scope, and key design questions that need answering before implementation. These are NOT filed until the user approves the pivot. Store them as a markdown appendix to the ADR.

## Dependencies

- Blocked by franken_numpy-azql (cross-engine benchmark) — can't define a "median ratio under M" criterion without measurements.

## Unit test requirements

1. **Parity debt scanner:** Test that the scanner correctly identifies fixtures with no corresponding test (use a mock fixture dir with 3 entries, 1 missing coverage). Test grouping by op-family.
2. **Threshold evaluator:** Given a mock cross-engine report and a threshold config, verify the evaluator correctly returns PIVOT or CONTINUE. Test edge cases: exactly at threshold, one workload exceeds R while median is under M.
3. **ADR template generation:** Verify the generated ADR contains all required sections (Status, Context, Decision, Criteria, Consequences).

## E2E requirements

The ADR must be reviewable by running:
```bash
cat docs/adr/ADR-001-parity-pivot.md
```
and seeing a complete, well-formatted document with real data (not placeholders) in the Context and Criteria sections. The Decision section must say "PROPOSED (awaiting user decision)" — never "ACCEPTED" without human sign-off.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Parity debt list produced, grouped by op-family, with rough effort estimates.
- [ ] Cross-engine gap summary consumed from azql output.
- [ ] Pivot threshold proposed with concrete numbers.
- [ ] ADR filed at docs/adr/ADR-001-parity-pivot.md with all sections filled (except Decision = PROPOSED).
- [ ] Draft beads for 4 disclaimed limitations included as ADR appendix.
- [ ] Unit tests for scanner, evaluator, and template pass.
- [ ] ADR clearly states that the user makes the final decision.
- [ ] No code changes outside docs/ and fnp-conformance.

## How this serves the over-arching goals

Prevents the failure mode where the swarm grinds parity debt indefinitely while never returning to the README's actual end-state vision. The README says "parity debt, not feature cuts" — but it ALSO says "future BLAS linkage is planned" and "[Python FFI] is planned but not implemented." Without an explicit pivot point, those items have zero gravitational pull on the work queue.
close_reason: ADR-001-parity-pivot.md created with full analysis. Pivot criteria are MET. Awaiting user decision to accept/defer/modify. Draft beads for 5 Phase3 work items included.

title: [perf][no-gaps] Zero-copy np.flatnonzero for bool/f64 — scan buffer to int64 indices
description: np.flatnonzero(mask) was 40x slower than numpy (60ms vs 1.5ms @4M, found via op-family sweep). FIX: try_zerocopy_flatnonzero scans the bool (uint8 view) or f64 buffer directly, two-pass count-then-fill into numpy.empty(count,int64). MONOMORPHIZED per dtype (first used &dyn Fn closure = virtual call/elem, 19ms; inlined = 12ms). Bit-exact (int64 indices; f64 excludes +-0.0, includes nan). Self-speedup 4.7x@1M (14.4->3.0ms), 4.9x@4M (60->12ms). Golden sha256==numpy. Other dtypes fall through. Lock flatnonzero_zerocopy_bool_f64_bit_exact_matches_numpy. NOTE: residual ~8x vs numpy is safe-Rust cell-scan ceiling (numpy SIMD-compacts); 4.9x self-win passes Score. CORRECTS prior memory claim that flatnonzero was at-parity/host-noise — it was a real 40x gap.

title: [HIGH] cumsum/ptp/convolution conformance harnesses compare NumPy to itself
description:
Reviewer pass on the latest fnp-python native-wrapper commits found that the new subprocess-based conformance harnesses do not reliably exercise fnp_python.

Evidence:
- crates/fnp-python/tests/conformance_cumsum.rs:116-123 builds the NumPy oracle script and the rust_script from the same `import numpy as np; np.cumsum(...)` body. Axis and dtype cases repeat the same pattern at lines 163-172, 202-212, 286-293, 371-379, and 407-415.
- crates/fnp-python/tests/conformance_ptp.rs:117-123 compares `np.ptp(...)` to the same `np.ptp(...)`; axis/dtype/nan cases repeat at lines 163-170, 199-209, 239-243, and 264-268.
- crates/fnp-python/tests/conformance_convolution.rs only tries `fnp.convolve` in the full-mode test, then swallows every import/runtime failure and prints NumPy fallback at lines 107-123. The same/same-valid/correlate tests never import fnp_python at all and just run NumPy on both sides (for example lines 155-163, 194-202, 238-246, 277-285, 316-324).

Why this is HIGH:
These tests were committed with native cumsum/cumprod/ptp/convolution coverage claims, but they can stay green if fnp_python is missing, if the native fast path is never called, or if the wrapper regresses. That creates a false green signal on exactly the recent parity surface being reviewed.

Reproduction:
1. Inspect the line ranges above; both commands passed to numpy_oracle are identical NumPy scripts, or the fnp_python path is wrapped in a catch-all fallback.
2. Temporarily break `fn cumsum`, `fn cumprod`, or `fn ptp` in crates/fnp-python/src/lib.rs, then run `cargo test -p fnp-python --test conformance_cumsum` or `--test conformance_ptp`. The harness still exercises NumPy-only scripts instead of the PyO3 module.

Expected fix:
Rewrite these harnesses to use the existing crates/fnp-python/tests/common/mod.rs helpers (`with_fnp_and_numpy`, `run_case`, `run_case_resolved`) or an equivalent embedded-PyO3 path that imports the in-process fnp_python module and compares it against NumPy without catch-all NumPy fallback. Add at least one negative-control assertion or structure that would fail if fnp_python.<function> is missing.
close_reason: Rewrote cumsum/cumprod, ptp, and convolution harnesses to use in-process fnp_python-vs-NumPy conformance helpers without NumPy-only fallback.

title: [perf][deep] SIMD-vectorize f64 unary kernel (fix/floor/ceil/trunc residual ~2.7x vs numpy)
description: FRONTIER after boundary-tax campaign (15 zero-copy levers shipped). zerocopy_f64_unary_flat (abs/floor/ceil/trunc/rint/sqrt/sign/...) uses a scalar cell loop: for (slot,cell) in output.zip(input) { slot.set(op.apply(cell.get())) }. The PyBuffer access is &[ReadOnlyCell<f64>]/&[Cell<f64>], which the autovectorizer cannot SIMD across (Cell opacity). Residual ~2.7x vs numpy's C SIMD on elementwise unary (also the axis-last residual in roll/diff/cumsum per-axis). BLOCKER: safe-Rust cannot get &mut[f64] from a numpy buffer without an unsafe transmute (AGENTS forbids hand-written unsafe in fnp-python), and a scratch Vec reintroduces the cold first-touch page-fault cost that IS the boundary tax. CANDIDATE: chunked std::simd (nightly portable_simd) — read LANES cells into a stack [f64;8], v.trunc() via StdFloat, scatter back; vectorizes the math, scalar gather/scatter stays. UNCERTAIN whether gather/scatter overhead eats the gain on bandwidth-adjacent ops. EXPERIMENT FIRST: microbench chunked-simd vs scalar-cell-loop for trunc on 4M f64 in an isolated module behind a feature flag; only ship if >=2x over current AND bit-exact (trunc/floor/ceil are exact; sqrt/recip need ULP check vs numpy). Target: 7.5ms->~3.5ms (numpy parity). Do NOT add #![feature(portable_simd)] to the multi-agent-hot lib.rs until proven in isolation.
notes: DIAGNOSED DEAD-END 2026-06-05: ran diagnostic (positive/copy vs floor/trunc/square on same 4M buffer) — fnp is ~7ms for ALL incl PURE COPY; the op compute adds ~0. Residual ~2.7x vs numpy is BUFFER MEMORY THROUGHPUT (cell loop ~9GB/s vs numpy ~19GB/s), NOT compute. SIMD-vectorizing the op does nothing. Faster cell load/store needs &mut[f64] = unsafe (forbidden in fnp-python). Genuinely blocked in safe Rust; the cell-access bandwidth is the ceiling. Closing the vectorize-compute premise.
close_reason: Wrong premise: residual is cell-access memory bandwidth (not op compute, which is free). Fixing needs unsafe &mut[f64] (forbidden). No safe-Rust lever.

title: [perf][no-gaps][SHIPPED] Francis double-shift implicit QR for eig — 5.5x QR iteration
description: SHIPPED 862bb15a. np.linalg.eigvals -> eig_nxn used single-shift QR (linear convergence on complex pairs, ~14x suboptimal, ad-hoc exceptional-shift hacks). Replaced with Francis DOUBLE-SHIFT implicit QR (Golub-VanLoan 7.5.1): shifts = trailing 2x2 eigenvalues as a conjugate pair in real arithmetic; form first column of H^2-sH+tI, introduce bulge, chase down subdiagonal with 3x3 then final 2x2 Householder reflectors; cubic convergence, handles complex pairs natively. Schur Z accumulation applies same reflectors; stagnation counter -> exceptional shift. Tolerance parity. Householder math: H=I-tau·v·v^T, v=(1,v1,v2), tau=(alpha-a0)/alpha, alpha=-sign(a0)·||.||. Ranges: left rows k..k+nr cols [max(lo,k-1),n); right cols k..k+nr rows [0,(k+nr+1).min(p)). LANDED FIRST TRY. Proof: same-process A/B (eigenvalues match <1e-6) 5.47x@256 5.64x@512; eig suite 27/27 + 268/268; eig(nonsym) n=512 3650->1876ms. eig overall ~1.9x (unblocked Hessenberg reduction now the bottleneck). NEXT: block Hessenberg reduction (dgehrd, two-sided like dsytrd) -> eig over 2x; then SVD bidiagonalization (but svd passes through to numpy). LESSON: hard classical algorithm (Francis) landed first try by carefully working out the math (tau, ranges) BEFORE coding.

title: Allow non-finite propagation in NxN solve/inv
description:
## Problem
`solve_nxn`, `solve_nxn_multi`, and `inv_nxn` currently fail closed because `lu_decompose` rejects any non-finite matrix entries. NumPy instead propagates or computes through many non-finite NxN solve/inv cases.

## Evidence
- `np.linalg.inv` and `np.linalg.solve` on 3x3 matrices with `NaN` or `inf` entries produce concrete outputs instead of raising
- off-diagonal `NaN` often contaminates whole results; diagonal `inf` can still yield finite sub-block outputs
- current Rust path errors before decomposition

## Goal
Match NumPy-style non-finite propagation for the LU-backed NxN inverse/solve paths without changing unrelated finite error handling.

## Files
- `crates/fnp-linalg/src/lib.rs`
close_reason: Completed

title: [PORT] fnp_python.random.BitGenerator abstract base class exposure
description:
numpy.random.BitGenerator is an abstract base that MT19937/PCG64/etc.
inherit from. Users write `isinstance(rng, np.random.BitGenerator)` to
test. fnp_python exposes the concrete bit generators but not the base.

Options:
1. Declare PyBitGenerator as a pyclass with no methods; make the 5
   concrete classes have it as their #[pyclass(extends = PyBitGenerator)].
2. Passthrough: just re-export numpy.random.BitGenerator so isinstance
   checks work against numpy's actual class.

Preferred: option 2, because numpy allows subclassing BitGenerator in
user code and the wire shape needs to match numpy's real class.

Success: `from fnp_python.random import BitGenerator` resolves, and
isinstance(fnp_python.random.PCG64(), numpy.random.BitGenerator) returns
True (re-export equivalence). Parity test gated on numpy_available.

Part of franken_numpy-lxmb.

title: perf(fnp-python): wire remaining native batched linalg (det/slogdet/cholesky/solve) instead of numpy delegation
description: FINDING (cc, 2026-06-08): fnp-linalg has 15 native batch_* functions (batch_inv/det/slogdet/cholesky/solve/svd/eig/eigh/qr/...), all now parallel across the stack (total-work gate, 9d4e10f7, 2.5-20x). But NONE were wired into the fnp-python np.linalg.* API — every batched (3-D+) call delegated to numpy (against no-gaps directive; parallel batch_* unreachable). batch_inv NOW WIRED (1b244e85): batched square real -> fnp_linalg::batch_inv, singular-lane fallback to numpy for exact LinAlgError. NEXT (each same pattern, low-risk STABLE ops): det (batch_det, scalar-per-lane out), slogdet (batch_slogdet, 2 outputs), cholesky (batch_cholesky, lower-tri; check upper kwarg), solve (batch_solve, A+B). AVOID svd/eig/eigh/qr batched-native (U/V/eigvec SIGN + eigenvalue ORDER ambiguities vs numpy = parity minefield; keep numpy delegation). Each wiring: gate shape.len()>=3 && square trailing && real f64; on Err fall back to numpy (exact error parity); existing batched conformance tests (det_and_inv_match.../cholesky.../slogdet batched) will exercise the native path vs numpy allclose. Verify tolerance + run linalg_error_surface_probe (flaky under PyO3 env — re-run on fail).
notes: PROGRESS (dbc39e01, cc): eigvalsh NOW WIRED native batched (symmetrize each lane from UPLO triangle -> parallel batch_eigvalsh; output batch_dims+[n]; Err->numpy). eigvalsh is wireable (eigenVALUES only, ascending order = no sign/order ambiguity, UNLIKE eig/eigh/svd/qr). Conformance eigvalsh_matches_numpy_across_uplo_batched_and_complex (both UPLO) green. STATUS: inv+det+cholesky+slogdet+eigvalsh DONE native-batched. solve STILL being done by BlackThrush (uncommitted in shared tree). Remaining-but-INTENTIONALLY-numpy-delegated: eig/eigh/svd/qr (eigenvector/U/V sign + general-eig order ambiguity vs numpy LAPACK). matrix_rank (SVD-based, singular values order-defined) + trace + matrix_norm could be wired too if desired (lower value: trace/norm are cheap reductions). Committed via isolated-hunk patch (line-range 15900-16000) excluding BlackThrush's solve hunk(15887)+test hunk(51490); verified HEAD compiles independently (stash+build); peer WIP restored.

Rejected the fnp-python batch_solve bridge on same-worker Criterion vmi1153651: vec 4.8904 ms baseline -> 4.8490 ms after, mat2 8.8336 ms baseline -> 9.4473 ms after. title: fnp-runtime golden tests: 43 tests locking decision system behavior
description:
## Finding

fnp-runtime had only inline unit tests but no comprehensive golden tests
to lock in the behavior of the runtime decision system. This system decides
whether operations are allowed, validated, or rejected based on compatibility
class and risk scores.

## Golden Test Categories Added (43 tests)

1. **RuntimeMode parsing** (4 tests) - from_wire, as_str, invalid inputs
2. **CompatibilityClass parsing** (5 tests) - all wire formats, fallback behavior
3. **decide_compatibility** (11 tests) - strict/hardened modes, risk thresholds, NaN/Inf handling
4. **decide_compatibility_from_wire** (4 tests) - wire parsing + decision
5. **posterior_incompatibility** (4 tests) - Bayesian posterior calculation
6. **expected_loss_for_action** (5 tests) - loss model calculations
7. **evaluate_policy_override** (8 tests) - policy evaluation, audit ref format
8. **DecisionAction/LossModel** (2 tests) - enum behavior, default values

## Why This Matters

The runtime decision system gates all compatibility decisions. Any accidental
change to decision logic would break downstream consumers. Golden tests ensure
deliberate review of any behavior changes.
close_reason: shipped

title: [perf] fnp-ufunc::fft — Cooley-Tukey 65536 at 2.51ms (26 M elem/s); twiddle reuse / iterative in-place candidate
description:
## Scenario
core_ops baseline, run tests/artifacts/perf/2026-06-01_perf_baseline. fft of 65536 complex samples.

## Baseline (criterion n=30)
- fft_65536: 2.509 ms  CI[2.506, 2.517], MAD 0.006 ms (26.1 M elem/s). Very low variance.

## Evidence
10_baseline.md row fft; criterion core_ops/fft_65536/new/estimates.json. Source: UFuncArray::fft (Cooley-Tukey + Bluestein) in fnp-ufunc. 65536 = 2^16 so the radix-2 Cooley-Tukey path is exercised (not Bluestein).

## Hypothesized fix
Flamegraph-confirm the hot recursion/butterfly first. Candidates: precompute/reuse twiddle-factor table across calls instead of recomputing; iterative in-place radix-2 with bit-reversal permutation (avoid per-stage allocation); possibly split-radix. Note: any change must preserve the bit-exact / numerical parity the conformance suite checks. Lower priority than the construction tax (franken_numpy-71n7p).

## Owner
Filed by BlackThrush profiling pass (measurement only).
close_reason: Profiled FFT radix-2 candidate and rejected it: rch baseline center 3.2021 ms, candidate center 5.2127 ms despite focused tests passing; no source change kept.

title: [perf][no-gaps] Fast-path Generator.normal identity loc/scale
description: Fresh rch fnp-random Criterion profile on vmi1149989: generator_normal/loc_scale/10000 is 140.21/146.81/154.68 us while generator_standard_normal/ziggurat/10000 is 78.335/81.477/85.920 us. Code path calls standard_normal(size) then maps every z through loc + scale*z. One lever: after the existing negative-scale guard, make the exact identity parameters loc == 0.0 and scale == 1.0 return standard_normal(size) directly. This preserves RNG draws, order, output bits, error behavior, tie behavior N/A, and avoids the second pass/allocation transform for the common standard-normal spelling through normal(). Acceptance: targeted rch Criterion baseline/rebench, golden SHA-256 proof against standard_normal, focused tests/check/clippy/fmt/UBS, Score >= 2.0, commit before/after numbers, close, push main + main:master.
close_reason: Measured no-ship. Fresh rch fnp-random profile on vmi1149989 ranked generator_normal/loc_scale/10000 at 140.21/146.81/154.68 us, with generator_standard_normal/ziggurat/10000 at 78.335/81.477/85.920 us. Targeted rch baseline on vmi1227854 for generator_normal/loc_scale/10000: 97.622/101.24/107.74 us. Candidate one-lever exact identity fast path for Generator::normal(loc=+0.0, scale=1.0) returned standard_normal(size) directly after the existing negative-scale guard; proof SHA-256 9a75af65b1c0e76aa894306af75cca230bf4eaa0df9ea80e42ab613498903d87 passed remotely on vmi1153651, preserving RNG stream, sample order, output bits, error behavior, and tie behavior N/A. Rebench could not confirm a win: vmi1156319 samples were 148.13/162.27/181.83 us and 147.76/150.98/153.62 us, both slower than the targeted baseline and not comparable as a win. Score < 2.0; source/proof/dependency trial removed; no optimization kept.

title: [perf][no-gaps][NEXT][BlackThrush] GEMM n>1024 beats reference BLAS to remove the C-BLAS delegation cap
description: PY_NATIVE_GEMM_MAX_DIM=1024 (fnp-python): np.matmul/dot above 1024 delegate to numpy C-BLAS because our packed GEMM loses ~1.3x at n=1280 (the flagship NO-GAPS gap). Needs an autotuned register-blocked microkernel / communication-avoiding GEMM (alien-artifact, bit-parity-preserving since numpy uses plain GEMM too). BlackThrush's reserved fnp-ufunc matmul_accumulate + fnp-linalg packed_gemm territory (they closed rejected complex-register-tile 2419566e). COORDINATION REQUIRED — cc-numpy not to solo-edit. Provide profile data: at n=1280 numpy ~32ms vs fnp ~41ms (per e6468d9c comment). On a host with OPTIMIZED BLAS the gap is far worse.
close_reason: Measured no-ship/re-routed after RCH cap refresh: native Rust GEMM baseline on ts1 = n512 2.545ms 105.46GF/s, n768 4.973ms 182.18GF/s, n1024 10.435ms 205.80GF/s, n1536 27.628ms 262.33GF/s, n2048 100.535ms 170.89GF/s; Python crossover parity=True but loses at n1280 59.00ms vs numpy 50.88ms and n2048 146.57ms vs numpy 107.86ms. Same-repo Strassen/Winograd already rejected in franken_numpy-strassen-sort-noship-ycnz5. Next non-micro unlock is policy bead franken_numpy-3q3pe (+fma with tolerance contract) or a disjoint profile-backed bead; no source retained for this cap pass.

title: [perf][no-gaps][SHIPPED] B-micropanel packing in matmul_accumulate_serial — 1.9-5.2x bit-exact GEMM
description: SHIPPED e509860c. The register-tiled GEMM read B with stride-n in the hot kk-loop; for large n the prefetcher cannot follow it and the [KxNR] strip is re-fetched from L3/DRAM per row block (single-thread 1.9 GFLOP/s @2048). Fix: reorder panel loops jc->j0->i0 and pack B[:,j0..j0+NR] into a contiguous kk-major scratch ONCE per NR column micropanel (amortized across all m/MR row tiles — THE difference from the earlier rejected per-row-block packing b945b945). Bit-identical (verbatim copy, same k-order): matmul golden + einsum golden unchanged, fnp-python matmul/tensordot/vdot conformance green. Same-process A/B (real kernel vs inline pre-pack loop, bitmatch=true): 512 1.89x, 1024 2.85x, 2048 5.22x. All consumers inherit (UFuncArray matmul/dot, einsum/tensordot routing, fnp-python np.matmul/dot route). NO FMA dependence — orthogonal to 3q3pe. NEXT: A-panel packing + pack-whole-nc-panel-once-per-jc (keeps A reuse across j0) could add more; or widen MR (register tile) now that B is contiguous. cc-numpy 2026-06-04.

title: [MEDIUM] fnp-conformance single-command fixture regeneration workflow missing
description:
per /testing-conformance-harnesses skill: 'Regenerate fixtures when reference impl updates; diff review'. Currently only one oracle-capture bin exists (capture_numpy_oracle.rs) which covers ufunc fixtures. There is no end-to-end script that regenerates all 45 fixture JSON files with a single command.

Impact: when a new numpy release lands (e.g. numpy 2.5), fixture regeneration requires manual orchestration across:
- datetime_* (3 files)
- dtype_promotion_cases
- fft_* (3 files)
- io_* (3 files)
- iter_* (3 files)
- linalg_* (3 files)
- masked_* (3 files)
- polynomial_* (3 files)
- rng_* (3 files)
- signal_* (3 files)
- shape_stride_cases
- string_* (3 files)
- ufunc_* (3 files)
- runtime_policy_cases (manual — policy test, no numpy dependency)
- workflow_scenarios (manual — orchestration test)

Required fix: create crates/fnp-conformance/src/bin/regenerate_all_fixtures.rs (or a Python script) that:
  1. Imports numpy + records version + git-ref
  2. For each domain, captures the expected oracle outputs
  3. Writes the updated JSON atomically (tempfile + rename)
  4. Emits a diff summary (cases added / removed / changed per domain)

CI integration: add a 'cargo run --bin regenerate_all_fixtures' check that runs on the release branch of numpy.

title: [perf][no-gaps] Zero-copy sequential np.prod reduction f64 — 6.5x ax0, beats numpy
description: np.prod had a native path (extract+reduce+build) 7.26x@ax0/2.9x@ax1/all slower than numpy (sum delegates to numpy=parity, prod did not). KEY: numpy.prod multiplies SEQUENTIALLY (verified bit-exact vs naive left-to-right, unlike pairwise sum), so a zero-copy sequential reduction is bit-identical. try_zerocopy_f64_prod: outer*axis_len*inner; inner==1 register-acc per lane, inner>1 slab-by-slab (cache-friendly); init 1.0 (=empty-axis identity, 1.0*x==x bit-exact preserves -0.0/inf/nan). axis=None/int, keepdims. Self-speedup ax0 6.46x (7.49->1.16ms, beats numpy 1.37), ax1 1.65x (4.95->2.99ms, numpy parity 3.16), all 1.56x (4.86->3.12ms, parity 3.05). ax1/all are at the dependent-multiply-chain floor=numpy. Golden sha256==numpy all axes/keepdims/extremes/empty(->1.0). Tuple axis/out/dtype/initial/where/other dtypes fall through. Lock prod_reduction_zerocopy_f64_bit_exact_matches_numpy. LESSON: numpy.prod=sequential (matchable), numpy.sum=pairwise (not; sum delegates).
close_reason: Shipped: try_zerocopy_f64_prod sequential reduction, ax0 6.46x (beats numpy)/ax1+all numpy parity, bit-exact golden, lock prod_reduction_zerocopy_f64_bit_exact_matches_numpy

title: [perf] zero-copy float32 np.around/round any-decimals (f32 218-278x over base, beats numpy)
close_reason: Shipped zero-copy float32 np.around/round (89041112): try_zerocopy_f32_around all decimals, covers around+round. KEY: numpy uses f=10^|d| and DIVIDES-FIRST for negative decimals (rint(a/f)*f) — naive a*0.001 diverges 73% at d=-3 in f32; divide-first bit-exact (0/8M prototype). d==0 plain rint loop; absurd |d| delegates to numpy. Score 218-278x over base, vsNP d!=0 beats numpy 2.2-2.4x, d=0 parity 1.09x. Golden 141f61da==numpy decimals -8..11 + ties/specials/absurd, 0/600. conformance_around(14)+rounding(22). f32 class: diff+clip+ediff1d+around done.

title: [perf][no-gaps] Zero-copy flat gather np.take(axis=None) f64 + int64 idx
description: np.take paid extract+build boundary tax (lglck), 7x slower than numpy at 4M. try_zerocopy_f64_take (axis=None, mode=raise): fused single-pass validate-and-gather over int64 index buffer into numpy.empty(indices.size), reshaped to indices.shape; numpy negative wraparound idx+=n; OOB bails (discards partial buffer) so numpy raises exact IndexError. Pure copy=bit-exact (incl -0.0/inf/nan). Per-axis take, non-int64 idx, OOB fall through. Self-speedup 23.3x@1M (29.41->1.26ms, numpy parity), 4.17x@4M (146.64->35.17ms). Fusing (no index Vec) was key: 1M 9.74->1.26ms. Golden sha256==numpy. Lock test take_zerocopy_f64_bit_exact_matches_numpy. Residual 1.45x vs numpy at 4M = random-gather memory latency (prefetch lever later).

title: perf(fnp-linalg): parallel row-fill Kronecker product (kron)
description: np.kron (kron_nxn) filled its O(m*n*p*q) output with a serial 4-nested loop. Each output row R=i*p+k is an independent function of a-row i and b-row k (result[R][j*q+l]=a[i*n+j]*b[k*q+l]); each cell is a single product (no accumulation) so filling the disjoint contiguous rows across rayon is bit-identical. Inner scaled copy of a contiguous b-row autovectorizes (vs old strided cell writes). Score 3.34x [64x64 kron 64x64] / 3.77x [256x256 kron 16x16] / 3.25x [16x16 kron 256x256]. Proof kron_parallel_matches_serial_reference_and_golden (to_bits vs serial ref across square/tall/wide incl NaN/inf/-0.0, sha256 6d16bc3a).
close_reason: Shipped: parallel row-fill kron, 3.34x/3.77x/3.25x, bit-exact golden 6d16bc3a.

title: Profile baseline: broadcast_add 22x slower than reduce_sum
description:
Applied /profiling-software-performance to establish baseline and identify hotspots.

BASELINE ESTABLISHED (2026-05-05):
| Operation | Size | Time | Throughput |
|-----------|------|------|------------|
| ufunc_add_broadcast | 1024x1024×1024 | 16.85ms | 62.3M elem/s |
| reduce_sum_axis1 | 1024x1024 | 766µs | 1.37B elem/s |
| matmul | 256x256² | 3.11ms | 10.7M FLOP/s |
| sort_quicksort | 1M | 4.85ms | 206M elem/s |
| fft | 65536 | 2.87ms | 22.8M elem/s |

PRIMARY HOTSPOT: ufunc_add_broadcast
- 22x slower than reduce_sum for same element count
- Throughput: 62M elem/s vs 1.37B elem/s
- Root cause hypothesis: chunked iteration overhead in tail_contiguous_broadcast_span path

HYPOTHESIS LEDGER:
- broadcast_shape_calc_per_element: REJECTS - shape calc is O(1) via aligned_broadcast_axis_steps
- chunked_iteration_overhead: SUPPORTS - chunks_exact + zip has allocation/bounds overhead
- simd_not_vectorizing: INVESTIGATE - need perf/samply to confirm

ARTIFACT: tests/artifacts/perf/baseline_2026-05-05.md

Next: Profile with samply to identify exact line, consider SIMD intrinsics for broadcast add fast path.
close_reason: Verified committed profiling baseline artifact and release p50/p95/p99 gate. rch exec -- cargo test -p fnp-conformance --test profiling_baseline --release -- --nocapture passed.

title: fnp-dtype benchmarks: 7 groups covering dtype ops baseline
description:
## Finding

fnp-dtype (3.2K LOC) had ZERO performance benchmarks despite being the dtype system
that underlies all array operations. These functions are called millions of times
in typical workloads.

## Benchmark Groups Added

1. **result_type** - dtype promotion (pairs, triples, 10-type combinations)
2. **can_cast** - casting rule checks (safe, unsafe, same_kind)
3. **min_scalar_type** - scalar type inference from values
4. **common_type** - common type finding for float types
5. **dtype_parse** - parsing dtype strings ("float64", "i8", etc.)
6. **array_storage_cast** - i32→f64, f64→i32 at 100/1K/10K elements
7. **array_storage_access** - get_f64/get_i128 sequential access

## Why This Matters

- result_type is called on every binary operation
- can_cast gates every astype() call
- array_storage_cast is the inner loop of dtype conversion
- Without baselines, we can't detect performance regressions
close_reason: shipped

title: [perf][no-gaps][SHIPPED] row-contiguous left apply in Hessenberg reduction — 7-9x bit-exact
description: SHIPPED 89cd2752. hessenberg_reduce (behind np.linalg.eigvals/eig) left Householder apply H:=P·H used per-column walk h[i*n+col] (stride-n down rows) = cache-thrash, dominant cost of the reduction (and of eig after the Francis double-shift). Right apply + Q were already row-contiguous; left was the lone stride-n loop (tridiag already had the contiguous two-pass form — brought Hessenberg to parity). Rewrote as two row-contiguous passes (d[col]=Σv[i]h[i][col], then (scale·d[col])·v[i]). BIT-IDENTICAL (assert maxd==0.0). Proof: same-process A/B 9.03x@512 7.00x@1024, suite 268/268, hessenberg 512 899->100ms. eig now ~3.4x over eig-campaign start. ALMOST OVER-ENGINEERED: was about to implement the hard blocked dgehrd (dlahr2) when the real issue was the SAME stride-n pathology (now found in 4 places: GEMM, eigenvector accumulation, and Hessenberg left apply; cf tridiag/QR Householder audits). LESSON CONFIRMED: before a hard blocked rewrite, scan for stride-n column-walk loops arr[i*n+col] — transpose/two-pass to row-contiguous, bit-exact, often 7-60x. NEXT: profile remaining slow reachable ops; eig fully optimized (double-shift + contiguous Hessenberg); svd passes through to numpy (no reach).

description: take/flip/flipud/fliplr use extract_numeric_array (canonicalizes to i64/u64/f64) instead of extract_precise_numeric_array, so np.take(int8_arr,...) returns int64 where numpy returns int8; same for float32->float64. Sibling reorder ops roll/repeat preserve dtype correctly. These are pure reorder/select ops so dtype must pass through unchanged. Fix: route through precise extraction (or fall back to numpy for non-f64/i64/u64 widths) + golden parity test across all widths. Found alongside 7pfzz.
close_reason: Shipped 8cc489b6. Tightened take/flip/flipud/fliplr native-path gate to bool+8-byte int/uint/float (widths where extract_numeric_array widening is dtype-exact); narrower numeric + float128 now delegate to numpy preserving dtype. Verified all 12 numeric widths x 4 funcs vs numpy 2.4.3. Lock test take_and_flip_preserve_numeric_dtype_like_numpy.

title: numpy.ma: masked_inside python wrapper parity
