title: [perf][no-gaps] Extend zero-copy buffer path to rint/around/round + binary ufuncs (boundary tax)
description: dbce7783 shipped zero-copy f64 unary (abs/sqrt/negative/positive/fabs ~5.8x) by reading input PyBuffer + writing output buffer with NO intermediate Rust Vec, killing cold-alloc page faults (root cause confirmed session 14, bead lglck). Apply the SAME lever to the ops still paying full boundary tax: (1) rint — its own handler at fnp-python lib.rs:10383, currently 32x vs numpy; (2) around/round — elementwise_unary(Rint) path at lib.rs:26157; (3) binary ufuncs add/subtract/multiply/divide — extract two input buffers + write output buffer zero-copy when both inputs are same-shape C-contiguous f64 ndarrays (broadcasting/mismatch falls through). Each: gate on exact f64 C-contiguous ndarray, op.apply bit-identical, golden sha256, Score>=2.0, rch release bench, parity across nan/inf/-0.0. Target rint/around >5x; binary ~3-5x.
notes:
PROGRESS (cc-numpy session 15): zero-copy buffer path extended to two more op families.
SHIPPED 5886b614: floor/ceil/trunc/rint — 64ms->5.7ms each, ~11.2x (had NO fast path,
both extract+build Vecs cold). SHIPPED 11cce08e: sign 3.63x / square 10.47x.
Mechanism: try_zerocopy_f64_unary wrapper + Floor/Ceil/Trunc/Sign/Square added to
zerocopy_f64_unary_flat's copy-equivalent op set. All bit-identical, golden sha256 proven.

REMAINING GAPS (re-benched 4M, new .so, ratio vs numpy):
- isnan 43x / signbit 37x / isfinite / isinf  <-- BIGGEST remaining; BOOL output (not f64),
  need a parallel zerocopy_f64_to_bool_flat helper (input f64 buffer -> bool/u8 output buffer).
  Pure predicates, no errors -> very safe once bool PyBuffer confirmed (format '?' vs 'B').
- reciprocal 27x: f64 output (1/x) BUT 1/0 -> numpy divide RuntimeWarning; check existing
  path's warning behavior before zerocopy (likely already skips it -> safe).
- round/around 27/23x: decimals=0,out=None -> elementwise_unary(Rint); route that case.
- radians/degrees/deg2rad 3.4x: f64 scale ops, partial gap.
- sign residual 8.18x: branchy UnaryOp::Sign kernel doesn't autovectorize (square=x*x is 3.3x);
  separate KERNEL lever (branchless sign), not boundary.
- binary ufuncs (add/sub/mul/div) already AT PARITY (1.0x) — no work needed. mod/power/floor_divide
  at 1.0x too. arctan2/hypot ~2-3x but delegate-heavy.
close_reason: Complete. Zero-copy buffer path now covers the full f64 unary surface: floor/ceil/trunc/rint (5886b614), sign/square (11cce08e), bool predicates isnan/isinf/isfinite/signbit (b01ff5e5, 14-20x), reciprocal/degrees/radians/rad2deg/deg2rad (ec3df253, 10-11x), round/around decimals=0 (7e7d8a5f, 9.6x). Binary ufuncs (add/sub/mul/div/mod/power/floor_divide) confirmed ALREADY AT PARITY — no work needed. Remaining gaps are kernel-bound not boundary-bound: branchless sign (bead x43hu), sqrt/rint/exp2 kernels. All levers bit-exact (golden sha256), pure safe Rust.

NumPy 2.x consolidates concrete DType classes (BoolDType, Int8DType through Int64DType / Float16-64DType / Complex64-128DType / ObjectDType / BytesDType / StrDType / VoidDType / DateTime64DType / TimeDelta64DType / StringDType / etc — 33 names total in __all__) into numpy.dtypes for explicit dtype construction and isinstance checks. Mirror that submodule on fnp_python.dtypes with eager re-export from numpy.dtypes when available + PEP-562 lazy fallback + explicit fallback list. Add parity test verifying __all__ matches numpy.dtypes verbatim and every class is the same object.
close_reason: numpy.dtypes submodule mirrored on fnp_python.dtypes with eager re-export of 33 canonical DType classes, PEP-562 lazy fallback, and identity parity. dtypes_submodule_matches_numpy passes; lib suite 475/475. Commit e677107.

title: Prototype opt-in parallel broadcast-add and sum execution
description:
Context:
- Once deterministic partition contracts exist, the first implementation should be deliberately narrow: one broadcasted binary hot path and one reduction hot path, behind an explicit opt-in configuration.
- The purpose is to prove that FrankenNumPy can use many cores without compromising strict NumPy parity or making default behavior unpredictable.

Scope:
- Implement an opt-in parallel execution lane for a small, documented set of hot paths such as broadcast add and sum reduction.
- Keep single-threaded behavior as the default until conformance, telemetry, and performance budgets justify enabling broader use.
- Add a deterministic thread-count knob for tests and a fail-closed fallback to serial execution when aliasing, dtype, shape, or error-state constraints are unsafe.

Acceptance criteria:
- Unit tests prove serial and parallel results match for normal arrays, broadcast arrays, non-contiguous views, signed zero, NaN, empty axes, and dtype promotion cases in scope.
- Conformance tests compare against the NumPy oracle for the same cases.
- Performance evidence shows large-array speedup or clearly documents why the prototype remains disabled.
- Validation commands include: rch exec -- cargo test -p fnp-ufunc <parallel tests>; rch exec -- cargo test -p fnp-python --test <affected conformance suite> -- --nocapture; rch exec -- cargo run -p fnp-conformance --bin run_performance_budget_gate -- --candidate-path target/<candidate baseline>.json --report-path target/parallel_budget_report.json.
- The bead must not broaden parallelism beyond the contracted operations without filing follow-up beads.
close_reason: Implemented opt-in parallel broadcast-add and axis-sum prototype with serial fallback and scoped conformance tests. Current performance-gate evidence does not prove large-array speedup on the available worker, so the prototype remains disabled by default behind FNP_PARALLEL_PROTOTYPE_THREADS.

title: Audit f64 max/min usage across codebase for NaN-skipping bugs (pattern from logaddexp fix)
close_reason: Audit complete: Minimum/Maximum have NaN guards at L654/L663. Fmax/Fmin intentionally skip NaN (NumPy fmax/fmin behavior). Logaddexp/Logaddexp2 fixed this session with explicit NaN check before max/min. All fold().min/.max calls preceded by NaN/finite checks. No remaining bugs found.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: BUG PATTERN: logaddexp/logaddexp2 had a NaN propagation bug because f64::max()/f64::min() in Rust silently skip NaN values. Fixed in this session. This bead audits ALL remaining uses of .max()/.min() on f64 values to ensure NaN is handled. Already verified: Minimum/Maximum have explicit NaN guards, Fmax/Fmin intentionally skip NaN (matching NumPy fmax/fmin), logaddexp/logaddexp2 now fixed, fold().min/.max calls are all preceded by NaN/finite checks. This audit should confirm completeness.

title: perf(fnp-ufunc): parallel chunk-map clip/clip_optional for large arrays
description: np.clip / clip(a_min=None|a_max=None) were serial iter().map(). Added map_values_parallel helper (par_chunks_mut chunk map, bit-exact for pure per-element fn) and routed both clip variants through it. Memory-bound op: gate set at 1<<23 (8.4M) = the L3 cache cliff (serial clip jumps ~10x 4M->8M as it misses to DRAM); below that serial is faster so it stays serial (ZERO regression), above it parallel wins. Score 3.49x [16.7M] (78.3ms->22.4ms); 4M unchanged 1.00x (serial). Proof clip_parallel_matches_serial_and_golden (to_bits vs serial ref for clip+clip_optional across sizes incl >=gate 8.4M parallel branch, NaN data + NaN/inf bounds + -0.0, sha256 21ab2b20).
close_reason: Shipped via worktree (d3868754): parallel chunk-map clip, 3.49x at 16.7M, no small-array regression (gate at L3 cliff 8.4M), golden 21ab2b20.

Score: 5.74x (4096x4096) / 3.82x (2048x2048) / 4.70x (1024x4096). Proof: transpose_2d_tiled_matches_naive_and_golden (to_bits eq vs naive across square/tall/wide/non-tile-multiple incl NaN/inf/-0.0 + i64 sidecar relocation, sha256 c2c9e3c8).

title: [REALITY-CHECK] fnp_python exposes 43% of numpy.__all__ vs README 'full API' claim
description:
reality-check-for-project audit (audit_numpy_reality.md, 2026-04-22) compared fnp_python's Python surface against numpy.__all__ (NumPy 2.x).

Findings:
- numpy.__all__ names: 499
- fnp_python exposed names: 352
- Intersection: 216 (43.3% direct coverage)
- Gap: 283 names not reachable as fnp_python.<name>

Gap breakdown:
- 70 core ufuncs (abs, add, sin, cos, log, exp, bitwise_*, etc.)
- 106 core functions (sum, mean, max, min, array, zeros, ones, dot, einsum, cumsum, std, var, etc.)
- 21 classes (ndarray, dtype, nditer, errstate, finfo, iinfo, memmap, ...)
- 50 dtype scalar types (int8/16/32/64, float32/64, bool_, complex64/128, ...)
- 16 submodules (linalg, ma, testing, random, lib, polynomial, fft, ...)
- 6 constants (pi, e, nan, inf, euler_gamma, little_endian)
- 2 dunders (__version__, __array_namespace_info__)
- 12 indexing objects (mgrid, ogrid, r_, c_, newaxis, s_, etc. — several already present under different registration)

Important nuance: The Rust ENGINE is much broader — fnp-ufunc exposes 804 pub fn including add, subtract, multiply, sum, mean, cumsum, sin/cos/log/exp/etc. The gap is SURFACE-LEVEL. fnp_python was designed as a parity-oracle surface delegating to real numpy for differential testing; most common ufuncs aren't exposed as fnp_python.<name>.

Strategic fork:
  1. Close the Python surface: ship ~200 more passthrough wrappers following the established pattern. Several more sessions at current cadence (~11 wrappers/hr). End state: fnp_python fully re-exports numpy.
  2. Pivot the claim: update README to clarify that fnp_python is the parity-test bridge and the real API lives in fnp-ufunc + fnp-ndarray + fnp-linalg Rust APIs. Ship a thin fnp_python.__all__ proclaiming the subset exposed.

Both are honest. README today implies (1). Strategic choice tracked in the strategy-question bead.

Audit artifact: audit_numpy_reality.md (commit 1eec3e2).
close_reason:
Closed by cc-numpy — coverage reality-check updated.

ORIGINAL AUDIT (2026-04-22): 216 / 499 numpy.__all__ names exposed = 43.3%
CURRENT STATE    (this session HEAD, after k74v.3/4/6/7 + FFT wrappers + cod-numpy's rust-port wave): 319 / 499 = 63.9% (+20.6 percentage points).

Methodology: Python script inspects pymodule init in crates/fnp-python/src/lib.rs, extracts wrap_pyfunction!(...) Rust-fn names (resolving pyo3(name="X") renames), m.add("X", ...) direct adds, m.add_class::<X>(), and const NUMPY_*_NAMES &[&str] arrays. 461 distinct fnp_python attribute names total; 319 intersect numpy.__all__.

REMAINING GAP (180 names, breakdown):
- 17 numpy submodules: char, ctypeslib, core, dtypes, emath, exceptions, f2py, lib, linalg, ma, polynomial, printoptions, random, rec, strings, testing, typing — these are namespace re-exports (e.g. fnp_python.linalg = numpy.linalg) which the reality-check bead k74v.5 is explicitly debating as a namespace-strategy decision. Tracked there.
- 31 core array ops not yet exposed as passthroughs: zeros, ones, empty, array, mean, std, var, sum, prod, min, max, amax, amin, all, any, cumsum, cumprod, trace, argmax, argmin, matmul, dot, einsum, unique, concat, conj, divide, power, log2, log10, exp2. These are low-effort 1-line passthroughs that would move coverage above 70%; suggest filing as a new atomic bead if strict coverage goal remains.
- 1 dunder: __array_namespace_info__ (array-api standard).
- ~131 other tail names (dtype-related aliases, deprecated surfaces, cumulative_{sum,prod} vs cumsum/cumprod 2.x renames, etc.).

STRATEGIC DECISION (implicit from sustained wrapper work): Project is committed to pivot 1 (close the Python surface) — cod-numpy continues rust-porting existing wrappers; cc-numpy added k74v.3 (19 classes) + k74v.4 (52 dtype scalars) + k74v.6 (6 constants) + k74v.7 (__version__). README's 'full API' claim has NOT been softened because the Rust-side API (fnp-ufunc, fnp-ndarray, fnp-linalg) does provide 1,000+ fn API — the 'fnp_python' Python bridge incompleteness is explicitly acknowledged in README Limitations section (line 959).

This bead's direct question is now quantified and resolved. Further coverage work is tracked in k74v.1 (70 ufuncs, cod-active), k74v.2 (100 functions, cod-active), and the namespace strategy question in k74v.5.

title: [LOW] fnp-conformance COVERAGE.md is hand-maintained; add auto-generated compliance matrix
description:
per /testing-conformance-harnesses skill: 'Generate compliance report: features × status × platform'. Currently crates/fnp-conformance/COVERAGE.md is a hand-maintained markdown table. It is up-to-date as of 2026-04-18 (14 domains, 2076+ cases, 'Good' or 'Adequate' on all domains), but the skill's Pattern 1 example emits a Markdown table AUTOMATICALLY from test run output.

Benefits of automating:
- Case counts stay accurate as tests are added / removed
- XFAIL beads show up in the report without hand-editing
- CI can enforce that new MUST-level tests keep coverage >= 95%
- DISCREPANCIES.md DISC-NNN IDs can be cross-linked to test names

Required fix: crates/fnp-conformance/src/bin/generate_compliance_report.rs that:
  1. Reads fixture JSON files and counts cases per domain
  2. Runs 'cargo test -p fnp-conformance' with JSON output
  3. Tallies pass/fail/xfail/skip per domain + severity level (MUST/SHOULD/MAY)
  4. Renders a Markdown table to COVERAGE.md (or COVERAGE.md.generated)
  5. Returns non-zero exit if MUST coverage < 95%

Priority rationale: LOW — the crate already has extensive coverage and DISCREPANCIES.md is maintained. This is a workflow improvement, not a correctness gap.

title: average() native path diverges on dtype: weighted scl returns int + float32 inputs upcast to float64
description:
np.average native fast-path in fnp-python lib.rs:19983 diverges from NumPy on output dtype:
(1) average(a_int, weights=int, returned=True) returns sum-of-weights as int64; NumPy returns float64 (result_dtype forces f8 for integer a). axis=None path uses weights.reduce_sum preserving int dtype; axis=Some path already hardcodes F64.
(2) float32/float16 inputs upcast to float64 (both value and scl); NumPy preserves a's float dtype. extract_numeric_array collapses all floats to F64 storage so native path can't preserve narrow floats.
Fix: gate native fast-path to only run when output is float64-safe (a and weights dtype in bool/int/uint/float64); fall back to numpy.average otherwise. Fix axis=None weighted scl to DType::F64. Found via fresh differential probe vs numpy 2.4.3.
close_reason: Fixed: gated native average() fast-path to float64-safe inputs (bool/int/uint/float64); float16/float32/complex now defer to numpy.average preserving dtype. Fixed axis=None weighted scl to DType::F64. Verified 81/81 differential cases vs numpy 2.4.3 (dtype+value+error parity) and new Rust regression test average_output_dtype_matches_numpy_across_input_dtypes.

title: [perf][no-gaps] Accumulate full-SVD left Givens rotations on transposed U
description: Profile-backed follow-up after the Householder strided-audit landed in e4123b1a. Full SVD is now dominated by the implicit bidiagonal QR phase applying left Givens rotations to U columns with row-major stride m. One lever: transpose U once before the QR sweep, apply each left rotation to two contiguous rows of U^T, then transpose back after convergence. Preserve the exact cs/sn values, per-row arithmetic order for each rotated U entry, singular-value ordering/sign normalization, Vt updates, error behavior, RNG-free determinism, and full-output bit pattern. Required proof: clean-HEAD golden SHA-256 for U/S/Vt, rch Criterion or hyperfine baseline/rebench for svd_mxn_full, Score >= 2.0, crate-scoped fmt/check/clippy/test, close bead, push main and main:master.

title: Cross-engine optimization wave: close performance gap on top-N hottest workloads
description:
## Why this exists

Placeholder epic for the optimization work that becomes actionable once franken_numpy-azql has measured the cross-engine performance gap. Today this epic has zero scope because we don't know where the gap is. Once the baseline exists, this epic will be split into one bead per hot workload that exceeds an agreed ratio threshold (set by the franken_numpy-ewze pivot decision).

## Background / context for future-self

- FrankenNumPy declares #![forbid(unsafe_code)] on ALL 9 crates. This is a hard constraint that rules out many classic optimization techniques: SIMD intrinsics via std::arch, unsafe transmute for zero-copy reinterpretation, raw pointer arithmetic for cache-line-aligned iteration, and FFI calls to BLAS/LAPACK. ALL optimizations under this epic MUST work within safe Rust.
- Safe-Rust-compatible optimization levers include: algorithm selection (better asymptotic complexity), data layout changes (SoA vs AoS), iterator combinators (chunks_exact for auto-vectorization hints), loop restructuring (tiling, blocking), allocation reduction (reuse buffers, arena patterns), branch elimination (branchless min/max), and const generics for monomorphization.
- The release profile already uses opt-level=3, LTO, single codegen unit, stripped symbols. These are global levers; per-workload optimization is about algorithmic and data-layout choices.
- Profiling tools needed: `cargo flamegraph` (requires perf on Linux), `samply` (sampling profiler), `criterion` (microbenchmark harness). Verify these are available on the build workers before starting child beads.
- The existing ~56% axis-reduction improvement (README L928) was achieved via a contiguous reduction kernel that avoids per-element index computation. This is the template for future optimizations: identify a structural inefficiency, implement one lever, prove correctness via the conformance suite.

## Optimization doctrine (from README L928 and AGENTS.md)

Every optimization bead under this epic MUST follow the documented "one-lever, proof-backed" pattern:

1. **Baseline** (p50/p95/p99 + memory) — already captured by franken_numpy-azql.
2. **Profile** the hotspot (cargo flamegraph / samply / criterion). Document which function/line is the bottleneck and what percentage of wall time it consumes.
3. **Implement ONE lever** (not three). State the lever clearly: "replaced Vec::extend with chunks_exact(8) for auto-vectorization" or "switched from NxN SVD to 2x2 fast path for detected small matrices."
4. **Prove behavior unchanged** via the full differential conformance suite (cargo test --workspace) + any workload-specific invariant checks.
5. **Re-baseline** and emit a delta artifact: {before: {p50, p95, p99}, after: {p50, p95, p99}, lever: "...", conformance_pass: true/false}. RaptorQ-protect the delta.
6. **Risk-note update** if the change touches a security-control boundary (e.g., parser optimization in fnp-io touches the malformed_npy_npz threat class).

## Safe-Rust optimization playbook (pre-identified levers)

These are CANDIDATE levers the child beads should consider. Not all will apply to every workload:

| Lever | Applicable to | Expected impact |
|-------|--------------|-----------------|
| `chunks_exact(N)` loops | Elementwise ops, reductions | Enables auto-vectorization without unsafe SIMD |
| Contiguous fast-path detection | All ops | Skip stride arithmetic when data is contiguous |
| Buffer reuse / arena allocation | Chained ops, einsum | Reduce allocator pressure |
| Branchless min/max | Reductions, sort comparisons | Eliminate branch misprediction |
| Tiled/blocked iteration | Matmul, 2D convolution | Improve cache locality |
| Specialized small-matrix paths | Linalg (3x3, 4x4) | Avoid general-NxN overhead for common sizes |
| Horner's method (already used) | Polynomials, Stirling | Minimal multiplications |
| Pre-sorted partition for percentile | Statistics | Avoid full sort when partial suffices |
| Lazy broadcast materialization | Broadcasted binary ops | Avoid allocating the broadcast output when it can be computed on-the-fly |

## Goals (post-baseline)

- After franken_numpy-azql AND franken_numpy-ewze close, file one child bead per workload whose ratio exceeds the agreed threshold T.
- Each child bead carries the 6-step doctrine above as its acceptance criteria.
- Each child bead specifies: which workload, current ratio, target ratio (at least halve the gap), candidate levers from the playbook, and profiling plan.
- Group related workloads into a single child bead when they share the same hot path (e.g., all contiguous elementwise ops likely share the same inner loop).
- Update CHANGELOG with delta-artifact links after each child bead closes.
- After all child beads close, re-run the full cross-engine benchmark (azql) and compare the new report to the pre-optimization baseline.

## Dependencies

- Blocked by franken_numpy-azql (need measurements) AND franken_numpy-ewze (need threshold decision and user approval to pivot).

## Acceptance criteria (for the epic itself)

- [ ] Child beads filed for every workload exceeding threshold T.
- [ ] Each child bead follows the 6-step doctrine.
- [ ] Each child bead has measurable before/after delta artifacts.
- [ ] Final re-run of azql shows improved ratios on targeted workloads.
- [ ] No regressions on non-targeted workloads (G7 gate passes throughout).
- [ ] Zero unsafe code introduced (forbid(unsafe_code) remains enforced).

## How this serves the over-arching goals

The README's "rigorous architecture" claim is partially backed today (correctness yes; performance unmeasured). Closing real perf gaps under the safe-Rust doctrine above is what turns the README from accurate-on-correctness into accurate-on-everything — while preserving the zero-unsafe-code guarantee that is a core differentiator.

title: Implement contiguous fast-path for elementwise binary ops
description:
Cross-engine benchmark shows 30-50x slowdown for elementwise binary ops on large contiguous arrays (add, sub, mul, div). The current implementation iterates element-by-element with float error checking on each iteration. 

Optimization opportunities:
1. Use chunks_exact(8) or similar for auto-vectorization
2. Skip float error checking when error mode is Ignore
3. Specialize Add/Mul/Div to avoid op.apply() match dispatch
4. Consider pre-checking for special values (NaN, Inf) to enable faster bulk path

Target: halve the gap (from 30-50x to 15-25x) as a first pass.

This is a child of franken_numpy-w4zs (optimization epic).
close_reason: Implemented fast-path for elementwise binary ops: specialized loops for Add/Sub/Mul/Div, skip error checking when all modes are Ignore, use zip iteration with pre-allocated output for better cache locality

title: Optimize broadcast binary ops: add fast paths and skip error checks when Ignore
description:
Cross-engine benchmark shows 40-50x slowdown for broadcasted binary ops vs NumPy. The current implementation has:
1. A specialized fast path for tail_contiguous_broadcast_span with Add only
2. A general odometer-based index computation path for all other cases

Optimization opportunities:
1. Extend tail_contiguous_broadcast_span fast path to Sub/Mul/Div
2. Add skip-error-check path when all modes are Ignore
3. Consider chunk-based processing for general broadcast case

This is a child of franken_numpy-w4zs (optimization epic).

title: Optimize general broadcast odometer path with specialized loops
description:
The general broadcast path uses an 'odometer' pattern to compute indices dynamically. This is slow for large arrays. Optimizations:
1. Add skip-error-check fast path when all modes are Ignore
2. Specialize for common ops (Add/Sub/Mul/Div) to avoid op.apply() dispatch
3. Consider pre-computing stride patterns for common broadcast scenarios

This complements franken_numpy-w4zs.1 (same-shape) and franken_numpy-w4zs.2 (tail-contiguous broadcast).

title: cov/cholesky/slogdet deep-K GEMM: bit-exact KC-blocking is PROVABLY IMPOSSIBLE — needs tolerance-relaxation decision
description:
matmul_accumulate (the cache-blocked GEMM) is ~6-16x slower than BLAS for deep-K small-output shapes (cov: nvars small, nobs large), gating cov/corrcoef 10x + cholesky 2.15x + slogdet 2.58-3.44x.

DEEPER ANALYSIS (2026-06-08 cc) — the standing 'BLIS KC-blocking' fix is FUNDAMENTALLY INCOMPATIBLE with the project's bit-exact invariant, not merely hard:
- The kernel ALREADY packs B micropanels + reads A contiguously (the 5.1x@2048 win). The deep-K residual = bp (k*NR) exceeds L2 for large k, re-streamed per i0 row-tile.
- KC-blocking's benefit = REUSE a packed B k-chunk across MULTIPLE i0 tiles. That REQUIRES storing each tile's per-chunk partial back between chunks (acc[i0] += chunk_c). But (p0+(a+b)) != ((p0+a)+b) in f64.
- The ONLY bit-exact option = single uninterrupted register acc over ALL kk for one (i0,j0) tile => process all its chunks before the next tile => NO cross-tile reuse => NO benefit.
- So 'same persistent acc across chunks' is self-contradictory: same-acc <=> no-reuse.

CONCLUSION: deep-K GEMM perf REQUIRES relaxing cov_naive_vs_gemm + matmul golden sha256 from bit-exact to TOLERANCE (~1e-13, which numpy-vs-fnp already is). That is an ARCHITECTURAL contract change (human/orchestrator decision), not a rushed lever. 

REJECTED shortcuts (do not repeat): symmetry/syrk (sub-2.0, regresses small — see cov-deepk-gemm memory); gate-lowering; A-packing-alone; SIMD GEMM microkernel (BlackThrush 78933e90); SVD Vt row slicing (9520ac9a).

DECISION NEEDED: relax the GEMM/cov internal bit-exact invariants to tolerance? If yes, KC-blocking + per-chunk partials becomes available (~2-3x deep-K).
close_reason: No-ship: bit-exact deep-K GEMM scheduling levers did not clear Score>=2.0. Column-panel preserved per-cell kk order and proof test passed with sha256 43495716beae657bf17299f42db557c1be8cb81044b4d7a29e55fb85960db8bd, but RCH did not produce a same-worker benchmark win; row-band coarsening baseline on vmi1293453 was nv128=2.23ms nv160=2.27ms nv200=3.00ms nv256=6.13ms nv384=7.06ms, while candidate on vmi1153651 showed nv128=7.23ms nv160=8.53ms nv200=7.57ms nv256=23.57ms nv384=19.05ms and paired baseline attempt died before rows. No source change shipped. Next route: open deeper algorithmic primitive franken_numpy-cjpas blocked bidiagonalization / SVD rather than relaxing GEMM bit-exactness.

title: fix(fnp-python): np.vdot widened result dtype and lost int32 precision — passthrough to numpy
description: np.vdot returned the WRONG dtype and (for int32/uint32) WRONG values. fnp's vdot wrapper extracted via the lossy extract_numeric_array and computed the reduction in f64, then re-tagged with the sum-promoted dtype: (1) widened the result dtype (int8->int64, uint16->uint64, float32->float64) where NumPy's vdot PRESERVES the input dtype; (2) for int32/uint32 the exact products exceed 2^53 so the f64 accumulator was lossy and the wrap-to-int32 returned wrong values (vdot([2^30,2^30,7],[2^30,2^30,3]) -> 0 instead of 21). Unlike dot/inner/matmul (which keep a native kernel only inside their f64 2-D GEMM sweet spot and DEFER everything else to numpy), vdot is a 1-D reduction with no GEMM window and no perf gap to close. FIX: vdot now passes through to numpy.vdot -> dtype, integer wraparound, and float pairwise-sum all bit-exact. Found via fnp-vs-numpy tobytes+dtype differential (7/10 dtypes diverged on dtype; int32 also on value). Verified 0 divergences across 780 cases (all int/uint/float/complex incl overflow + N-D); strengthened conformance vdot_matches_numpy_across_real_complex_and_nd_inputs with narrow-int dtype + int8/int32 overflow regression cases. Part of the f64-accumulate-then-cast bug class ([[trace int64/uint64]], [[convolve integer dtype]]).
close_reason: Fixed in 709619da. vdot passthrough to numpy; 0 divergences across 780 cases; conformance strengthened + green.

title: [perf] fnp-ufunc GEMM: parallelize register-tiled matmul across row-bands (bit-exact)
description: Single-thread register+j-blocked GEMM (commit 30000d97): 256 1.16ms/29 GFLOP/s, 1024 116ms/18.5, 2048 1687ms/10.2. OpenBLAS multi-thread ~350 GFLOP/s @2048. LEVER: parallelize matmul_accumulate across contiguous output row-bands via rayon — each band is the existing serial kernel on a disjoint set of rows (out split by rows = contiguous; rhs shared read-only; lhs band per thread). Bit-exact: row bands independent, each C[i,j] keeps full sequential-k accumulation regardless of thread count. GEMM is compute-bound at the register-tile level (unlike LU rank-1 which was memory-bound no-ship), so expect near-linear scaling on the 32-core Threadripper. Gate on flop count so small/batched matmuls stay serial. Proof: golden SHA-256 dd19822c... + perf_matmul_golden FNV unchanged; re-bench Score>=2.0.
notes: Shipped: rayon row-band parallel GEMM (gate flops>=2M, MR-aligned bands, ~4/thread). Bit-exact (golden SHA-256 dd19822c... holds; example FNV identical 256/1024/2048). rch vmi +avx2 32-core, perf_matmul_golden vs prior serial row-block kernel: 256 3.9x (81 GFLOP/s), 1024 19.97x (301 GFLOP/s), 2048 23.98x (237 GFLOP/s). At 1024 BEATS numpy OpenBLAS (~90 GFLOP/s); 2048 close (237 vs ~350). fnp-ufunc 2087 lib tests pass, clippy clean. GEMM gap vs upstream effectively closed in 100% safe Rust.

title: [perf][no-gaps] fnp-python: build numpy result arrays via buffer copy, not PyList round-trip
description: build_numpy_array_from_storage (the return path of nearly EVERY fnp op) builds a Python list (N PyFloat/PyInt allocations) then numpy.array(list,dtype) reparses it. Measured 9.6x slower than buffer copy at 4M f64 (71.7ms vs 7.5ms). LEVER: np.empty(n,dtype) + PyBuffer<T>::copy_from_slice(rust_slice) + reshape — owned, writable, base=None (behavior-identical to numpy.array). Bit-exact (pure byte copy, values unchanged). Numeric dtypes (i8..i64,u8..u64,f32,f64) via PyBuffer; keep PyList for Bool/F16. Score huge.

Bit-exact (byte copy, 486 value/array/dtype tests pass; 10 module-surface failures PROVEN pre-existing host-variance flakes via stash test). Before/after (construction of 4M f64, the two code paths): PyList+np.array(list)=71.7ms vs np.empty+buffer-copy=7.5ms = 9.6x. Owned/writable/base=None (behavior-identical). title: fix(fnp-ufunc): to_array() reads buffer and sidecar atomically
description:
## Problem
`UFuncArrayView::to_array()` had a data consistency race condition. It called
`materialize_with_indices()` which acquired/released the buffer lock, then
separately acquired the sidecar lock to copy sidecar values.

If `itemset()` ran between these two reads, the returned array could have
buffer[i] from time T1 and sidecar[i] from time T2 - an inconsistent snapshot.

## Fix
Inlined the buffer reading into `to_array()` while holding both locks
atomically: sidecar first (per lock-ordering contract), then buffer. Both
are read and copied before releasing either lock.

## Evidence
- New test: `to_array_buffer_sidecar_consistency_under_contention`
- Uses one i64::MAX value to force sidecar creation
- Writers update indices 1-63 with exact-representable values
- Readers verify buffer[i] == sidecar[i] for all small-integer indices
- Without fix: would detect buffer/sidecar mismatch
- With fix: all 13 concurrency tests pass

## Files changed
- crates/fnp-ufunc/src/lib.rs: rewrote to_array() to hold both locks
- crates/fnp-ufunc/tests/concurrency_safety.rs: added consistency test

title: [perf][no-gaps] Branchless UnaryOp::Sign kernel — sign still 8x vs numpy after zero-copy
description: After zero-copy (11cce08e) sign is 21.7ms vs numpy 2.66ms (8.18x) while square (x*x) is 6.66ms — the gap is the BRANCHY UnaryOp::Sign kernel (is_nan/>0/<0/else, fnp-ufunc lib.rs:1189) not autovectorizing. Lever: branchless sign preserving exact parity incl nan->nan and ±0->0, e.g. ((x>0.0) as i32 - (x<0.0) as i32) as f64 with an explicit nan guard (x!=x ? nan : ...). Must stay bit-identical (golden sha256). This is a KERNEL lever in fnp-ufunc (BlackThrush territory — coordinate / selective-commit). Target ~6.6ms (match square), ~3.3x further. NOTE fnp-ufunc currently has BlackThrush's uncommitted from_storage diff.
close_reason: Shipped fb9844cb: Sign-specific unary path skips irrelevant float-error accounting and uses numpy_sign_f64. RCH ts1 criterion elementwise_sign/1000000 improved 3.6361 ms mean [3.5001, 3.7269] -> 330.92 us mean [306.03, 375.94], ~11.0x. Golden SHA-256 f3eae2c1ce147ea83ccad296d72ded59277489795c9f0d39452a605fba0c405e; rch focused test/check/clippy passed; git diff --check passed.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text: Expand oracle differential to verify dtype promotion across binary ops. When np.add(int32_array, float16_array) is computed, what dtype is the result? Our promote() table handles this but the oracle differential doesnt test cross-type binary operations. Add oracle cases for: I32+F16, U64+I8, Bool+Complex128, F32+I64, and edge cases like U8+I8 where promotion to I16 is non-obvious.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text:
REVISION (plan-space refinement round 1):

WHERE: Same fixture infrastructure. Add to artifacts/fixtures/ and test in fnp-conformance.

EXACT TEST CASES TO ADD (oracle-captured):
The promote() table in fnp-dtype handles all 324 DType pairs, but the oracle differential has never tested cross-type binary ops. These cases verify that our promotion matches NumPy exactly for non-obvious pairs.

1. np.add(np.int32(100), np.float16(1.5)) → result dtype? (should be F16 per table)
2. np.multiply(np.uint64(2**60), np.int8(-1)) → result dtype F64 (U64+signed→F64)
3. np.add(np.bool_(True), np.complex128(1+2j)) → result dtype Complex128
4. np.subtract(np.float32(1.0), np.int64(1)) → result dtype F64 (F32+I64→F64)
5. np.add(np.uint8(200), np.int8(-50)) → result dtype I16 (U8+I8→I16), value=150
6. np.multiply(np.uint16(60000), np.int16(-1)) → result dtype I32, value=-60000
7. np.add(np.float16(1.0), np.int16(1000)) → result dtype F32 (F16+I16→F32)
8. np.divide(np.int32(7), np.int32(2)) → result dtype F64, value=3.5
9. np.floor_divide(np.int32(7), np.int32(2)) → result dtype I32, value=3
10. np.add(np.complex64(1+0j), np.int32(1)) → result dtype Complex128
11. np.multiply(np.float64(1.5), np.bool_(True)) → result dtype F64, value=1.5
12. np.add(np.uint8(255), np.uint8(1)) → overflow behavior: NumPy wraps to 0
13. np.add(np.int8(127), np.int8(1)) → overflow behavior: NumPy wraps to -128
14. np.power(np.int64(2), np.int64(63)) → large value, integer overflow edge case
15. np.add(np.float64(1.0), np.float32(1.0)) → result dtype F64

For each case capture: input dtypes, operation, result dtype, result value.

VALIDATION: Both result dtype and result value must match NumPy. Integer overflow wrapping behavior (cases 12-14) is platform-dependent in NumPy — if our behavior differs, document the divergence as a known parity gap rather than failing.

ACCEPTANCE: 12+ of 15 pass exact match. Any failures documented as known gaps with rationale.

comments.author: ubuntu
comments.text:
REVISION 2 (plan-space refinement round 3):

CORRECTIONS FROM REVISION 1:
- Case 1 was WRONG: np.add(np.int32(100), np.float16(1.5)) promotes to F64 in NumPy (NOT F16). I32 exceeds F16 mantissa. Our promote(I32, F16) correctly returns F64. Fixed expected dtype.
- Case 9 clarified: np.floor_divide(np.int32(7), np.int32(2)) returns int32(3) in NumPy. Our implementation should match.

IMPLEMENTATION APPROACH (two options, pick whichever fits better):

Option A — Add as standalone Rust tests in crates/fnp-conformance/src/lib.rs:
  Each test creates the two input arrays with specific dtypes, runs the binary op through UFuncArray::elementwise_binary(), and asserts result dtype + value. This does NOT need the Python oracle — it tests our promote() table directly.

Option B — Add as fixtures to ufunc_differential_cases.json for oracle comparison:
  Each test is a JSON fixture that gets run through PY_CAPTURE_SCRIPT against real NumPy, then compared to FrankenNumPy output. This requires FNP_ORACLE_PYTHON pointing to a Python with NumPy installed.

RECOMMENDATION: Use Option A for the 15 cases (fast, no Python dependency). Then add a SEPARATE test that runs a subset (cases 1,2,5,7,10,15) through the oracle to verify our promote() table matches NumPy. This gives both fast CI coverage AND oracle verification.

CORRECTED EXPECTED VALUES:
1. np.add(np.int32(100), np.float16(1.5)) → dtype F64, value 101.5
2. np.multiply(np.uint64(2**60), np.int8(-1)) → dtype F64, value -1.152921504606847e+18
3. np.add(np.bool_(True), np.complex128(1+2j)) → dtype Complex128, value 2+2j
4. np.subtract(np.float32(1.0), np.int64(1)) → dtype F64, value 0.0
5. np.add(np.uint8(200), np.int8(-50)) → dtype I16, value 150
6. np.multiply(np.uint16(60000), np.int16(-1)) → dtype I32, value -60000
7. np.add(np.float16(1.0), np.int16(1000)) → dtype F32, value 1001.0
8. np.divide(np.int32(7), np.int32(2)) → dtype F64, value 3.5
9. np.floor_divide(np.int32(7), np.int32(2)) → dtype I32, value 3
10. np.add(np.complex64(1+0j), np.int32(1)) → dtype Complex128, value 2+0j
11. np.multiply(np.float64(1.5), np.bool_(True)) → dtype F64, value 1.5
12. np.add(np.uint8(255), np.uint8(1)) → dtype U8, value 0 (wraps)
13. np.add(np.int8(127), np.int8(1)) → dtype I8, value -128 (wraps)
14. np.power(np.int64(2), np.int64(63)) → dtype I64, value -9223372036854775808 (overflow wraps)
15. np.add(np.float64(1.0), np.float32(1.0)) → dtype F64, value 2.0

NOTE ON COMPLEX DTYPE HANDLING: FrankenNumPy stores complex as Vec<f64> pairs. Cases 3 and 10 need special handling to verify both real and imaginary parts. The test should extract real/imag from the result values array.

NOTE ON INTEGER OVERFLOW (cases 12-14): These are same-type operations (U8+U8, I8+I8, I64^I64). NumPy wraps on overflow for integer types. If FrankenNumPy routes these through f64, the behavior will differ. Document any divergence rather than forcing a match.

title: [perf][no-gaps] Zero-copy two-input bool path for isclose (5.4x boundary tax)
description: isclose(a,b,rtol,atol,equal_nan) is 5.38x vs numpy (94ms vs 17.5ms, 4M f64) — boundary tax (cold-alloc page faults, bead lglck) on two input extracts + bool build. Needs a two-input bool zero-copy path: read a + b f64 PyBuffers (both exact same-shape C-contiguous f64 ndarrays, else fall through), compute |a-b| <= atol + rtol*|b| (and equal_nan handling) into a uint8 buffer, .view(bool). Mirror zerocopy_f64_predicate_flat but two inputs + the isclose formula. CAUTION: isclose has asymmetric rtol*|b| (not |a|), inf handling (inf close to inf), and equal_nan param — match numpy exactly (golden sha256). Pure safe Rust. Target ~3x. NOTE: arithmetic + comparison binary ufuncs already at parity; this is the main remaining binary gap.
close_reason: Shipped b5cc509e: zerocopy_f64_isclose_flat (two-input bool path, uint8+view). isclose 110->2.78ms (39.7x), now 17.5x FASTER than numpy (numpy's mask/broadcast temporaries are slow). Bit-exact golden d22da06a across 8 rtol/atol/equal_nan combos + inf/nan/overflow edges. Pure safe Rust.

title: Return infinity for spectral cond on infinite inputs
description:
## Problem
`cond_p_nxn` currently routes default / `2` / `-2` condition numbers through `svd_nxn`, which rejects non-finite inputs. NumPy instead returns `inf` for these spectral condition-number orders when the matrix contains `inf` values.

## Evidence
- Exhaustive 2x2 sampling over 80 finite-plus-infinity sign/placement cases returned `inf` for `np.linalg.cond(A, None)`, `np.linalg.cond(A, 2)`, and `np.linalg.cond(A, -2)`
- current Rust path fails before producing a value

## Goal
Match NumPy by returning `inf` for spectral condition-number orders on infinity-containing matrices, without changing the existing `NaN` error behavior.

## Files
- `crates/fnp-linalg/src/lib.rs`
close_reason: Completed

title: fnp-python clip: native fast path ignores NEP50 result_type promotion (crashes/wrong dtype on int+float bounds, bool, strong np-scalar bounds)

Diverges from numpy's result_type(a,a_min,a_max) promotion in 4 cases: clip(int_arr,1.5,2.5) raises ValueError (storing 1.5 into i64); clip(int_arr,1.0,5.0) returns int64 not float64; clip(f32_arr,np.float64(..)) returns float32 not float64; clip(bool_arr,0,1) returns bool not int64. Fix: gate native path on numpy.result_type(a,a_min,a_max)==asarray(a).dtype, else fall back to numpy. Found via differential probe vs numpy 2.4.3.

title: divmod returns float64 for integer inputs instead of preserving int dtype

Sibling floor_divide/remainder preserve dtype. Fix: gate divmod to native path only when both operands are float64 (like floor_divide's fallback philosophy), else fall back to numpy.divmod. Found via differential probe vs numpy 2.4.3. Existing divmod test uses array_equal (value-only) so dtype drift slipped through.
close_reason: Fixed: divmod gated to native f64 kernel only when both operands are float64 (via numpy_dtype_is_f64); integer and narrow-float inputs now defer to numpy.divmod, preserving int64/int32/uint8/float32/float16 result dtypes. Also defers zero-divisor cases for RuntimeWarning parity (mirrors floor_divide). Verified 16/16 dtype cases vs numpy 2.4.3 + new test divmod_preserves_input_dtype_like_numpy; broad probe3 now 0 mismatches.

title: [perf][no-gaps][fnp-linalg] recursive cache-oblivious LU getrf — close the OpenBLAS det/inv/solve gap (target 2.0x+)
description: Profile-backed (2026-06-09, fnp-vs-numpy timing sweep + same-load Criterion A/B). fnp's LU (lu_factor_unblocked_into / lu_decompose_blocked, shared by det/slogdet/inv/solve) trails scipy-OpenBLAS getrf: det_512 fnp ~8.8ms serial vs numpy ~3ms (OpenBLAS, Haswell, 64-thread). Commit 3bccdc21 lowered the blocked-LU threshold 896->512 (blocked level-3 path wins 1.38x@512..1.93x@896 — the old 896 cutoff was too conservative), halving the band gap but topping out at 1.93x (<2.0). TWO levers already REJECTED (do not repeat): (1) parallelizing the unblocked rank-1 trailing update across rows = REGRESSION (memory-bound: T=16 slower than T=1 at 256/384/512; barrier-per-k dispatch overhead, no compute to amortize — confirms the memory-bound-parallelization lesson). (2) fixed-panel blocked LU caps at 1.93x because the serial panel factorization + l21/u12 extraction overhead limits GEMM amortization at these n. THE 2.0+ LEVER: recursive cache-oblivious LU (Toledo's recursive getrf) — split columns in half, recursively factor the left half, apply the pivots + TRSM to the right half, update via one large GEMM, recurse on the right. This is strictly more GEMM-dominant than fixed-panel blocking (1.3-1.7x over blocked typically) and cache-oblivious (no nb tuning). Must preserve: identical partial-pivot sequence (so tolerance-equivalent to current path), NaN-pivot passthrough for non-finite inputs (det path), singular detection, perm/sign. Prove with the existing 284 linalg tests + blocked_lu_band_matches_serial_reference_within_tolerance + a recursive-vs-serial tolerance test; bench det_nxn/inv_nxn 512..2048 via rch Criterion same-load A/B. Keep only Score>=2.0 else unwind. Also re-profile cholesky (sweep showed ~1.9x slower than OpenBLAS — likely the same blocked-vs-serial threshold story) and qr (~1.3x) as sibling targets.
close_reason: Measured no-ship (cc, 2026-06-09). Implemented LAPACK xGETRF2 recursive cache-oblivious LU (split-cols, recurse-left, TRSM+packed_gemm the right half, recurse-right; full-row pivots → identical pivot sequence, tolerance-equal; all 284 linalg tests + n=600 band test green). Same-load Criterion A/B (RAYON_NUM_THREADS=16) vs the fixed-panel blocked LU: recursive is NOT faster — det 512 7.03 vs 7.21ms (noise), 768 19.10 vs 16.79 (1.14x SLOWER), 1024 39.9 vs 37.5, 1536 104.9 vs 97.5ms. Both paths route the trailing update through the SAME packed_gemm; recursion only adds per-node sub-block extraction + GEMM-output allocation. Source fully reverted (tree pristine). Also rejected this session: (2) cholesky nb 64→128 (CHOL_BLOCK_MIN 896→256) = 2.5x REGRESSION (cholesky cost is the O(bw^3) serial diagonal/panel factorization, not GEMM parallelism — larger nb is worse). (3) relaxing packed_gemm parallel gate to parallelize thin-k GEMM (k>=32 instead of >=128) so cholesky's k=64 trailing update parallelizes = REGRESSION (chol 512 same-binary T=1 serial 5.7ms vs T=16 parallel 8.0ms; the per-call thin GEMMs are too small, dispatch+alloc overhead dominates, serial vectorized GEMM already efficient). CONCLUSION: the LU/cholesky/GEMM SCHEDULING lever family is exhausted (3 no-ships). fnp f64 factorizations are within ~2x of scipy-OpenBLAS at n<=2048; the remaining gap is the GEMM microkernel being AVX2-no-FMA (project bit-parity policy) vs OpenBLAS AVX2+FMA. Strassen's 2x crossover is n>=4096 (sub-2.0 at benched sizes). The real unlock is bead 8vdtg (vetted std::arch SIMD+FMA module) — needs user sign-off. Do not re-attempt recursive LU / cholesky-nb / gemm-gate.

title: perf(fnp-linalg): zero-per-lane-alloc batched det + slogdet for tiny matrices
description: Complete the batched LU zero-alloc family (after solve/inv/cholesky). det_nxn/slogdet_nxn each call lu_decompose_for_det (allocs lu n*n + perm n) then read the diagonal — scalar output per lane (NO Vec<Vec>, batch_det collects Vec<f64> directly), so the only per-lane alloc is lu+perm. For tiny n the ~2 allocs (~200ns) dwarf the ~30-flop compute. Add det_nxn_into / slogdet_nxn_into reusing per-thread lu/perm scratch via for_each_init; non-finite/singular defer to det_nxn/slogdet_nxn for exact semantics (det: Singularity->0.0). Gate small n. Byte-identical + full golden. Lower value than inv/cholesky (no Vec<Vec> to kill) but completes the family.
close_reason: REJECTED (sub-2.0): zero-alloc batch_det/batch_slogdet reuse only lu+perm per thread — but det/slogdet are SCALAR-OUTPUT ops (batch_det returns Vec<f64> directly, batch_slogdet 2 Vecs), so there is NO Vec<Vec>/flatten to eliminate (which was the BIG part of the inv 4.82x / cholesky 7.60x wins). Same-worker A/B: det 1.21x@n=3, 1.17x@n=8, 0.89x@n=12, 0.91x@n=16 — marginal at tiny n, REGRESSES at n>=12. Reverted (byte-identity was proven; the lever just fails Score>=2.0). LESSON: the zero-alloc batched-LU win is dominated by killing the per-lane Vec + Vec<Vec> + flatten; for scalar-out ops only the lu/perm alloc remains and reusing it alone is ~1.2x, not worth it. The batched-LU zero-alloc family is DONE at the matrix-output ops (solve/inv/cholesky).

title: Native np.zeros/np.ones: replaced passthroughs with Rust impl
description:
Applied /porting-to-rust skill to replace numpy passthroughs.

PORTED FUNCTIONS:
- np.zeros(shape, dtype=None, order='C') → UFuncArray::zeros
- np.ones(shape, dtype=None, order='C') → UFuncArray::ones

FAST PATH:
- Native Rust when: simple shape + optional dtype + order='C' (default)
- Falls back to numpy when: order='F', device, like, or other kwargs

- ones: 1d default, 2d tuple, int dtype, float32 dtype, 3d shape
close_reason: done

title: [modes-of-reasoning] numerical-correctness
description:
## Synthesis

FrankenNumPy demonstrates strong numerical correctness foundations with 995 explicit NaN/Inf handling points, proper signed-zero semantics via copysign, and ULP-based tolerance in critical paths like linspace endpoint trimming. The FloatErrorKind enum provides structured over/underflow tracking. However, the reduction implementations (reduce_sum, reduce_mean) use naive sequential accumulation without compensated summation (Kahan, Neumaier), risking precision loss on large arrays. No fused multiply-add (FMA) usage was found for precision-critical polynomial evaluations.

The project correctly propagates NaN through operations and has explicit tests for edge cases (+/-0.0, +/-Inf, NaN), but subnormal/denormal handling is not explicitly addressed. The f64::EPSILON comparisons in histogram binning and range normalization are appropriate but could benefit from explicit documentation of their tolerance semantics.

## Bead Suggestions

1. **Add compensated summation for large reductions** - Implement Kahan or Neumaier summation as opt-in for reduce_sum/reduce_mean when array length exceeds threshold (e.g., 1e6 elements)

title: unique_values returns sorted but numpy 2.x returns unsorted (Array API spec)
description: Tests unique_values_sorted and unique_functions_consistent in conformance_unravel_unique.rs fail because fnp_python.unique_values returns a sorted array (matching np.unique's contract) but numpy 2.x's np.unique_values follows the Array API standard which does NOT guarantee sorting. Verified: np.unique_values([5,3,1,4,2]) returns [2 4 3 1 5] (unsorted) while np.unique([5,3,1,4,2]) returns [1 2 3 4 5] (sorted). Fix options: (A) change fnp_python.unique_values to match numpy 2.x's actual return order (preserving first-seen order, NOT sorted) or pass through to np.unique_values directly; (B) update test to remove sorted assumption since the test goal of 'matches numpy' is incompatible with 'is sorted' under numpy 2.x semantics. Recommended: option A for drop-in parity. Also affects unique_functions_consistent which compares unique_all.values vs unique_values: unique_all returns sorted but unique_values now does not — these need to match numpy's actual contracts.
close_reason: Fixed in commit 8b55b7b. unique_values_sorted renamed to unique_values_matches_numpy_set_semantics — drops the sortedness pin and uses set equality (np.sort(result)==np.sort(expected)) plus exact equality with numpy's actual return order, since numpy 2.x's unique_values follows the Array API spec and is unsorted. unique_functions_consistent updated to do set equality on values (since unique_values is unsorted but unique_all/unique_counts/unique_inverse return sorted) plus exact equality on counts/inverse_indices. fnp_python.unique_values is already a pure passthrough so no implementation change needed. All 27 conformance_unravel_unique tests pass.

title: [EPIC] Centralize and harden hard-coded numeric constants across all crates
description:
EPIC: Systematic audit and remediation of hard-coded numeric constants in FrankenNumPy.

## Background

A comprehensive audit of all 9 crates identified 34 significant hard-coded numeric constants in production code (non-test). These fall into four categories:

1. **Algorithm convergence thresholds** — absolute epsilon values (1e-10, 1e-15, 1e-30) used as convergence/singularity tests that don't adapt to problem scale (matrix norm, condition number).
2. **Iteration limits** — arbitrary loop bounds (30, 50, 60*n², 100*n²) for iterative algorithms without documentation of why those specific values were chosen.
3. **Security/resource bounds** — MAX_* constants for IO parsing, archive handling, and RNG state. These are already named constants and mostly correct, but some lack documentation.
4. **Display formatting defaults** — threshold, edgeitems, precision for array printing.

The audit found ZERO instances of TODO, FIXME, or "will"/"would" comments indicating unfinished code.

## What Was Already Fixed (in the audit session)

- `1e-300` in complex QR → `f64::MIN_POSITIVE` (3 occurrences in fnp-linalg)
- Taylor series terms `30` → `MATRIX_FUNC_TAYLOR_TERMS` constant (2 occurrences)
- Denman-Beavers iterations `50` → `SQRTM_MAX_ITERATIONS` constant
- Logm scaling iterations `20` → `LOGM_MAX_SCALING_ITERATIONS` constant
- 5 new named constants added to fnp-linalg top-level

## What Remains

The remaining work is organized as child beads below, covering:
- Eigenvalue detection thresholds that should scale with matrix norms
- Iteration limit coefficients that should be centralized (SVD vs eigen QR)
- Polynomial and root-finding thresholds that should scale with input
- Display formatting constants that should match NumPy's defaults exactly
- Documentation for all security/resource bound constants

## Guiding Principles

- **Parity first**: If NumPy uses a specific hard-coded value, we match it exactly. Don't "improve" NumPy's constants.
- **Scale-adaptive thresholds**: Where NumPy uses relative tolerances (e.g., `rcond * max_singular_value`), we should too.
- **Named constants over magic numbers**: Even if the value stays the same, a named constant with a doc comment is better than a bare `1e-15`.
- **No behavioral changes without oracle verification**: Any tolerance change must be validated against the differential test suite.
close_reason: Epic created and partially executed during audit session. Remaining work tracked in child beads.

title: [perf/bug][fnp-python] einsum single-operand: zero-copy trace/diagonal + reduction dtype preservation
description: Found by cc 2026-06-09 profiling einsum. (A) PERF: einsum('ii')/'ii->i' (trace/diagonal) and 'ij->ji' (transpose) are slow because fnp-python einsum_native (L36828) extract_precise_numeric_array's the FULL operand before UFuncArray::einsum runs — so even an O(n) trace pays an O(n^2) extract (einsum('ii') on 4000x4000 = 103ms vs numpy 0.015ms view-based). A UFuncArray-level single-operand fast path (permutation->transpose, ii->trace/diagonal) was added in db14f928 (correct, 48 tests green) but does NOT win at the Python boundary for this reason; transpose is also build-bound. REAL fix: a zero-copy diagonal read in einsum_native for 'ii'/'ii->i' (read only the numpy buffer diagonal, skip the full extract) -> O(n) end-to-end, target >10x at large n. Transpose can't beat numpy's view; leave on general path. (B) BUG: einsum single-operand REDUCTIONS ('ij->i','ij->j','ij->') WIDEN the result dtype to float64 — numpy preserves the input dtype (float32->float32, int32->int32). The reduce_sum_axes path computes in f64. Fix: cast the result back to input dtype for float (clean, tolerance); for int defer to numpy (overflow/wrapping semantics). Same dtype-widening class as nanstd/spacing/degrees. Verify across float32/int32/int64 x ij->i/j/(empty).
close_reason: Part B (dtype bug) FIXED + SHIPPED 00c6df4b (cc, 2026-06-09): np.einsum now preserves the promoted operand dtype (was always float64 because the native kernel extracts operands to f64). Added einsum_operand_dtype_policy: all-f64 -> native; result float32 -> native+cast-to-f32 (keeps the 1.8x GEMM win); int/bool/float16/complex -> defer to numpy (exact dtype + int-overflow wraparound that f64-then-cast can't reproduce). 0 dtype/value mismatches across float64/32/16/int8/32/64/uint8/bool x {reduce,transpose,trace,GEMM}; 24 einsum conformance + new einsum_preserves_operand_dtype regression green. Part A (zero-copy trace/diagonal perf) NOT pursued: (1) numpy's einsum('ii') is a O(1) view-based diagonal-sum = unbeatable; (2) einsum reductions on narrow dtypes now DEFER to numpy (which is faster than fnp's old reduce_sum_axes path anyway), so the perf gap there is gone for the cases that matter. Low remaining value; closing.

comments.author: ubuntu

Part A is present in crates/fnp-python/src/lib.rs as try_zerocopy_f64_einsum_single_diagonal: exact square f64 ndarray einsum('ii') and einsum('ii->i') bypass full operand extraction and operate through the diagonal view; diagonal output preserves NumPy shared-memory/writeability behavior, and trace sums only the extracted diagonal. Criterion evidence from the new python_einsum_boundary rows: baseline trace 82.400/83.085/84.251 ms and diag 83.056/84.364/85.710 ms; after trace 38.830/39.872/40.808 us (~2084x center) and diag 4.0024/4.0648/4.1479 us (~20754x center). title: [BUG] fnp_python.histogram bin-edge assignment off-by-one for left-inclusive boundary
description:
While shipping conformance_special_math.rs (3bey), the harness caught
a real divergence in fnp_python.histogram against numpy.histogram for
inputs containing bin-edge values.

Reproducer:
  import fnp_python as fnp
  import numpy as np
  data = np.array([1.0, 2.0, 1.5, 3.0, 2.5, 1.2])
  ours,   _ = fnp.histogram(data)        # default 10 bins
  theirs, _ = np.histogram(data)
  # ours   = [2, 0, 1, 0, 0, 1, 0, 1, 0, 1]
  # theirs = [1, 1, 1, 0, 0, 1, 0, 1, 0, 1]

Both bin-edge arrays match exactly: [1.0, 1.2, 1.4, ..., 3.0]. The
divergence is in COUNT assignment: numpy puts 1.2 in bin 1 (left-
inclusive [1.2, 1.4)) but fnp_python puts it in bin 0 (left-edge
1.0–1.2 absorbs the right edge as well).

Numpy.histogram semantics: bin[i] catches values where
edge[i] <= x < edge[i+1], except the last bin which catches
edge[-2] <= x <= edge[-1]. fnp_python's native fast path appears to
flip the left-inclusive rule on internal bin boundaries.

Acceptance:
- fnp_python.histogram puts boundary values in the higher-index bin
  for all but the last bin (left-inclusive, right-exclusive).
- conformance_special_math.rs special-histogram-default with the
  original [1.0, 2.0, 1.5, 3.0, 2.5, 1.2] input passes; remove the
  off-edge workaround.

title: Wire SVD_QR_ITERATION_COEFF and EIGEN_QR_ITERATION_COEFF into actual QR loops
description:
## Problem

The named constants SVD_QR_ITERATION_COEFF (100) and EIGEN_QR_ITERATION_COEFF (60) were created during the audit but the actual QR iteration loops still use the literal expressions. The constants are defined but not yet referenced by the code.

## What Needs to Happen

Search for all occurrences of iteration limits in QR loops:
1. SVD bidiagonal QR: look for `100 * n * n` or similar pattern → replace with `SVD_QR_ITERATION_COEFF * n * n`
2. tridiag_eig_qr: look for `60 * n * n` pattern → replace with `EIGEN_QR_ITERATION_COEFF * n * n`
3. Hessenberg QR (for general eigenvalues): look for `60 * n * n` → replace with `EIGEN_QR_ITERATION_COEFF * n * n`

Note: The actual code might not use `* n * n` literally — it might precompute `let max_iters = 60 * n * n;` or use a different expression. Search for any integer literal multiplied by n in the QR iteration context.

## Validation

- All linalg tests must pass
- No behavioral change (same values, just named)
- Verify the constants are actually referenced (not dead code)

## Files to Modify

- `crates/fnp-linalg/src/lib.rs` — QR iteration loops in SVD, tridiag eigenvalue, and Hessenberg eigenvalue functions
close_reason: Done as part of franken_numpy-0p9 — all 3 QR loops now use named constants.

title: [perf][no-gaps][NEXT] zero-copy output: write fresh-result kernels straight into the numpy empty() buffer
description:
PROFILED 2026-06-04 (cc-numpy). Float per-op kernels (diff/ediff1d/outer) are ALREADY at-parity with numpy at scale; the residual Python-level gap is the Py<->Rust OUTPUT boundary, not the kernel.

Evidence (median, host-loaded => ratios are upper bounds):
- np.outer vs fnp.outer: n=500 12.8x / n=1000 2.43x / n=2000 1.48x / n=3000 1.62x. Gap SHRINKS with size => fixed+copy overhead not per-element. parity=True all sizes.
- diff Rust kernel alone = 1.79ms for 2M f64 vs numpy 1.5ms (parity); fnp.diff Python ~10ms — delta is extract+build copies.
- tiny per-call floor (us): fnp.outer 7.7 vs 1.6; fnp.sqrt 4.6 vs 0.4; fnp.diff 5.6 vs 1.9 — negligible.

ROOT CAUSE: build_numpy_array_from_ufunc -> numpy_array_from_slice does numpy.empty() + buffer.copy_from_slice(vec). Kernel writes a Rust Vec (pass 1), then we copy into the numpy buffer (pass 2). numpy writes once.

LEVER: allocate numpy.empty() first, borrow its writable C-contig f64 PyBuffer as &mut [f64], have the kernel write DIRECTLY into it (build_numpy_array_into_buffer + write_into(&self,&mut[f64]) on outer/diff/elementwise/polyval/vander/cumsum). Bit-identical. ~1.3-2x output-bound.

CAVEAT: cross-cutting fnp-python/lib.rs; BlackThrush owns it (GEMM/Strassen WIP). Two micro-levers A/B-REJECTED this session (ediff1d reroute, diff zip-subtract — bandwidth-bound ~1.0-1.76x). Real op-level frontier is GEMM/Strassen (BlackThrush) + fma policy (3q3pe).
close_reason: Measured no-ship for first direct-output lever. Profile-backed candidate wrote f64 diff results straight into a freshly allocated numpy buffer through safe PyO3 PyBuffer Cell<f64> writes and preserved output bits: focused SHA-256 proof f1566d8438174a6174324865bd0671cf296ccd16b8fc5d277af28e421c834bae passed via rch. Perf did not clear Score>=2.0: in-process 2M f64 diff baseline 21.396ms vs candidate 21.686ms; hyperfine startup-inclusive candidate only 1.02x +/-0.08 faster (306.6ms baseline vs 301.9ms candidate). Source/proof trial removed; next optimization moved to profile-backed franken_numpy-inner-vdot-gemm-oaacm.

title: [parity-gap] fnp-python where fast path widens NumPy dtypes
description:
Review finding from the six-hour commits touching `fnp-python` `where` parity (`449b560`, `7285d5d`). The native `where_py` path in `crates/fnp-python/src/lib.rs` lines 7384-7402 uses `extract_numeric_array`, whose bridge collapses every signed integer dtype to `I64`, every unsigned integer dtype to `U64`, and every float dtype to `F64` (lines 3656-3693). That means supported numeric inputs take the Rust fast path but lose NumPy dtype/promotion parity. Recent `where` tests cover values, object fallback, and arity, but they do not cover narrow dtype preservation or mixed narrow signed/unsigned promotion.

Concrete repro steps:
1. Build the fnp-python extension and run a Python snippet equivalent to:
   `cond = np.array([True, False])`
   `x = np.array([1, 2], dtype=np.int8)`
   `y = np.array([3, 4], dtype=np.int8)`
   `ours = fnp.where(cond, x, y); theirs = np.where(cond, x, y)`
   `assert ours.dtype == theirs.dtype`
2. Repeat with `x.dtype=np.float32, y.dtype=np.float32`, and with `x.dtype=np.int16, y.dtype=np.uint16`.

Expected: dtype and NumPy promotion match upstream exactly (`int8` stays `int8`, `float32` stays `float32`, `int16` + `uint16` promotes like NumPy).
Actual from code inspection: the fast path builds `UFuncArray` values through the coarse bridge and exports widened dtypes (`int64`, `uint64`, `float64`, or downstream promotion from those widened types), so values can pass while dtype parity is wrong.

Suggested fix: switch `where_py` to `extract_precise_numeric_array` for condition/x/y where appropriate, add conformance cases that assert dtype/shape/value for narrow signed, unsigned, float32, and mixed signed/unsigned choices, and fall back to NumPy only for unsupported dtype families such as object/string/complex if needed.
close_reason: Fixed where_py dtype widening by using extract_precise_numeric_array for condition/x/y and adding NumPy dtype promotion conformance; validated with rch exec -- cargo test -p fnp-python --test conformance_where -- --nocapture, rch exec -- cargo check -p fnp-python --all-targets, rch exec -- cargo clippy -p fnp-python --all-targets -- -D warnings, and git diff --check.

title: [perf] fnp-ufunc GEMM: register-blocked matmul kernel (close gap vs OpenBLAS)
description: BASELINE (rch, AMD Ryzen Threadripper 5975WX, single-thread, build=generic SSE2): fnp naive matmul_accumulate (i-k-j) = 10.57 GFLOP/s at 1024x1024 f64 (median 203ms; self-measured vs inlined naive reference in perf_matmul_golden example). numpy OpenBLAS multi-thread = ~90 GFLOP/s @1024, ~350 @2048; single-thread OpenBLAS ~35 GFLOP/s. ALIEN PRIMITIVE: register/cache blocking — MRxNR micro-kernel with register accumulators + full-K inner loop, preserving per-element sequential-k accumulation (bit-exact with naive: each C[i,j] still sums k=0..K-1 in order, no FMA contraction). LEVER: replace matmul_accumulate with MRxNR register-tiled kernel; B-row reuse across MR rows + MR independent accumulator chains for ILP. PROOF: perf_matmul_golden FNV-1a checksum must stay identical (bit-exact); re-bench median for Score>=2.0. Forbid(unsafe) stays — autovectorization only. Follow-up levers tracked separately: AVX2 build target (x86-64-v3), j-panel cache blocking, parallelism.
notes: Shipped: register-tiled (MR=4 x NR=8 register accumulators, full-K) + j-panel (NC) cache blocking replacing ROW_BLOCK=8. title: Add fuzz target for binary fromfile parsing (fromfile/tofile round-trip)

title: Add golden artifact tests for fnp-random RNG implementations (tick-22)
